items.subtitle: 2000 - Wuxia elegance

items.name: Face/Off
items.reasoning: John Woo's 1997 gonzo actioner swaps Cage and Travolta's faces amid balletic gun battles. Excessive, operatic, and endlessly rewatchable.
items.subtitle: 1997 - Woo excess

items.name: Bad Boys II
items.reasoning: Michael Bay's 2003 sequel is loud, expensive, and chaotic, epitomizing the maximalist 2000s buddy-cop action style for better and worse.
items.subtitle: 2003 - Bay maximalism

items.name: Fast & Furious
items.reasoning: The 2009 fourth entry began the franchise's pivot toward absurd set-pieces, a fun-but-formulaic ride that leans on nostalgia over craft.
items.subtitle: 2009 - Family, gears up

items.name: Rambo: First Blood Part II
items.reasoning: The 1985 sequel traded the original's grounded pathos for Reagan-era jingoism and body counts. Iconic, but a step down from First Blood.
items.subtitle: 1985 - Muscles and machine guns

items.name: Transformers: Revenge of the Fallen
items.reasoning: The 2009 sequel is a notoriously incoherent, roughly 150-minute assault criticized for its plot, humor, and racial caricatures despite huge box office.
items.subtitle: 2009 - Loud and empty

items.name: Batman & Robin
items.reasoning: Joel Schumacher's 1997 neon disaster with nipple-suits and endless ice puns nearly killed the Batman franchise and routinely lands on worst-films lists.
items.subtitle: 1997 - Bat-nipples

items.name: Catwoman
items.reasoning: The 2004 Halle Berry vehicle swept the Razzies including Worst Picture and Worst Actress, and is a byword for a botched comic-book action movie.
items.subtitle: 2004 - Razzie sweeper

title: Sports Cars, Ranked
tagline: Sports cars ranked on legacy, engineering, and honest performance — from eternal benchmarks to genuine lemons.
basis: Placement weighs historical significance, driving merit, and cultural impact. Every factual claim in the reasoning was scrutinized against production records, performance data, and documented history; shaky attributions were tightened and one item that is not actually a sports car was removed.

items.name: Porsche 911
items.reasoning: Unveiled in 1963 (originally as the 901) and in continuous production since 1964, the rear-engine 911 has evolved for six decades while staying instantly recognizable, and remains the yardstick every rival sports car is measured against.
items.subtitle: The eternal benchmark

items.name: Mazda MX-5 Miata
items.reasoning: Guinness-certified as the best-selling two-seat sports car in history, it debuted at the 1989 Chicago Auto Show and distilled lightweight, balanced, affordable fun, single-handedly reviving the affordable roadster segment.
items.subtitle: The people's roadster

items.name: Ferrari F40
items.reasoning: The last car personally approved by Enzo Ferrari before his 1988 death, its 2.9-liter twin-turbo V8 pushed it to roughly 201 mph, making it widely credited as the first street-legal production car to break 200 mph, and it remains the poster benchmark of raw supercars.
items.subtitle: Enzo's last blessing

items.name: McLaren F1
items.reasoning: With a central driving seat and a naturally aspirated BMW V12, its 240.1 mph run in 1998 made it the fastest production car for years (until the Bugatti Veyron in 2005) and it is still revered as the purest hypercar ever built.
items.subtitle: The analog hypercar

items.name: Chevrolet Corvette
items.reasoning: In production since 1953, the Corvette went mid-engine with the C8 in 2020, delivering supercar performance for a fraction of European prices and cementing its status as America's definitive sports car.
items.subtitle: America's sports car

items.name: Nissan GT-R
items.reasoning: The 'Godzilla' nickname was coined by Australia's Wheels magazine for the R32 Skyline GT-R and carried over to the R35, whose twin-turbo V6 and advanced all-wheel drive let it humble far pricier exotics at a fraction of the cost.
items.subtitle: Godzilla

items.name: Toyota Supra
items.reasoning: The A80's bulletproof 2JZ inline-six became a tuner icon capable of huge power, and its starring role in 2001's The Fast and the Furious made it one of the most beloved Japanese sports cars ever.
items.subtitle: The 2JZ legend

items.name: Ford Mustang
items.reasoning: Introduced in April 1964, it created the pony car class and reached one million sales within about two years, becoming the most enduring American performance icon.
items.subtitle: The original pony car

items.name: Lamborghini Countach
items.reasoning: Entering production in 1974, its wedge shape and scissor doors defined the supercar silhouette, dominated 1980s bedroom posters, and set the design template for every Lamborghini flagship since.
items.subtitle: Bedroom-wall royalty

items.name: Jaguar E-Type
items.reasoning: Enzo Ferrari reportedly called it the most beautiful car ever made, and its 1961 Geneva debut paired stunning looks with a claimed 150 mph top speed at a startlingly low price.
items.subtitle: Enzo's 'most beautiful'

items.name: Audi R8
items.reasoning: Built on a platform shared with the Lamborghini Gallardo, the V10 R8 offered genuine supercar performance with Audi build quality and daily usability, making exotic ownership approachable.
items.subtitle: Everyday exotic

items.name: Subaru BRZ
items.reasoning: A lightweight, rear-drive, naturally aspirated coupe co-developed with Toyota (as the 86/GR86), it channels old-school Miata philosophy for drivers who prize balance over horsepower.
items.subtitle: Modern purist's pick

items.name: Dodge Viper
items.reasoning: The original 1992 Viper's massive 8.0-liter V10 and near-total lack of driver aids made it a brutal, uncompromising American brute that demanded respect and punished mistakes.
items.subtitle: Raw V10 menace

items.name: BMW Z4
items.reasoning: A comfortable, well-built roadster co-developed with (and sharing its platform with) the Toyota Supra, but its grand-touring tuning leaves enthusiasts wishing it were sharper.
items.subtitle: Handsome but soft

items.name: Mitsubishi 3000GT
items.reasoning: The VR-4 crammed in all-wheel drive, active aero and four-wheel steering, but its considerable heft blunted the payoff of all that 1990s gadgetry compared to lighter rivals.
items.subtitle: Overweight tech showcase

items.name: Pontiac Fiero
items.reasoning: A promising mid-engine layout was undercut by parts-bin economy-car suspension and a weak 2.5-liter Iron Duke four, and its handling was only sorted out in its final 1988 year, too late to save it.
items.subtitle: Mid-engine misfire

items.name: Chevrolet SSR
items.reasoning: A retractable-hardtop sport pickup that was heavy, expensive and underpowered at its 2003 launch, satisfying neither truck buyers nor sports car fans until a late V8 upgrade.
items.subtitle: Retro pickup-roadster

items.name: DeLorean DMC-12
items.reasoning: Its gullwing doors and stainless-steel body looked futuristic, but a gutless ~130-hp PRV V6 made it slow, and the company collapsed in scandal barely a year after the car reached customers in 1981.
items.subtitle: Style over substance

title: Soulslike Games, Ranked
tagline: From Lordran to Yharnam and every clone in between — the soulslike genre, ranked.
basis: Ranked on design influence, combat quality, critical/commercial standing, and lasting genre impact.

items.name: Elden Ring
items.reasoning: Won The Game Awards' Game of the Year 2022 and has sold over 30 million copies, expanding the Souls formula into a sprawling open world whose underlying mythology and backstory were written by George R. R. Martin. Its scope, boss design, and freedom make it the genre's definitive statement.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2022

items.name: Dark Souls
items.reasoning: The game that named and popularized the 'soulslike,' with an interconnected Lordran and bonfire loop that defined the category (building on Demon's Souls two years earlier). Its brutal difficulty and cryptic storytelling reshaped action RPGs for a decade.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2011

items.name: Bloodborne
items.reasoning: A PS4 exclusive that traded shields for aggressive rally-based combat and drenched the formula in cosmic Lovecraftian horror. Widely praised for its Gothic Yharnam and still regarded as a FromSoftware peak.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2015

items.name: Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice
items.reasoning: Won The Game Awards' Game of the Year 2019 by swapping RPG stats for a precise posture-and-parry rhythm around a single katana. Its deflection-based dueling redefined what a soulslike could be.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2019

items.name: Dark Souls III
items.reasoning: A faster, fan-service-heavy sendoff to the trilogy with the acclaimed Ashes of Ariandel and The Ringed City DLC. Tightly designed bosses make it many players' favorite of the three.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2016

items.name: Demon's Souls
items.reasoning: The original prototype for the entire genre, introducing the Nexus hub, soul economy, and online messages. Bluepoint's 2020 PS5 remake proved how ahead of its time it was.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2009

items.name: Lies of P
items.reasoning: A Bloodborne-inspired Pinocchio retelling widely praised as one of the strongest non-FromSoftware soulslikes, noted for its Belle Epoque setting and perfect-guard combat. Its 2025 Overture DLC further cemented its reputation.
items.subtitle: Round8 Studio, 2023

items.name: Nioh 2
items.reasoning: Deepened the original's stance-switching, ki-pulse combat with yokai transformations and Burst Counters, offering some of the most mechanically dense action in the genre. A high skill ceiling rewards mastery.
items.subtitle: Team Ninja, 2020

items.name: Dark Souls II
items.reasoning: The divisive middle child, criticized for disjointed world geography and adaptability-gated i-frames but praised for build variety and its Scholar of the First Sin edition. Its DLC trio is genuinely strong.
items.subtitle: FromSoftware, 2014

items.name: Nioh
items.reasoning: Team Ninja's Sengoku-era debut fused Ninja Gaiden speed with Souls structure and loot-driven progression. Mission-based levels and repeated environments hold it a notch below its sequel.
items.subtitle: Team Ninja, 2017

items.name: Star Wars Jedi: Survivor
items.reasoning: Respawn's sequel expanded the accessible soulslike lightsaber combat with new stances and semi-open zones. Strong design was undercut at launch by notable performance problems.
items.subtitle: Respawn, 2023

items.name: Star Wars Jedi: Fallen Order
items.reasoning: Brought Souls-style checkpoints and stamina dueling to a mainstream Star Wars adventure, making the genre approachable for millions. Metroidvania traversal broadens its appeal.
items.subtitle: Respawn, 2019

items.name: Hollow Knight
items.reasoning: A 2D metroidvania that borrows Souls' bench-checkpoint, geo-on-death, and cryptic lore into a hand-drawn Hallownest. Its brutal bosses and atmosphere earned a massive following.
items.subtitle: Team Cherry, 2017

items.name: Remnant II
items.reasoning: A rare gun-based, co-op soulslike with procedurally generated worlds and a deep archetype/class system. Its shooter twist on the formula won a dedicated audience.
items.subtitle: Gunfire Games, 2023

items.name: Wo Long: Fallen Dynasty
items.reasoning: Team Ninja's Three Kingdoms action leaned on a fast deflect-heavy spirit system and reviewed solidly, but its morale mechanic and uneven difficulty spikes drew mixed player reactions. Good, but a step below their Nioh work.
items.subtitle: Team Ninja, 2023

items.name: The Surge
items.reasoning: A sci-fi soulslike with a clever limb-targeting system that lets you sever and loot specific gear. Repetitive industrial environments keep it middling.
items.subtitle: Deck13, 2017

items.name: Mortal Shell
items.reasoning: An ambitious indie built around possessing dead warrior 'shells' and a Hardening defense mechanic. Short length and rough edges limit it despite an atmospheric world.
items.subtitle: Cold Symmetry, 2020

items.name: Lords of the Fallen
items.reasoning: The 2023 reboot's dual-world Umbral lantern mechanic was inventive, but launch-window performance issues and messy balance held it back. A heavily patched cult curiosity.

art.title: Lords of the Fallen (2014 video game)

items.subtitle: Hexworks / CI Games, 2023

items.name: Lords of the Fallen
items.reasoning: An early, sluggish Souls imitator with clunky combat and a forgettable world, notable mainly for being one of the first clones. It aged into a cautionary tale.

art.title: Lords of the Fallen (2014 video game)

items.subtitle: Deck13 / CI Games, 2014

items.name: Immortal: Unchained
items.reasoning: A gun-focused soulslike widely panned for stiff shooting, drab levels, and technical jank. It sits among the genre's most notorious low points.
items.subtitle: Toadman Interactive, 2018

title: World Cities, Ranked
tagline: The great cities of the world, sorted by the ruthless calculus of scale, beauty, safety, and soul.
basis: Ranked on a blend of global influence, culture and history, livability/safety, and sheer iconic draw — with points docked for traffic, pollution, and instability.

items.name: Tokyo
items.reasoning: The world's largest metropolitan area at ~37 million people, yet famed for trains that run to the minute and for holding more Michelin stars than any other city on Earth. A near-impossible fusion of scale, safety, and precision.
items.subtitle: Japan

items.name: Paris
items.reasoning: One of the most visited cities on the planet, home to the Louvre — the world's most visited museum at roughly 8–9 million visitors a year — and an unmatched concentration of art, cuisine, and architecture. The global benchmark for beauty.
items.subtitle: France

items.name: New York City
items.reasoning: A leading global financial and cultural capital, home to Wall Street, Broadway, and the UN headquarters. Its 24/7 subway and relentless density make it the definitive global city.
items.subtitle: United States

items.name: London
items.reasoning: A global financial hub with four UNESCO World Heritage Sites and the world's oldest underground railway, opened in 1863. Centuries of history stacked beside cutting-edge finance and theatre.
items.subtitle: United Kingdom

items.name: Singapore
items.reasoning: Consistently ranked among the world's safest and cleanest cities, with Changi Airport repeatedly voted the best on Earth by Skytrax. A hyper-efficient garden city, though famously strict.
items.subtitle: Singapore

items.name: Barcelona
items.reasoning: Gaudi's still-unfinished Sagrada Familia and a Mediterranean beachfront make it a rare city that pairs world-class architecture with sun and sea. Vibrant, walkable, and endlessly photogenic.
items.subtitle: Spain

items.name: Rome
items.reasoning: An open-air museum where the Colosseum and Pantheon sit blocks from Vatican City. Nearly 3,000 years of history layered into one chaotic, delicious capital.
items.subtitle: Italy

items.name: Hong Kong
items.reasoning: A dramatic skyline against Victoria Harbour with more skyscrapers than any city on Earth, backed by the highly efficient MTR metro. Dense, dynamic, and utterly unique.
items.subtitle: China (SAR)

items.name: Sydney
items.reasoning: The Opera House and Harbour Bridge frame one of the world's great natural harbours, paired with beaches like Bondi. Perennially near the top of global livability rankings.
items.subtitle: Australia

items.name: Istanbul
items.reasoning: The largest city straddling two continents, where the Hagia Sophia and Grand Bazaar bridge Europe and Asia. Immense history, though traffic and sprawl weigh it down.
items.subtitle: Turkey

items.name: Amsterdam
items.reasoning: Its 17th-century canal ring is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and it is one of the most bike-friendly cities on Earth. Charming and liberal, if increasingly overrun by tourists.
items.subtitle: Netherlands

items.name: Berlin
items.reasoning: A reunified capital that turned Cold War scars into one of the world's most influential nightlife and art scenes. Gritty and affordable, but still catching up on infrastructure.
items.subtitle: Germany

items.name: Dubai
items.reasoning: Home to the Burj Khalifa, the tallest building on Earth, and a byword for engineered luxury in the desert. Spectacular but artificial, with brutal summer heat.
items.subtitle: United Arab Emirates

items.name: Mexico City
items.reasoning: One of the largest cities in the Americas, with a food scene that earned it global culinary acclaim and a stunning historic center. Vast and vibrant, if plagued by traffic.
items.subtitle: Mexico

items.name: Bangkok
items.reasoning: Topped global rankings as the world's most visited city in several pre-pandemic years, famous for street food and ornate temples. Electric energy undercut by notorious gridlock and pollution.
items.subtitle: Thailand

items.name: Rio de Janeiro
items.reasoning: Copacabana, Christ the Redeemer, and Carnival make it one of the world's most beautiful settings. Stunning scenery held back by persistent crime and inequality.
items.subtitle: Brazil

items.name: Los Angeles
items.reasoning: The entertainment capital of the world and home to Hollywood, but its car-dependent sprawl and traffic routinely rank among the worst in the country.
items.subtitle: United States

items.name: Cairo
items.reasoning: Gateway to the Pyramids of Giza and a millennia-old cultural giant, but choked by some of the world's worst air pollution and traffic. History outpaces livability.
items.subtitle: Egypt

items.name: Kabul
items.reasoning: Repeatedly ranked among the world's least livable cities by the EIU, scarred by decades of war and severe restrictions under Taliban rule. A capital in crisis.
items.subtitle: Afghanistan

items.name: Caracas
items.reasoning: For years one of the most dangerous cities on Earth by homicide rate, ravaged by hyperinflation and economic collapse. A tragic fall from oil-boom prosperity.
items.subtitle: Venezuela

title: Sandwiches, Ranked
tagline: From summer-tomato perfection to jelly-only despair.
basis: Ranked on flavor balance, structural integrity, and how well each holds up at its best versus its worst execution.

items.name: BLT
items.reasoning: Three ingredients plus mayo on toast, yet the salt-crunch-acid balance is nearly perfect and impossible to improve. It peaks with a summer tomato and needs nothing else.
items.subtitle: Bacon, lettuce, tomato

items.name: Cuban sandwich
items.reasoning: Roast pork, ham, Swiss, mustard and pickles pressed on Cuban bread until crisp; a Florida staple rooted in the Cuban communities of Tampa's Ybor City and Miami, with a genuinely balanced sweet-salty-tangy profile. The press is what elevates it above a deli stack.
items.subtitle: Pressed pork, ham, Swiss, pickle

items.name: Banh mi
items.reasoning: A crackly baguette (a French colonial legacy) loaded with pate, cold cuts, pickled daikon and carrot, cilantro and chili for a full sweet-sour-savory-spicy hit. Few sandwiches pack this much contrast into one bite.
items.subtitle: Vietnamese-French fusion

items.name: Reuben
items.reasoning: Grilled corned beef, Swiss, sauerkraut and Russian dressing on rye; a deli classic that turns rich and tangy when griddled. It is heavy but deeply satisfying.
items.subtitle: Corned beef, kraut, Russian

items.name: Club sandwich
items.reasoning: Turkey, bacon, lettuce, tomato and mayo across three slices of toast, held by frilled picks; a diner and hotel staple traceable to the 1890s. Dependable and complete, if rarely surprising.
items.subtitle: The triple-decker standard

items.name: Philly cheesesteak
items.reasoning: Thin-sliced griddled beef with onions on a hoagie roll, born around 1930 in Philadelphia as a plain steak sandwich; the cheese (now Whiz, provolone or American) came later, with Cheez Whiz not arriving until the 1950s. Molten and messy in the best way.
items.subtitle: Griddled ribeye and cheese

items.name: Grilled cheese
items.reasoning: Buttered bread griddled around melting cheese; the ultimate comfort sandwich and gateway to tomato soup. Simple, universal, and near-impossible to dislike.
items.subtitle: Butter, bread, melt

items.name: Croque monsieur
items.reasoning: A French cafe classic of ham and cheese blanketed in bechamel and broiled until bubbling; add a fried egg and it becomes a croque madame. Indulgent and unmistakably rich.
items.subtitle: Ham, cheese, bechamel

items.name: Italian sub
items.reasoning: Salami, capicola, mortadella and provolone with oil, vinegar and shredded lettuce on a long roll. A deli workhorse whose quality lives or dies on the meat and bread.
items.subtitle: Cured meats and provolone

items.name: Pastrami on rye
items.reasoning: Cured, peppery smoked beef steamed and piled high on rye with mustard, immortalized by New York delis like Katz's. Glorious done right, dry and stringy done wrong.
items.subtitle: Katz's-style deli icon

items.name: Muffuletta
items.reasoning: A round Sicilian loaf stuffed with cured meats, cheese and a briny olive salad, credited to Central Grocery in New Orleans around 1906. The olive salad is the whole personality.
items.subtitle: New Orleans olive-salad round

items.name: Peanut butter and jelly
items.reasoning: An American childhood default that is cheap, shelf-stable and genuinely tasty. It scores on nostalgia and reliability more than culinary ambition.
items.subtitle: Lunchbox legend

items.name: Egg salad sandwich
items.reasoning: Chopped hard-boiled egg bound in mayo on soft bread; comforting and cheap but polarizing thanks to its smell and mushy texture. A picnic staple with a ceiling.
items.subtitle: Creamy, humble, divisive

items.name: Sloppy joe
items.reasoning: Loose seasoned ground beef in tomato sauce on a hamburger bun; a cafeteria favorite that is tasty but structurally hopeless. You need a fork within two bites.
items.subtitle: Ground beef in sauce on a bun

items.name: Tuna melt
items.reasoning: Tuna salad topped with cheese and grilled; the warm-mayo-and-fish combination is comforting to some and off-putting to many. A diner staple that swings hard on execution.
items.subtitle: Tuna salad, griddled with cheese

items.name: Bologna sandwich
items.reasoning: A slice of processed bologna on white bread with mustard or mayo; cheap, nostalgic, but bland and nutritionally thin. Fried it improves; plain it plateaus.
items.subtitle: White bread and cold cut

items.name: Cucumber sandwich
items.reasoning: Thin cucumber on crustless buttered white bread, a British afternoon-tea tradition; delicate and refreshing but almost aggressively unfilling. All texture, little payoff.
items.subtitle: Tea-party finger food

items.name: Fluffernutter
items.reasoning: Peanut butter and marshmallow creme on white bread, a New England creation built on Massachusetts-made Marshmallow Fluff and essentially dessert masquerading as lunch. Cloyingly sweet and one-note.
items.subtitle: Peanut butter and marshmallow

items.name: Filet-O-Fish
items.reasoning: A breaded fish patty with a half-slice of American cheese and tartar sauce on a steamed bun; inoffensive but soggy and famously divisive. It survives on convenience, not craft.
items.subtitle: Fast-food fish patty

items.name: Jelly sandwich
items.reasoning: Jelly alone on bread with nothing to balance it: pure sugar, instantly soggy, and structurally a mess. It only exists as PB&J with the good part removed.
items.subtitle: Just jelly, no peanut butter

title: Hip-Hop Albums, Ranked
tagline: Hip-Hop Albums, Ranked — fact-checked
basis: Ranked by critical acclaim, influence, and cultural impact, with every factual claim in the blurbs verified.

heroArt.term: Kendrick Lamar good kid m.A.A.d city

items.name: Illmatic
items.reasoning: Nas' debut is widely cited as the gold standard of lyricism, packing ten tightly constructed tracks and vivid Queensbridge storytelling into about 40 minutes. Its production roster (DJ Premier, Pete Rock, Q-Tip, Large Professor, L.E.S.) made it a boom-bap benchmark.
items.subtitle: Nas · 1994

items.name: good kid, m.A.A.d city
items.reasoning: Kendrick's major-label debut is a cinematic concept album — subtitled 'A Short Film by Kendrick Lamar' — chronicling a day in Compton, praised for its narrative structure and detail. It is multi-platinum and set the record for the most weeks on the Billboard 200 by a hip-hop studio album, cementing him as his generation's most acclaimed rapper.

art.term: Kendrick Lamar good kid m.A.A.d city

items.subtitle: Kendrick Lamar · 2012

items.name: The Chronic
items.reasoning: Dr. Dre's solo debut codified G-funk and reshaped West Coast rap while launching Snoop Dogg. It remains one of the most influential production blueprints in the genre.
items.subtitle: Dr. Dre · 1992

items.name: To Pimp a Butterfly
items.reasoning: A dense fusion of jazz, funk, and spoken word tackling Black identity and self-worth, it topped countless decade-end lists. 'Alright' became a modern protest anthem.

art.term: Kendrick Lamar To Pimp a Butterfly

items.subtitle: Kendrick Lamar · 2015

items.name: Ready to Die
items.reasoning: Biggie's debut married gritty street narratives with radio-ready hooks, revitalizing East Coast rap. Its storytelling and flow set a template countless MCs still chase.

art.term: The Notorious B.I.G. Ready to Die

items.subtitle: The Notorious B.I.G. · 1994

items.name: Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)
items.reasoning: RZA's raw, kung-fu-sampling production and nine distinct MCs redefined group rap and hardcore hip-hop. It launched a franchise of acclaimed solo careers.

art.term: Wu-Tang Clan Enter the Wu-Tang 36 Chambers

items.subtitle: Wu-Tang Clan · 1993

items.name: The Blueprint
items.reasoning: Released on 9/11, Jay-Z's soul-sampling classic showcased career-best writing and helped break Kanye West as a producer. It's frequently ranked among the best rap albums ever.
items.subtitle: Jay-Z · 2001

items.name: My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy
items.reasoning: Kanye's maximalist opus is a near-universally acclaimed comeback with lush, operatic production and a stacked guest list. It scored a rare perfect Pitchfork 10.0.

art.term: Kanye West My Beautiful Dark Twisted Fantasy

items.subtitle: Kanye West · 2010

items.name: Aquemini
items.reasoning: OutKast's third LP fused funk, soul, and Southern rap into a genre-expanding statement that proved the South's artistic depth. It earned a rare five-mic rating from The Source.
items.subtitle: OutKast · 1998

items.name: Madvillainy
items.reasoning: MF DOOM and Madlib's collaboration is a cult-classic masterclass in abstract wordplay and dusty, sample-driven beats. It's a cornerstone of underground hip-hop.
items.subtitle: Madvillain · 2004

items.name: The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill
items.reasoning: Blending hip-hop, neo-soul, and reggae, it won five Grammys including Album of the Year. It remains a landmark of both rap and R&B.

art.term: Lauryn Hill The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill

items.subtitle: Lauryn Hill · 1998

items.name: Paid in Full
items.reasoning: Rakim's internal rhymes and laid-back cadence revolutionized MCing and raised the bar for lyrical complexity. It's foundational to the golden age.

art.term: Eric B. & Rakim Paid in Full

items.subtitle: Eric B. & Rakim · 1987

items.name: The Marshall Mathers LP
items.reasoning: Eminem's provocative technical showcase sold roughly 1.76 million copies in its first week — then a record for a solo studio album — and won the Best Rap Album Grammy. Its dense rhyme schemes influenced a generation.
items.subtitle: Eminem · 2000

items.name: Straight Outta Compton
items.reasoning: N.W.A's raw debut popularized gangsta rap and gave voice to inner-city frustration, sparking national controversy. Its impact on hip-hop's direction is undeniable.
items.subtitle: N.W.A · 1988

items.name: Get Rich or Die Tryin'
items.reasoning: Backed by Dr. Dre and Eminem, 50 Cent's debut was a commercial juggernaut with wall-to-wall hits like 'In da Club.' It dominated the early-2000s mainstream.

art.term: 50 Cent Get Rich or Die Tryin'

items.subtitle: 50 Cent · 2003

items.name: The College Dropout
items.reasoning: Kanye's debut broke from thug tropes with soulful, self-aware production and relatable themes. It launched one of hip-hop's most influential careers.
items.subtitle: Kanye West · 2004

items.name: Take Care
items.reasoning: Drake's moody, melodic sophomore album helped popularize the introspective, R&B-tinged sound that dominated the 2010s. It won the Best Rap Album Grammy but leans commercial over classic.
items.subtitle: Drake · 2011

items.name: Tha Carter III
items.reasoning: Lil Wayne's peak-era blockbuster sold roughly a million copies in its first week and produced 'A Milli' and 'Lollipop.' Uneven at points but a commercial and cultural high.
items.subtitle: Lil Wayne · 2008

title: Gemstones, Ranked
tagline: Nature's showcase, sorted by rarity, durability, and what collectors actually pay.
basis: Ranked on a blend of rarity, hardness/durability, and market value, with treatment and abundance dragging common stones down.

items.name: Diamond
items.reasoning: The hardest known natural material at 10 on the Mohs scale, prized for the brilliance of its exceptionally high refractive index; 20th-century De Beers marketing cemented it as the default Western engagement stone.
items.subtitle: The undisputed king

items.name: Ruby
items.reasoning: A 9 on the Mohs scale; the finest 'pigeon's blood' Burmese rubies are the most valuable colored gemstone per carat, with top stones outselling most diamonds at auction.
items.subtitle: Red corundum royalty

items.name: Emerald
items.reasoning: The premier green beryl, so prized that top Colombian stones can command higher prices than diamonds despite their near-universal inclusions (the 'jardin').
items.subtitle: Green with envy

items.name: Sapphire
items.reasoning: The blue variety of corundum, hard at 9 and famously durable; Kashmir sapphires are among the most valuable colored gems on Earth.
items.subtitle: Blue corundum classic

items.name: Alexandrite
items.reasoning: A rare chrysoberyl that shifts from green in daylight to red under incandescent light, making fine specimens extraordinarily scarce and pricey.
items.subtitle: The color-changer

items.name: Tanzanite
items.reasoning: Found only in the Merelani Hills near Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania; its violet-blue glow (usually heat-enhanced) and single-source rarity made it a modern jewelry sensation after its 1967 discovery.
items.subtitle: One valley, one gem

items.name: Opal
items.reasoning: Prized for its play-of-color that flashes the full spectrum; Australian black opals are among the most sought-after gems worldwide.
items.subtitle: Fire in a stone

items.name: Aquamarine
items.reasoning: The blue-green cousin of emerald, valued for its clarity and calming color and often available in large, remarkably flawless crystals.
items.subtitle: Sea-blue beryl

items.name: Jade
items.reasoning: Revered for millennia in China; top imperial green jadeite fetches millions, though the tougher nephrite form of jade is far more common.
items.subtitle: The imperial stone

items.name: Amethyst
items.reasoning: Once counted among the cardinal gems alongside ruby and sapphire, but large Brazilian and Uruguayan deposits made this purple quartz affordable and abundant.
items.subtitle: Purple quartz

items.name: Topaz
items.reasoning: Hard at 8 on the Mohs scale and available in many colors; prized imperial topaz is rare, but blue topaz is almost always irradiated and heat-treated, and cheap.
items.subtitle: The versatile all-rounder

items.name: Garnet
items.reasoning: A durable silicate best known in deep red; rarer green demantoid and tsavorite varieties are genuinely prized, while common almandine is a bargain.
items.subtitle: January's dark horse

items.name: Pearl
items.reasoning: One of the few gems produced by a living creature, timelessly elegant but soft at 2.5-4.5 Mohs and now mostly cultured rather than wild.
items.subtitle: The organic classic

items.name: Peridot
items.reasoning: A pleasant lime-green stone formed deep in the mantle (and even found in meteorites), but its limited color range and modest hardness keep it mid-tier.
items.subtitle: Olive-green olivine

items.name: Turquoise
items.reasoning: Beloved in Native American and Persian jewelry, but soft, porous, and so often stabilized or dyed that untreated stones are hard to find.
items.subtitle: Southwest staple

items.name: Citrine
items.reasoning: A cheerful yellow quartz, but most on the market is just heat-treated amethyst rather than naturally occurring citrine.
items.subtitle: Budget sunshine

items.name: Onyx
items.reasoning: A banded chalcedony popular for cameos and men's rings, though commercial black onyx is routinely dyed to deepen its color.
items.subtitle: The black basic

items.name: Cubic zirconia
items.reasoning: A synthetic material developed by Soviet scientists in the 1970s and mass-produced as a diamond simulant; it has no natural rarity and sells for a tiny fraction of diamond's price.
items.subtitle: The diamond impostor

items.name: Hematite
items.reasoning: Really just polished iron oxide with a metallic sheen; it has none of the rarity of a fine gem, and the 'magnetic' healing-stone versions are actually synthetic hematine, not true hematite.
items.subtitle: Iron ore in disguise

title: Mario Games, Ranked
tagline: Every mainline Mario platformer plus the spin-offs that mattered, ranked on design, influence, and staying power.
basis: Ranked by a blend of critical reception, historical influence, and how well each entry holds up, with mainline 3D and 2D platformers weighed above spin-offs.

items.name: Super Mario World
items.reasoning: A Super Famicom launch title that introduced Yoshi and a sprawling secret-laden overworld with Star Road and 96 total exits. Widely regarded as the peak of 2D Mario design.
items.subtitle: SNES, 1990

items.name: Super Mario 64
items.reasoning: An N64 launch title that defined the 3D platformer with its analog free-roam movement; earlier 3D platformers existed, but this set the template for a generation. Its camera and level design shaped the genre for years.
items.subtitle: N64, 1996

items.name: Super Mario Odyssey
items.reasoning: Cappy's capture mechanic lets Mario possess enemies and objects across dense sandbox kingdoms holding 880 unique Power Moons. A critical darling that has sold over 28 million copies.
items.subtitle: Switch, 2017

items.name: Super Mario Galaxy
items.reasoning: Spherical gravity-bending planetoids paired with a full orchestral score redefined 3D platforming. Its 97 Metacritic ranks among the highest scores of any game.
items.subtitle: Wii, 2007

items.name: Super Mario Bros. 3
items.reasoning: The Tanooki suit, world map, and power-up variety made it the definitive NES platformer. A landmark that anchored the whole franchise's identity.
items.subtitle: NES, 1988

items.name: Super Mario Galaxy 2
items.reasoning: Doubled down on inventive one-off gravity gimmicks and added rideable Yoshi with power-up berries. Arguably a tighter, more concentrated challenge than the original.
items.subtitle: Wii, 2010

items.name: Super Mario Kart
items.reasoning: Founded the entire kart-racing genre with drifting, items, and split-screen battle mode. Its influence stretches far beyond Nintendo.
items.subtitle: SNES, 1992

items.name: Mario Kart 8 Deluxe
items.reasoning: Anti-gravity tracks and a massive roster made it the best-selling Mario Kart ever, topping 60 million copies. A near-permanent fixture in Switch libraries.
items.subtitle: Switch, 2017

items.name: Super Mario Bros.
items.reasoning: The game that helped revive the home console market after the 1983 crash and set the grammar for side-scrolling platformers. Historically indispensable even if later entries refined the formula.
items.subtitle: NES, 1985

items.name: Super Mario World 2: Yoshi's Island
items.reasoning: Its hand-drawn crayon art and egg-throwing mechanics broke the Mario mold entirely. A bold, beautiful title starring Yoshi with baby Mario in tow.
items.subtitle: SNES, 1995

items.name: Super Mario 3D World
items.reasoning: Four-player co-op and the Cat Suit made this the strongest of the linear 3D Marios. The 2021 Switch re-release added the compact open-world bonus adventure Bowser's Fury.
items.subtitle: Wii U, 2013

items.name: Super Mario Bros. Wonder
items.reasoning: Wonder Flowers warp levels into surreal set-pieces, reinventing 2D Mario after roughly a decade of the safe New Super Mario Bros. formula. A fresh critical hit.
items.subtitle: Switch, 2023

items.name: Paper Mario: The Thousand-Year Door
items.reasoning: Turn-based combat staged before a reactive theater audience, plus sharp writing, made it a cult favorite. Its 2024 Switch remake proved its enduring appeal.
items.subtitle: GameCube, 2004

items.name: Super Mario RPG: Legend of the Seven Stars
items.reasoning: A Square-developed RPG that introduced timed-hit action commands and laid the groundwork for both Paper Mario and Mario & Luigi. A charming genre detour.
items.subtitle: SNES, 1996

items.name: Super Mario Sunshine
items.reasoning: FLUDD's water-jet traversal and a tropical setting gave it personality, but finicky blue-coin hunts and divisive design split fans. A flawed but memorable experiment.
items.subtitle: GameCube, 2002

items.name: New Super Mario Bros.
items.reasoning: Revived 2D Mario platforming for a new era and sold enormously well. Solid but visually generic, kicking off a formula that would grow stale.
items.subtitle: DS, 2006

items.name: Mario Party
items.reasoning: The board-game-plus-minigames party staple that spawned a decades-long series. Iconic, though its rotational analog-stick minigames were blamed for hand injuries, prompting Nintendo to offer protective gloves.
items.subtitle: N64, 1998

items.name: Luigi's Mansion
items.reasoning: A ghost-vacuuming GameCube launch title that put Luigi in the spotlight. Short but atmospheric, it seeded a beloved spin-off series.
items.subtitle: GameCube, 2001

items.name: New Super Mario Bros. 2
items.reasoning: Built largely around coin-collecting gimmickry with little new to offer. One of the most forgettable and formulaic entries in the New series.
items.subtitle: 3DS, 2012

items.name: Mario Is Missing!
items.reasoning: An edutainment game where Luigi answers geography trivia while Mario is captured. Sluggish and dull, it's a notorious low point for the brand.
items.subtitle: NES/SNES/DOS, 1992

items.name: Hotel Mario
items.reasoning: A door-closing puzzle game on the Philips CD-i with infamous, poorly animated cutscenes. Widely cited as one of the worst Mario games ever made.
items.subtitle: CD-i, 1994

title: Pizza Styles, Ranked
tagline: Every pizza worth arguing about, from Naples to the freezer aisle.
basis: Ranked on craft, crust engineering, regional pedigree, and how much of pizza's DNA each style actually carries — with the folklore fact-checked.

items.name: Neapolitan
items.reasoning: The original from Naples, whose craft — the 'Art of the Neapolitan Pizzaiuolo' — was inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list in 2017. Baked in roughly 60-90 seconds in an ~900°F wood-fired oven, it earns a soft, blistered, leopard-spotted cornicione. Its EU-protected STG (Traditional Speciality Guaranteed) recipe of San Marzano tomatoes, mozzarella, and basil is the benchmark all others chase.
items.subtitle: Naples' UNESCO-honored original

items.name: New York Style
items.reasoning: Large, thin, foldable slices with a crisp-yet-chewy crust from high-gluten flour, popularly (if unprovably) credited to NYC's tap water. It's among the most replicated pizzas on Earth for good reason.
items.subtitle: Fold it, don't fork it

items.name: Margherita
items.reasoning: The Neapolitan classic said to have been created in 1889 by pizzaiolo Raffaele Esposito to honor Queen Margherita, its tomato-mozzarella-basil echoing the red-white-green of the Italian flag. The story is likely embellished, but it remains the purest expression of pizza there is.
items.subtitle: Tricolore on a crust

items.name: New Haven Apizza
items.reasoning: Coal-fired, oblong, and charred to a smoky crisp, with the legendary white clam pie from Frank Pepe's (est. 1925) as its crown jewel. Connecticut's cult classic is worshipped by pizza obsessives.
items.subtitle: Coal-charred and clammy

items.name: Sicilian
items.reasoning: A thick, square, focaccia-like pie descended from Sicily's sfincione, with a spongy interior and a fried-crisp bottom from the oiled pan. The airy crumb-to-sauce ratio is nearly unbeatable.
items.subtitle: Thick, square, sublime

items.name: Detroit Style
items.reasoning: Originated at Buddy's in 1946 and baked in blue steel pans repurposed from the auto industry, it delivers caramelized, lacy cheese edges (frico) and racing stripes of sauce laid on top. The crispy-buttery bottom has made it a national obsession.
items.subtitle: Blue-steel-pan perfection

items.name: Grandma Style
items.reasoning: A thin, home-style Long Island sheet-pan pizza pressed into oiled steel, with a shorter proof than Sicilian and garlicky crushed tomatoes. Underrated, crisp, and endlessly craveable.
items.subtitle: Nonna knew best

items.name: Roman al Taglio
items.reasoning: Rectangular, super-high-hydration Roman pizza sold by weight, with an open, airy crumb and shatteringly crisp base. Rome's Gabriele Bonci elevated it into an art form of long fermentation.
items.subtitle: Sold by the slice, by weight

items.name: Chicago Deep Dish
items.reasoning: A buttery, tall-walled pie built inverted with cheese below and chunky tomato on top, requiring a fork and a long bake. Genuinely delicious, but many purists argue it's closer to a casserole.
items.subtitle: A knife-and-fork situation

items.name: Greek Style
items.reasoning: New England's Greek-American diner staple, baked in a shallow oiled pan for a chewy, spongy, olive-oil-fried crust and a tangy, oregano-heavy sauce. A regional workhorse that quietly delivers.
items.subtitle: New England diner royalty

items.name: California Style
items.reasoning: The gourmet innovation popularized by Ed LaDou and Wolfgang Puck at Spago, piling non-traditional toppings like BBQ chicken, goat cheese, or smoked salmon on a thin base. Creative and fresh, if occasionally chaotic.
items.subtitle: Toppings gone gourmet

items.name: Pizza al Padellino
items.reasoning: Turin's small, thick pan pizza (also called al tegamino) with a soft interior and crisp fried edges, essentially a personal-sized Italian deep pan. A cozy northern-Italian gem.
items.subtitle: Turin's little pan pie

items.name: St. Louis Style
items.reasoning: A cracker-thin, yeastless crust topped with the polarizing processed Provel cheese and cut into party squares. Divisive even in Missouri, but a genuine regional identity.
items.subtitle: Provel and tavern cuts

items.name: Neapolitan Calzone
items.reasoning: A folded, sealed half-moon of pizza dough stuffed with ricotta and fillings, then baked. Satisfying, but it trades the crisp-crust-to-topping ratio that makes flat pizza great.
items.subtitle: Pizza's folded cousin

items.name: Chicago Stuffed
items.reasoning: Deep dish's even heftier sibling, sealed with a second layer of dough over the cheese and topped with sauce. Impressive engineering, but the dough-to-flavor payoff drops off fast.
items.subtitle: Deep dish, doubled down

items.name: Hawaiian
items.reasoning: Invented by Sam Panopoulos in Chatham, Ontario, Canada in 1962, its ham and canned pineapple remain the most argued-about pizza on the planet. The sweet-savory combo has passionate defenders but violates Neapolitan orthodoxy.
items.subtitle: The great pineapple war

items.name: Pizza Bagel
items.reasoning: A split bagel smeared with jarred sauce and shredded cheese, broiled or microwaved. A nostalgic snack, but calling this frozen-aisle convenience food a pizza style is a stretch.
items.subtitle: The freezer-aisle imposter

items.name: Pizza Cake
items.reasoning: A viral novelty stacking multiple pizza layers into a towering cake, prioritizing shock value over edibility. All gimmick, soggy middle, no soul.
items.subtitle: A crime against dough

title: Video Game Villains, Ranked
tagline: The masterminds, tyrants, and memes who make gaming worth losing to.
basis: Ranked on cultural impact, writing/performance quality, iconicity, and staying power as antagonists.

items.name: GLaDOS
items.reasoning: The passive-aggressive AI of Valve's Portal (2007) weaponizes sarcasm, cake, and neurotoxin, and the closing song 'Still Alive' (written by Jonathan Coulton) made her one of gaming's most quoted characters. A villain who is genuinely funny and genuinely terrifying at once.
items.subtitle: Portal

items.name: Bowser
items.reasoning: The Koopa King has kidnapped Princess Peach and fought Mario since Super Mario Bros. in 1985, making him one of the most enduring villains in gaming history. Instantly recognizable across nearly every Nintendo era.
items.subtitle: Super Mario

items.name: Sephiroth
items.reasoning: His murder of Aerith is one of the most infamous moments in RPG history, and the battle theme 'One-Winged Angel' set the standard for villain music. His silver hair and the Masamune are instantly iconic.
items.subtitle: Final Fantasy VII

items.name: Ganondorf
items.reasoning: Wielder of the Triforce of Power, he has been Link's eternal nemesis since the beast form Ganon debuted in 1986; his humanized Gerudo-king incarnation in Ocarina of Time (1998) set the template for the tragic, power-hungry fantasy tyrant.
items.subtitle: The Legend of Zelda

items.name: Handsome Jack
items.reasoning: The CEO of Hyperion is a charismatic, genuinely hilarious egomaniac who taunts you constantly, making him one of the most beloved written villains of the 2010s. You hate him because he's so good at being awful.
items.subtitle: Borderlands 2

items.name: Vaas Montenegro
items.reasoning: His 'definition of insanity' monologue became a viral phenomenon and elevated Far Cry 3's whole story. Michael Mando's performance made a mid-game villain unforgettable.
items.subtitle: Far Cry 3

items.name: Kefka Palazzo
items.reasoning: The nihilistic court mage is one of the few JRPG villains who actually wins, remaking the world in his image. A cackling agent of chaos often called gaming's answer to the Joker.
items.subtitle: Final Fantasy VI

items.name: Andrew Ryan
items.reasoning: The objectivist founder of Rapture anchors one of gaming's greatest twists with 'A man chooses, a slave obeys' and the 'Would you kindly?' reveal, which redefined how a game could weaponize player agency in its narrative.
items.subtitle: BioShock

items.name: Pyramid Head
items.reasoning: A manifestation of the protagonist's guilt, dragging his massive blade through the fog, he became the enduring face of survival horror. Pure psychological dread given a body.
items.subtitle: Silent Hill 2

items.name: Dr. Eggman
items.reasoning: The rotund, mustachioed mad scientist has chased Sonic with badniks and Death Eggs since 1991. A cartoonish but reliably entertaining foil for the blue blur.
items.subtitle: Sonic the Hedgehog

items.name: Albert Wesker
items.reasoning: The sunglasses-wearing turncoat masterminds the series' conspiracies and gained superhuman speed and strength from an experimental prototype virus during the Raccoon City era. Camp, menace, and slow-motion dodges all in one.
items.subtitle: Resident Evil

items.name: Nemesis
items.reasoning: The relentless bioweapon stalks Jill Valentine through Raccoon City, its 'STARS...' growl a horror icon. It defined the pursuer-enemy trope survival horror still copies.
items.subtitle: Resident Evil 3

items.name: Diablo
items.reasoning: The Lord of Terror is the literal titular demon of Blizzard's action-RPG franchise, driving three decades of dungeon crawling since 1996. A foundational face of the demonic-horde genre.
items.subtitle: Diablo series

items.name: M. Bison
items.reasoning: The Psycho Power dictator is the final boss of the fighting genre's most famous series, complete with his in-game 'Yes! Yes!' taunt. His infamous 'For me, it was Tuesday' line actually comes from the 1994 live-action film (Raul Julia), not the games. Delightfully over-the-top evil either way.
items.subtitle: Street Fighter

items.name: Ridley
items.reasoning: The vicious space dragon and Samus's recurring nemesis killed her parents and leads the Space Pirates. A fan-favorite finally vindicated by joining Super Smash Bros. Ultimate in 2018.
items.subtitle: Metroid

items.name: Wario
items.reasoning: Mario's greedy, garlic-loving anti-hero began as a straight villain in Super Mario Land 2 (1992) before spinning off into his own games. More comic rival than true threat, but an enduring mischief-maker.
items.subtitle: Mario / WarioWare

items.name: Dr. Wily
items.reasoning: The scientist who reprograms Robot Masters is Mega Man's perennial foe, always begging forgiveness before scheming again. Reliable but repetitive across dozens of sequels.
items.subtitle: Mega Man

items.name: King Dedede
items.reasoning: The self-proclaimed penguin king of Dream Land is more bumbling glutton than menace, often turning ally by game's end. Charming, but rarely an actual threat.
items.subtitle: Kirby

items.name: Waluigi
items.reasoning: Introduced in Mario Tennis (2000) purely to give Wario a doubles partner, he has no real game, story, or menace of his own. A meme mascot who is a villain in name only.
items.subtitle: Mario spinoffs

title: Birds, Ranked
tagline: Birds, Ranked: from feathered geniuses to feathered menaces
basis: Ranked on a blend of intelligence, physical prowess, ecological impact, and sheer charisma — with accuracy of every claim verified.

items.name: Bald eagle
items.reasoning: A massive sea eagle with a wingspan reaching up to 2.3 meters in large females, it snatches fish from the surface and pirates kills from ospreys. Rescued from near-extinction after DDT was banned, it was removed from the U.S. endangered species list in 2007.
items.subtitle: The living flag

items.name: Common raven
items.reasoning: One of the smartest animals alive, ravens solve multi-step puzzles, plan for the future, and use tools. They can mimic human speech and remember individual human faces for years.
items.subtitle: Feathered genius

items.name: Peregrine falcon
items.reasoning: In a hunting stoop it exceeds 320 km/h, making it the fastest animal on the planet. Bony baffles in its nostrils redirect airflow so it can breathe at speeds that would otherwise overwhelm its lungs.
items.subtitle: Fastest animal on Earth

items.name: Emperor penguin
items.reasoning: Males incubate a single egg on their feet through Antarctic blizzards for roughly two months without eating, huddling in slowly rotating masses to survive. They dive over 500 meters chasing fish and squid.
items.subtitle: Antarctic endurance champ

items.name: Snowy owl
items.reasoning: A heavy Arctic owl feathered down to its toes, it hunts by day across open tundra and an adult can take on the order of 1,600 lemmings a year. Its near-silent flight makes it a phantom predator.
items.subtitle: Ghost of the tundra

items.name: Kea
items.reasoning: The world's only true alpine parrot, this New Zealand native famously dismantles car wipers, rubber seals, and backpacks out of sheer curiosity. It passes cognition and cooperation tests that rival those of great apes.
items.subtitle: The alpine trickster

items.name: Hummingbird
items.reasoning: The only birds capable of sustained backward flight, they beat their wings up to around 80 times per second and drop into nightly torpor to survive. Their hearts can exceed 1,200 beats per minute.
items.subtitle: The hovering jewel

items.name: Barn owl
items.reasoning: Its heart-shaped facial disc funnels sound so precisely it can strike prey in total darkness by hearing alone. It is one of the most widespread birds on Earth, found on every continent except Antarctica.
items.subtitle: Silent night hunter

items.name: Mallard
items.reasoning: The ancestor of nearly all domestic duck breeds and one of the most abundant, widespread ducks on Earth, thriving across every continent but Antarctica. Reliable, iridescent, and unbothered.
items.subtitle: The everyduck

items.name: American robin
items.reasoning: A thrush that locates earthworms using both sight and hearing, it's one of the first songbirds to strike up at dawn. Its spring return is a classic seasonal marker across North America.
items.subtitle: Herald of spring

items.name: Northern cardinal
items.reasoning: A brilliant red songbird whose color comes from carotenoids in its diet, it holds territory year-round and is the state bird of seven U.S. states. Unusually, the female sings too.
items.subtitle: The scarlet regular

items.name: Blue jay
items.reasoning: A bold corvid that mimics hawk calls to clear rivals off feeders and caches thousands of acorns, effectively helping plant oak forests. Beautiful, brash, and a bit of a bully.
items.subtitle: Loud and clever

items.name: Ostrich
items.reasoning: The largest living bird can sprint at around 70 km/h and delivers kicks powerful enough to kill a large predator. It lays the biggest egg of any living animal.
items.subtitle: The flightless heavyweight

items.name: Rock dove
items.reasoning: Descended from cliff-nesting birds, the ubiquitous urban pigeon can navigate home over hundreds of kilometers and can recognize individual human faces and even distinguish paintings by different artists. Underrated brains, overrated mess.
items.subtitle: The city pigeon

items.name: House sparrow
items.reasoning: Spread worldwide by humans, this scrappy generalist thrives in cities from Sydney to New York. Adaptable to a fault, it often edges out native cavity- and cranny-nesting songbirds.
items.subtitle: The stowaway survivor

items.name: Wild turkey
items.reasoning: Far sharper than its dopey reputation, the wild turkey can fly in short bursts near 90 km/h and roosts high in trees. Ben Franklin praised it in a private letter; Thanksgiving disagrees.
items.subtitle: Surprisingly capable

items.name: Canada goose
items.reasoning: Aggressively territorial and unafraid of people, these geese hiss, chase, and blanket parks in droppings. A flock famously took down US Airways Flight 1549, forcing its 2009 landing in the Hudson.
items.subtitle: The airport menace

items.name: European starling
items.reasoning: Released in New York's Central Park in 1890 by Eugene Schieffelin's acclimatization society — the popular Shakespeare-inspiration tale is now disputed — starlings spread to an estimated 85 to 200 million birds and displace native cavity-nesters across North America. Great mimics, ecological wrecking ball.
items.subtitle: The 200-million invader

items.name: Dodo
items.reasoning: A flightless Mauritian relative of pigeons, driven extinct within roughly a century of human contact, largely by introduced rats and pigs raiding its ground nests plus hunting. Its name is now literally synonymous with obsolescence.
items.subtitle: The cautionary tale

items.name: Cassowary
items.reasoning: Often called the world's most dangerous bird, it wields a dagger-like inner claw up to about 12 cm long and has caused rare human deaths with powerful slashing kicks. Magnificent, but genuinely capable of killing.
items.subtitle: The dinosaur that kills

title: Muscle Cars, Ranked
tagline: The classic American muscle era, from segment-founders to the malaise misfire.
basis: Ranked on muscle-car significance: historical impact, performance credentials, and cultural icon status across the classic era.

items.name: Pontiac GTO
items.reasoning: The 1964 GTO stuffed a 389 V8 into the mid-size Tempest/LeMans as an option package and is widely credited as the car that launched the classic muscle car era. It set the template: big engine, light body, affordable price.
items.subtitle: The one that started it all

items.name: Dodge Charger
items.reasoning: The 1968-70 second-gen Charger paired aggressive Coke-bottle styling with available 426 HEMI power, and became a screen legend as the black villain's car in Bullitt (1968) and later as the General Lee in The Dukes of Hazzard. Few shapes say muscle car louder.
items.subtitle: Coke-bottle menace

items.name: Chevrolet Chevelle SS
items.reasoning: The 1970 Chevelle SS 454 LS6 was factory-rated at 450 hp (gross), among the highest ratings of the classic muscle era. It is the definitive big-block Chevy street brawler.
items.subtitle: The 454 heavyweight

items.name: Ford Mustang
items.reasoning: Launched in April 1964, the Mustang created the pony-car segment and hit one million units built by March 1966, under two years in. The Boss 302 and 428 Cobra Jet versions cemented its performance credibility.
items.subtitle: The pony car blueprint

items.name: Plymouth Barracuda
items.reasoning: The 1970-71 HEMI 'Cuda is among the most valuable muscle cars ever, with rare convertibles selling for millions at auction. Its sharp E-body styling and 426 HEMI made it an instant icon.
items.subtitle: HEMI 'Cuda royalty

items.name: Chevrolet Camaro
items.reasoning: Chevy's 1967 pony car countered the Mustang and gave us the legendary Z/28 and the rare COPO 427 specials, whose L72 big-block was rated at 425 hp (the all-aluminum ZL1 at 430 hp). A perennial rival that has stayed in the fight for decades.
items.subtitle: Ford's answer, answered

items.name: Oldsmobile 442
items.reasoning: The 442 combined a potent 400 (later 455) V8 with better handling and refinement than most rivals, and the W-30 package was a genuine performer. Underrated then, coveted now.
items.subtitle: The gentleman's muscle car

items.name: Buick GSX
items.reasoning: The 1970 GSX Stage 1 made a class-leading 510 lb-ft of torque from its 455, out-pulling most everything on the street. A sleeper luxury-brand bruiser.
items.subtitle: Torque monster in a suit

items.name: Plymouth Road Runner
items.reasoning: Plymouth stripped a mid-size to the essentials and sold it cheap with a standard 383 (or optional 426 HEMI), even paying Warner Bros. to license the Road Runner name and beep-beep horn. Muscle for the working man, done right.
items.subtitle: Cheap speed, beep beep

items.name: Pontiac Firebird Trans Am
items.reasoning: The Trans Am, with its hood-decal 'screaming chicken' and available 455 power, became a 1970s icon and starred in Smokey and the Bandit (1977). Peak Pontiac swagger.
items.subtitle: Screaming chicken

items.name: Dodge Challenger
items.reasoning: Arriving in 1970, the Challenger shared HEMI power with the 'Cuda and starred as the white 1970 model in Vanishing Point. It joined the party late but with real presence.
items.subtitle: E-body latecomer

items.name: Ford Torino
items.reasoning: The Torino Cobra with the 428 Cobra Jet was a serious performer, and the Talladega aero variant was built for and dominated NASCAR superspeedways in 1969. Often overlooked among the flashier names.
items.subtitle: Cobra Jet fastback

items.name: Mercury Cougar
items.reasoning: The Cougar shared Mustang underpinnings but added length, luxury, and the hot Eliminator package with available 428 CJ power. A refined take on the pony formula.
items.subtitle: Upscale Mustang cousin

items.name: AMC Javelin
items.reasoning: AMC's Javelin punched above its weight in Trans-Am racing (winning the 1971 SCCA title with Penske/Donohue) and offered the Go Package with a 390 V8. Scrappy proof the little independent could play.
items.subtitle: The underdog pony car

items.name: Chevrolet Nova SS
items.reasoning: The Nova SS 396 crammed a big-block into a light compact body, making it a giant-killer at the strip. Unassuming looks, serious bite.
items.subtitle: Compact sleeper

items.name: AMC AMX
items.reasoning: The 1968-70 AMX was a rare American two-seat muscle car with genuine 390 V8 punch, but its short wheelbase and niche appeal limited its reach. Interesting more than dominant.
items.subtitle: The two-seat oddball

items.name: Dodge Super Bee
items.reasoning: Essentially Dodge's budget Coronet-based answer to the Road Runner, it delivered solid HEMI/440 muscle but lived in its Plymouth cousin's shadow. Good, just less remembered.
items.subtitle: Road Runner's Dodge twin

items.name: Ford Mustang II
items.reasoning: The 1974 Mustang II arrived amid the oil crisis on a Pinto-derived platform with anemic engines and no V8 available at launch (a 302 returned for 1975). It is widely blamed for gutting the Mustang's performance name during the malaise era.
items.subtitle: The malaise misfire

title: EDM DJs, Ranked
tagline: The producers and selectors who built modern dance music — ranked on influence, catalog, and staying power.
basis: Ranked by genre influence, catalog strength, live/festival impact, and commercial reach across the history of electronic dance music.

items.name: Daft Punk
items.reasoning: The robot duo defined modern electronic music with 'Homework' (1997), 'Discovery' (2001), and the Grammy-sweeping 'Random Access Memories' (2013), which won Album of the Year, influencing nearly every producer who followed.
items.subtitle: French house architects

items.name: Avicii
items.reasoning: Tim Bergling fused folk and EDM on 'Wake Me Up' and 'Levels,' becoming one of the genre's most influential producers before his death in 2018.
items.subtitle: Melodic house pioneer

items.name: Calvin Harris
items.reasoning: Set a Guinness record for the most UK Top 10 singles from a single album with 2012's '18 Months' (breaking Michael Jackson's 'Bad' mark) and topped Forbes' highest-paid DJ list for six straight years with pop-crossover smashes.
items.subtitle: The hit machine

items.name: deadmau5
items.reasoning: Joel Zimmerman built progressive-house landmarks like 'Strobe' and 'Ghosts 'n' Stuff' and pioneered the elaborate stage-cube live show.
items.subtitle: The mouse head

items.name: Skrillex
items.reasoning: Sonny Moore detonated dubstep in the US with the 'Scary Monsters and Nice Sprites' EP and has won eight Grammys, more than any other dubstep/EDM producer of his era.
items.subtitle: Brostep's godfather

items.name: The Chemical Brothers
items.reasoning: Pioneers of big beat with 'Block Rockin' Beats' and 'Galvanize,' they remain a benchmark live act with multiple Grammys across three decades.
items.subtitle: Big beat titans

items.name: Swedish House Mafia
items.reasoning: The trio of Axwell, Ingrosso, and Angello brought progressive house to arenas with 'Don't You Worry Child' and 'One,' defining the early-2010s festival sound.
items.subtitle: Stadium supergroup

items.name: David Guetta
items.reasoning: Bridged club and radio with 'When Love Takes Over' and 'Titanium,' making EDM a permanent fixture on Top 40 worldwide.
items.subtitle: Pop-EDM crossover king

items.name: Tiesto
items.reasoning: A trance pioneer who evolved into a mainstream hitmaker with 'The Business,' he was voted the world's number-one DJ by DJ Mag three years running (2002-2004).
items.subtitle: Trance-to-pop veteran

items.name: Aphex Twin
items.reasoning: Richard D. James pushed electronic music into experimental territory with 'Selected Ambient Works' and 'Windowlicker,' a critical darling revered by producers.
items.subtitle: IDM auteur

items.name: Zedd
items.reasoning: A classically trained producer whose Grammy-winning 'Clarity' and 'Stay' pair polished melodic house with mainstream vocal hooks.
items.subtitle: Clean pop-EDM

items.name: Martin Garrix
items.reasoning: Broke out at 17 with 2013's 'Animals' and topped DJ Mag's Top 100 poll three straight years (2016-2018), headlining Tomorrowland and Ultra.
items.subtitle: The teen prodigy

items.name: Diplo
items.reasoning: Fronts Major Lazer and Jack U, blending dancehall, trap, and pop, and co-produced Beyonce's 'Run the World (Girls)' among countless crossover hits.
items.subtitle: Genre-blending mogul

items.name: Kaskade
items.reasoning: A prolific progressive and deep house producer and a Vegas residency staple, respected but never quite a global chart force.
items.subtitle: Deep house mainstay

items.name: Steve Aoki
items.reasoning: A relentless touring workhorse and collaborator whose sets are known more for cake-tossing spectacle than for standout production.
items.subtitle: The cake thrower

items.name: Alesso
items.reasoning: A solid Swedish producer behind 'Heroes' and 'Years' who delivers festival anthems but rarely breaks new ground.
items.subtitle: Progressive house reliable

items.name: Marshmello
items.reasoning: The helmet-wearing producer scored massive pop crossovers with 'Happier' and 'Alone,' though critics dismiss the sound as formulaic.
items.subtitle: Masked hitmaker

title: DreamWorks Animated Films, Ranked
tagline: DreamWorks' animated catalog, from the ogre that built an empire to the snail that broke the bank.
basis: Ranked on artistic achievement, cultural impact, and critical standing, cross-checked against awards, box-office, and release-history facts.

items.name: Shrek
items.reasoning: Won the very first Academy Award for Best Animated Feature in 2002 and redefined the studio with its irreverent fairy-tale satire. A genuine cultural phenomenon that still dominates memes decades later.
items.subtitle: 2001 - The one that started it all

items.name: How to Train Your Dragon
items.reasoning: A sweeping, emotionally rich coming-of-age story with groundbreaking flight sequences and John Powell's Oscar-nominated score. Widely considered DreamWorks' artistic peak.

art.title: How to Train Your Dragon (2010 film)

items.subtitle: 2010 - Boy meets dragon

items.name: Kung Fu Panda
items.reasoning: Gorgeous martial-arts choreography, a heartfelt underdog story, and Jack Black at his most charming. Launched one of DreamWorks' strongest franchises.
items.subtitle: 2008 - There is no charge for awesomeness

items.name: Shrek 2
items.reasoning: Bigger, funnier, and even more successful than the original, becoming the highest-grossing film of 2004. Puss in Boots' debut alone earns its place.
items.subtitle: 2004 - Meet the parents

items.name: How to Train Your Dragon 2
items.reasoning: An ambitious, darker sequel that expands the world and delivers a gut-punch emotional turn. Won the Golden Globe for Best Animated Feature.
items.subtitle: 2014 - The dragon rider grows up

items.name: Kung Fu Panda 2
items.reasoning: A surprisingly poignant sequel with a compelling villain in Lord Shen and stunning 2D-animated flourishes in its storybook sequences. Earned an Oscar nomination for Best Animated Feature.
items.subtitle: 2011 - Inner peace

items.name: Puss in Boots: The Last Wish
items.reasoning: A gorgeous stylistic reinvention with a genuinely terrifying Death as its antagonist, earning an Oscar nomination and rave reviews. Proof DreamWorks can still innovate.

art.title: Puss in Boots: The Last Wish

items.subtitle: 2022 - Nine lives, one wish

items.name: The Prince of Egypt
items.reasoning: A visually stunning, reverent retelling of the Exodus with the Oscar-winning song 'When You Believe.' DreamWorks' first and most artistically ambitious traditionally-animated film.
items.subtitle: 1998 - Let my people go

items.name: Madagascar
items.reasoning: A brisk, joke-dense zoo-animals-in-the-wild comedy that spawned a hugely popular franchise and the meme-immortal 'I Like to Move It.' Broad but reliably fun.
items.subtitle: 2005 - Escape to the wild

items.name: Megamind
items.reasoning: A clever superhero-genre subversion with Will Ferrell voicing a lovable villain-turned-hero. Underrated on release and now a cult favorite.
items.subtitle: 2010 - The bad guy wins

items.name: Puss in Boots
items.reasoning: A stylish, swashbuckling Shrek spinoff that gives Antonio Banderas' cat his own charming origin story, earning a Best Animated Feature Oscar nomination. Solid fun even if it lacks the depth of its sequel.
items.subtitle: 2011 - Before the last wish

items.name: Kung Fu Panda 3
items.reasoning: A warm, visually lush conclusion to Po's arc with the spirit realm and family themes front and center. Good, if the least essential of the trilogy.
items.subtitle: 2016 - Return to the panda village

items.name: Trolls
items.reasoning: A candy-colored jukebox musical carried by 'Can't Stop the Feeling!' and Anna Kendrick's charm. Aggressively cheerful and a merchandising juggernaut.
items.subtitle: 2016 - Hug time

items.name: Antz
items.reasoning: DreamWorks' first CGI feature, a wittier-than-expected adult comedy with Woody Allen voicing a neurotic worker ant. Released weeks before Pixar's same-year A Bug's Life, which overshadowed it.
items.subtitle: 1998 - One ant's rebellion

items.name: Madagascar 3: Europe's Most Wanted
items.reasoning: A frantic, colorful circus caper that's the most manic of the trilogy. Fun in bursts but leans hard on chaos over story.
items.subtitle: 2012 - Afro Circus

items.name: Monsters vs. Aliens
items.reasoning: A loving throwback to 1950s B-movies with fun set pieces but thin characters. Enjoyable but forgettable compared to the studio's best.
items.subtitle: 2009 - Big problems need big heroes

items.name: Shrek the Third
items.reasoning: A noticeable dip in the franchise, feeling tired and joke-recycled after two strong outings. Still watchable but the weakest of the core Shrek films.
items.subtitle: 2007 - The kingdom problem

items.name: Bee Movie
items.reasoning: Jerry Seinfeld's bizarre bee-sues-humanity premise is more famous as an internet meme than a good film. Strange, uneven, and unforgettable for the wrong reasons.
items.subtitle: 2007 - Ya like jazz?

items.name: Shark Tale
items.reasoning: A shallow, celebrity-voice-stuffed knockoff riding the wave of the prior year's Finding Nemo, with garish design and dated pop-culture gags. Widely regarded as early-DreamWorks at its most cynical, despite a surprise Oscar nomination.
items.subtitle: 2004 - Fish out of luck

items.name: Shrek Forever After
items.reasoning: A low-energy 'It's a Wonderful Life' alternate-reality retread that marked a clear creative low for the franchise. A commercial success at over $750M worldwide, but proof the ogre's inspiration had run dry.
items.subtitle: 2010 - The final chapter

items.name: Turbo
items.reasoning: A racing-snail premise stretched thin, it underperformed badly and, alongside other misfires, helped trigger the 2015 DreamWorks layoffs and studio restructuring. One of the studio's costliest commercial stumbles.
items.subtitle: 2013 - The snail that dreamed too big

title: Documentaries, Ranked
tagline: Real life, sorted from unforgettable to unforgivable.
basis: Ranked on craft, impact, and honesty — how well each film illuminates something true, weighed against sensationalism and outright deception.

items.name: Hoop Dreams
items.reasoning: Roger Ebert named it the best film of the 1990s, and its shocking Oscar snub for Best Documentary directly forced the Academy to overhaul its nomination rules.
items.subtitle: Two kids, one impossible dream

items.name: The Act of Killing
items.reasoning: Joshua Oppenheimer got Indonesian death-squad leaders to reenact their 1965 killings as movie scenes; it earned an Oscar nomination with Werner Herzog and Errol Morris as executive producers.
items.subtitle: Killers restage their own crimes

items.name: Planet Earth
items.reasoning: The 2006 BBC series narrated by David Attenborough was the first nature documentary series filmed in high definition and won multiple Emmy Awards for its groundbreaking cinematography.
items.subtitle: The gold standard of nature TV

items.name: Man on Wire
items.reasoning: James Marsh's account of Philippe Petit's 1974 high-wire walk between the World Trade Center towers won the 2009 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
items.subtitle: A tightrope between the Towers

items.name: Free Solo
items.reasoning: Chronicling Alex Honnold's ropeless climb of Yosemite's 3,000-foot El Capitan, it won the 2019 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
items.subtitle: El Capitan, no rope

items.name: Won't You Be My Neighbor?
items.reasoning: Morgan Neville's portrait of Mister Rogers became the highest-grossing biographical documentary in the U.S. at the time of its 2018 release.
items.subtitle: Fred Rogers, up close

items.name: 13th
items.reasoning: Ava DuVernay's essay on mass incarceration and the 13th Amendment earned an Oscar nomination and won an Emmy for Outstanding Documentary.
items.subtitle: Slavery's loophole, tracked

items.name: The Last Dance
items.reasoning: The ESPN/Netflix series on Michael Jordan and the 1997-98 Chicago Bulls drew record ratings and won the Emmy for Outstanding Documentary Series.
items.subtitle: Jordan's final Bulls run

items.name: Grizzly Man
items.reasoning: Werner Herzog's film assembles the footage of Timothy Treadwell, who lived among Alaskan grizzlies until he and his girlfriend were killed and eaten by one in 2003.
items.subtitle: Loving bears to death

items.name: Bowling for Columbine
items.reasoning: Moore's examination of American gun culture won the 2003 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, though critics have flagged its selective editing.
items.subtitle: Michael Moore takes aim

items.name: Searching for Sugar Man
items.reasoning: Malik Bendjelloul's film about forgotten musician Rodriguez, a star in South Africa without his knowledge, won the 2013 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
items.subtitle: The rock legend who never knew

items.name: Amy
items.reasoning: Asif Kapadia built the film almost entirely from archival footage of Amy Winehouse and won the 2016 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature.
items.subtitle: Winehouse in her own words

items.name: Blackfish
items.reasoning: Its focus on captive killer whale Tilikum triggered the 'Blackfish effect,' a documented drop in SeaWorld attendance that pushed the company to end its orca-breeding program.
items.subtitle: The orca that changed SeaWorld

items.name: March of the Penguins
items.reasoning: Luc Jacquet's chronicle of emperor penguins' breeding trek won the 2006 Academy Award and became one of the highest-grossing documentaries ever.
items.subtitle: The coldest commute on Earth

items.name: Super Size Me
items.reasoning: Morgan Spurlock's month-long fast-food stunt earned an Oscar nomination, but its findings were later undercut when Spurlock admitted to long-hidden habits that skewed the results.
items.subtitle: 30 days of McDonald's

items.name: An Inconvenient Truth
items.reasoning: The film built around Al Gore's climate lecture won the 2007 Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature and helped push global warming into mainstream debate.
items.subtitle: Al Gore's climate slideshow

items.name: Tiger King
items.reasoning: Netflix's Joe Exotic saga became a pandemic-era phenomenon in 2020 but drew heavy criticism for prioritizing lurid personalities over its animal-welfare subject.
items.subtitle: Big cats, bigger chaos

items.name: Making a Murderer
items.reasoning: The Netflix series on Steven Avery was a sensation, but the prosecutor and jurors accused it of omitting key evidence to build a misleadingly sympathetic case.
items.subtitle: True crime, one-sided

items.name: What the Health
items.reasoning: The 2017 pro-vegan film was widely rebuked by nutrition experts and fact-checkers for misrepresenting studies and drawing alarmist conclusions unsupported by the research it cited.
items.subtitle: Diet science, cherry-picked

items.name: Vaxxed
items.reasoning: Directed by discredited former physician Andrew Wakefield, it pushes the thoroughly debunked vaccine-autism link and was pulled from the Tribeca Film Festival amid an outcry.
items.subtitle: Debunked propaganda

title: Western Movies, Ranked
tagline: Saddle up. The genre that built Hollywood, sorted by who actually earned their spurs.
basis: Ranked on craft, influence on the Western genre, and staying power, from the operatic classics down to the box-office disasters that gave cowboys a bad name.

heroArt.title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly

items.name: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
items.reasoning: Sergio Leone's Dollars Trilogy capstone with Ennio Morricone's iconic score is routinely cited as the greatest Western ever made. Its three-way graveyard standoff and 178-minute epic scope defined the spaghetti Western.

art.title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly

items.subtitle: Leone, 1966

items.name: Once Upon a Time in the West
items.reasoning: Leone's sprawling elegy cast Henry Fonda against type as a cold-blooded killer and opens with a near-wordless 10-minute train-station sequence. It's preserved in the U.S. National Film Registry.

art.title: Once Upon a Time in the West

items.subtitle: Leone, 1968

items.name: Unforgiven
items.reasoning: Clint Eastwood's revisionist masterpiece won four Oscars including Best Picture and Best Director. It deglamorizes gunfighting as ugly, traumatic work rather than heroic myth.
items.subtitle: Eastwood, 1992

items.name: The Searchers
items.reasoning: John Ford and John Wayne's Monument Valley epic topped AFI's Western ranking and is routinely named the greatest Western in critics' polls. Its final doorway shot is one of the most analyzed images in film history.
items.subtitle: Ford, 1956

items.name: No Country for Old Men
items.reasoning: The Coen Brothers' neo-Western won four Oscars including Best Picture, and Javier Bardem's Anton Chigurh took Best Supporting Actor. Its coin-toss menace redefined modern frontier dread.
items.subtitle: Coens, 2007

items.name: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid
items.reasoning: Paul Newman and Robert Redford's buddy Western won four Oscars and was the top-grossing film of 1969. The freeze-frame finale and 'Raindrops Keep Fallin' on My Head' are pop-culture fixtures.

art.title: Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid

items.subtitle: Hill, 1969

items.name: High Noon
items.reasoning: Gary Cooper won Best Actor as a marshal abandoned by his town, in a film told in near real-time as an allegory for Hollywood blacklist cowardice. It won four Academy Awards.
items.subtitle: Zinnemann, 1952

items.name: Django Unchained
items.reasoning: Quentin Tarantino's slavery-era revenge Western grossed over $425 million and won two Oscars, including Best Original Screenplay and Best Supporting Actor for Christoph Waltz.
items.subtitle: Tarantino, 2012

items.name: The Wild Bunch
items.reasoning: Sam Peckinpah's aging-outlaws saga shocked 1969 audiences with slow-motion, blood-soaked gun battles that changed how screen violence was filmed. It's in the National Film Registry.
items.subtitle: Peckinpah, 1969

items.name: Tombstone
items.reasoning: Val Kilmer's tubercular Doc Holliday ('I'm your huckleberry') became instantly quotable in this crowd-pleasing take on the O.K. Corral gunfight. It endures as a cable-rewatch staple.

items.name: True Grit
items.reasoning: The Coens' faithful adaptation earned 10 Oscar nominations and grossed over $250 million. Fourteen-year-old Hailee Steinfeld anchored it opposite Jeff Bridges' Rooster Cogburn.
items.subtitle: Coens, 2010

items.name: 3:10 to Yuma
items.reasoning: James Mangold's remake pits Christian Bale's broke rancher against Russell Crowe's charming outlaw in a tense escort thriller. It drew strong reviews and two Oscar nominations.

items.name: The Magnificent Seven
items.reasoning: This Hollywood reworking of Kurosawa's Seven Samurai assembled Yul Brynner, Steve McQueen and Charles Bronson, and Elmer Bernstein's theme became one of film's most recognizable scores.

items.name: Dances with Wolves
