items.subtitle: Model of consistency

items.name: Miami Heat
items.reasoning: Three championships since 2006 and the launchpad for the LeBron-Wade-Bosh Big Three that reached four straight Finals.
items.subtitle: Heat culture

items.name: Philadelphia 76ers
items.reasoning: Three championships including the 68-13 1967 juggernaut, though the title drought now stretches back to 1983.
items.subtitle: Wilt to The Process

items.name: Detroit Pistons
items.reasoning: Three championships built on defense, with back-to-back titles in 1989-90 and a 2004 upset of the star-studded Lakers.
items.subtitle: Bad Boys, tough defense

items.name: Milwaukee Bucks
items.reasoning: Two championships, 50 years apart, with Kareem in 1971 and Giannis Antetokounmpo ending the drought in 2021.
items.subtitle: Fear the Deer

items.name: Oklahoma City Thunder
items.reasoning: Captured the 2025 NBA championship behind MVP Shai Gilgeous-Alexander after years of building a contender.
items.subtitle: Young core, fresh crown

items.name: Denver Nuggets
items.reasoning: Won their first-ever championship in 2023 led by three-time MVP Nikola Jokic after decades without a Finals berth.
items.subtitle: Mile High breakthrough

items.name: Dallas Mavericks
items.reasoning: Won the 2011 title when Dirk Nowitzki's Mavs upset the LeBron-led Heat, their lone championship.
items.subtitle: Dirk's crowning moment

items.name: New York Knicks
items.reasoning: Two championships in 1970 and 1973, but no title since and only sporadic contention in the half-century since.
items.subtitle: Big market, long wait

items.name: Cleveland Cavaliers
items.reasoning: Delivered the city's first major title in 52 years by erasing a 3-1 Finals deficit against Golden State in 2016.
items.subtitle: The Block, The Shot

items.name: Phoenix Suns
items.reasoning: A perennial playoff team that has reached the Finals three times but never won a championship in franchise history.
items.subtitle: Seven seconds, no ring

items.name: Sacramento Kings
items.reasoning: One title back in 1951 as the Rochester Royals, followed by a 16-season playoff drought, the longest in NBA history, that they finally snapped in 2023.
items.subtitle: Decades in the lottery

items.name: Los Angeles Clippers
items.reasoning: No championships and no Finals appearances despite recent superstar rosters, long dogged by the franchise's futility reputation.
items.subtitle: Star-crossed and ringless

items.name: Charlotte Hornets
items.reasoning: Zero titles and no Finals appearances, with just four playoff series wins across a history defined by lottery mediocrity.
items.subtitle: Buzz City blues

title: Soccer Clubs, Ranked
tagline: Trophies talk. Everyone else can queue for the Europa League.
basis: Ranked on trophy haul, European pedigree, sustained modern dominance, and global stature — not vibes or your granddad's memories.

items.name: Real Madrid CF
items.reasoning: Record 15-time European Cup/Champions League winners, having won the first five editions in a row from 1956 to 1960. No club has come close to that continental haul.
items.subtitle: Los Blancos · Madrid

items.name: FC Barcelona
items.reasoning: Five Champions League titles and the Guardiola-era tiki-taka side widely rated among the greatest club teams ever. Home of Messi's record 672 club goals.
items.subtitle: Blaugrana · Barcelona

items.name: FC Bayern Munich
items.reasoning: Six European Cups and record double-digit Bundesliga dominance, winning 11 straight German titles from 2013 to 2023. The 2020 side won all six competitions it entered.
items.subtitle: Die Roten · Munich

items.name: Liverpool F.C.
items.reasoning: Six European Cups, second only to Real and Milan, plus a 20th English league title in 2025 to match Manchester United's record. Anfield's European nights are legendary.
items.subtitle: The Reds · Liverpool

items.name: Manchester City F.C.
items.reasoning: Won the treble in 2023 including a first Champions League, capping the most dominant Premier League run of the modern era with six titles in seven seasons.
items.subtitle: The Citizens · Manchester

items.name: AC Milan
items.reasoning: Seven European Cups, second-most of any club, built on the great Sacchi and Ancelotti sides. A giant even through recent lean years.
items.subtitle: Rossoneri · Milan

items.name: Manchester United F.C.
items.reasoning: 20 English league titles — a record now matched by Liverpool — and three European Cups, including the 1999 treble. Global fanbase remains vast despite a trophy drought since Ferguson left.
items.subtitle: Red Devils · Manchester

items.name: Juventus FC
items.reasoning: Record Serie A champions — the club counts 36 titles, 34 recognised after the Calciopoli scandal — with nine straight Scudetti from 2012 to 2020, but a famous European choker who lost seven of their nine Champions League finals.
items.subtitle: La Vecchia Signora · Turin

items.name: Inter Milan
items.reasoning: Three European Cups and the only Italian club to win the treble, in 2010 under Mourinho. Reached two Champions League finals in the 2020s.
items.subtitle: Nerazzurri · Milan

items.name: Arsenal F.C.
items.reasoning: The 2003-04 'Invincibles' went a full Premier League season unbeaten, a feat matched by no other modern English side. Thirteen league titles overall.
items.subtitle: The Gunners · London

items.name: Borussia Dortmund
items.reasoning: 1997 European champions and Germany's biggest challenger to Bayern, with the Yellow Wall standing terrace among the largest in world football.
items.subtitle: Die Borussen · Dortmund

items.name: Chelsea F.C.
items.reasoning: Two Champions League titles since the 2003 Abramovich takeover, but the 2022 ownership change kicked off chaotic, big-spending squad churn.
items.subtitle: The Blues · London

items.name: Paris Saint-Germain F.C.
items.reasoning: Qatari money bought French dominance and finally a first Champions League in 2025, but a decade of stacking galacticos had delivered mostly domestic trophies.
items.subtitle: Les Parisiens · Paris

items.name: AFC Ajax
items.reasoning: Four European Cups and the cradle of Total Football, though its modern role is selling academy gems like De Ligt and De Jong to richer clubs.
items.subtitle: De Godenzonen · Amsterdam

items.name: Atlético Madrid
items.reasoning: Under Simeone they won LaLiga twice and reached two Champions League finals in the 2010s, both lost to neighbours Real Madrid.
items.subtitle: Los Colchoneros · Madrid

items.name: S.S.C. Napoli
items.reasoning: Maradona's team won two Scudetti in the late 1980s, then waited 33 years for a third in 2023 and added a fourth in 2025. Passionate, and long a feast-or-famine club.
items.subtitle: Partenopei · Naples

items.name: Tottenham Hotspur F.C.
items.reasoning: Reached the 2019 Champions League final but went 17 years without a major trophy until the 2025 Europa League. The 'Spursy' collapse is a genuine meme.
items.subtitle: The Lilywhites · London

items.name: Newcastle United F.C.
items.reasoning: A huge, fanatical club that had not won a major domestic trophy since 1955, until a 2021 Saudi PIF takeover ended that drought with the 2025 League Cup.
items.subtitle: The Magpies · Newcastle

items.name: Bury F.C.
items.reasoning: A 134-year-old club expelled from the English Football League in 2019 after financial collapse, the first EFL club expelled in 27 years. A cautionary tale of mismanagement.
items.subtitle: The Shakers · Bury

title: Countries, Ranked
tagline: 195 nations. One brutally honest scoreboard.
basis: Ranked on quality of life, safety, culture, food, and how much the world actually wants to visit or live there — with points docked for corruption, conflict, and cruelty.

items.name: United States
items.reasoning: The world's largest economy and its dominant cultural exporter, from Hollywood to Silicon Valley. Weighed down by the highest healthcare costs among rich nations and uniquely high gun violence.
items.subtitle: Superpower with an asterisk

items.name: Japan
items.reasoning: One of the world's safest large nations with a life expectancy near 84 — among the highest on Earth — plus Tokyo has for more than 15 years held more Michelin stars than any other city. Trains that apologize for being 20 seconds late are the cherry on top.
items.subtitle: Order, sushi, and bullet trains

items.name: Switzerland
items.reasoning: Consistently ranks at or near the top of the UN Human Development Index and quality-of-life surveys, with one of the highest GDPs per capita globally. Centuries of neutrality, clean streets, and Alps you can ski before lunch.
items.subtitle: The world's cheat code

items.name: Italy
items.reasoning: Holds the most UNESCO World Heritage Sites of any country, and its cuisine and Renaissance art are global reference points. Notorious bureaucracy and a sluggish economy keep it just out of the top tier.
items.subtitle: Where civilization eats best

items.name: New Zealand
items.reasoning: A perennial leader on Transparency International's least-corrupt list with jaw-dropping fjords, glaciers, and geysers. Only knock: it's a very long flight from literally everywhere.
items.subtitle: Middle-earth, minus the orcs

items.name: Germany
items.reasoning: Europe's largest economy and an engineering powerhouse from Bosch to BMW, backed by strong worker protections and free universities. Efficient, stable, occasionally allergic to fun.
items.subtitle: Europe's engine room

items.name: Canada
items.reasoning: Scores among the world's highest for quality of life and welcomes hundreds of thousands of immigrants a year with famously low friction. The winters, however, are a war crime.
items.subtitle: Sorry, but great

items.name: Australia
items.reasoning: Sydney and Melbourne routinely rank among the world's most livable cities, with high wages and endless coastline. Everything in the outback is trying to kill you, and it's far from everywhere.
items.subtitle: Paradise that bites back

items.name: France
items.reasoning: The most-visited country on Earth, drawing roughly 100 million tourists a year for its food, wine, and art. Docked for legendary strikes and bureaucracy that can grind daily life to a halt.
items.subtitle: Wine, cheese, and shrugging

items.name: Spain
items.reasoning: Holds one of the world's largest collections of UNESCO World Heritage Sites, a Mediterranean climate, and one of the highest life expectancies in the world. Chronic high unemployment is the persistent cloud over the siesta.
items.subtitle: Sun, tapas, and long lunches

items.name: South Korea
items.reasoning: A tech and cultural juggernaut exporting Samsung, K-pop, and Oscar-winning film worldwide within a generation. Held back by a punishing work culture and the lowest birth rate on the planet.
items.subtitle: K-everything, zero sleep

items.name: Portugal
items.reasoning: Ranks among the world's most peaceful countries with mild weather, great food, and a low cost of living that lured a wave of expats. Wages stay stubbornly below the Western European average.
items.subtitle: Europe's best-kept secret

items.name: Mexico
items.reasoning: Its cuisine is inscribed on UNESCO's cultural heritage list, backed by ancient ruins and vibrant cities. Cartel-driven violence in parts of the country is the serious drag on the score.
items.subtitle: The food alone earns a medal

items.name: Brazil
items.reasoning: Home to the Amazon, Rio's beaches, five World Cup titles, and Carnival energy nobody can match. High crime rates and deep inequality keep it mid-table.
items.subtitle: Beauty and chaos in equal parts

items.name: Thailand
items.reasoning: A tourism magnet famous for its cuisine, islands, and hospitality at a bargain price. Recurring military coups and political turmoil make governance a coin-flip.
items.subtitle: Beaches, temples, and street food

items.name: India
items.reasoning: The world's most populous country and one of its fastest-growing economies, with staggering cultural and culinary depth. Severe air pollution and stark inequality hold it back.
items.subtitle: A billion stories at once

items.name: Russia
items.reasoning: The largest country on Earth with a towering literary and musical legacy from Tolstoy to Tchaikovsky. Its authoritarian government and 2022 invasion of Ukraine triggered sweeping international sanctions.
items.subtitle: Great books, grim politics

items.name: Somalia
items.reasoning: Decades of civil war and state collapse left it near the bottom of global stability and corruption indexes, with the extremist group Al-Shabaab still active. Piracy off its coast became a global symbol of the failed-state era.
items.subtitle: A cautionary tale of collapse

items.name: North Korea
items.reasoning: A totalitarian dynasty that runs political prison camps and cut its people off from the outside world, having presided over a 1990s famine that killed hundreds of thousands. There is no freedom of movement, press, or speech to speak of.
items.subtitle: The world's largest prison

title: Snakes, Ranked
tagline: Legends, pets, and one bird-murdering menace.
basis: Ranked on evolutionary impressiveness, iconic status, and cultural footprint — with F reserved for the genuinely destructive.

items.name: King cobra
items.reasoning: The world's longest venomous snake at up to 18 feet, it preys mainly on other snakes and can raise a third of its body off the ground to look a person in the eye.
items.subtitle: The snake that eats snakes

items.name: Black mamba
items.reasoning: The fastest land snake on Earth at up to about 12 mph in short bursts, its highly potent neurotoxic venom was almost always fatal before antivenom existed.
items.subtitle: Africa's fastest fear

items.name: Reticulated python
items.reasoning: The longest snake species in the world, with reliably measured individuals exceeding 20 feet, it kills by constriction rather than venom.
items.subtitle: Longest snake alive

items.name: Green anaconda
items.reasoning: The heaviest snake on the planet, it can exceed 200 pounds and ambushes prey from South American swamps and rivers.
items.subtitle: Heaviest of them all

items.name: Inland taipan
items.reasoning: Considered the most venomous land snake by LD50, a single bite carries enough toxin to theoretically kill scores of people, yet it is shy and rarely encountered.
items.subtitle: Most venomous, drop for drop

items.name: Gaboon viper
items.reasoning: It has the longest fangs of any venomous snake at up to 2 inches and delivers one of the largest venom yields, while its leaf-pattern camouflage is nearly perfect.
items.subtitle: Two-inch fangs

items.name: Ball python
items.reasoning: The most popular pet snake globally, prized for its docile temperament and huge range of bred color morphs, it curls into a ball when stressed.
items.subtitle: The world's pet snake

items.name: Corn snake
items.reasoning: A calm, hardy North American rat snake that is the go-to first pet for keepers, harmless to humans and a natural rodent controller.
items.subtitle: The perfect beginner

items.name: Boa constrictor
items.reasoning: An iconic New World constrictor that gives birth to live young and squeezes prey until circulation stops, ranging widely across Central and South America.
items.subtitle: The name is the method

items.name: Eastern diamondback rattlesnake
items.reasoning: The largest rattlesnake and heaviest venomous snake in the Americas, its warning rattle and hemotoxic bite make it the icon of the US Southeast.
items.subtitle: America's biggest rattler

items.name: Milk snake
items.reasoning: A harmless colubrid whose red, black, and yellow banding mimics the venomous coral snake, spawning the old 'red touches black' rhyme.
items.subtitle: The coral snake cosplayer

items.name: Common garter snake
items.reasoning: With the greatest distribution of any snake in North America, this small, harmless species is the one most people meet first in gardens and fields.
items.subtitle: Everyone's backyard snake

items.name: Western hognose snake
items.reasoning: When threatened it flattens its neck, hisses, then flops over and plays dead with its tongue out, using an upturned snout to dig for toads.
items.subtitle: The drama-queen bluffer

items.name: Sidewinder
items.reasoning: This small rattlesnake moves by throwing loops sideways across loose sand, leaving distinctive J-shaped tracks and minimizing contact with hot ground.
items.subtitle: The desert's J-walker

items.name: Boomslang
items.reasoning: A tree-dwelling African snake whose potent hemotoxin famously killed herpetologist Karl Schmidt in 1957 after he assumed the rear-fanged bite was harmless.
items.subtitle: The rear-fanged killer

items.name: Burmese python
items.reasoning: Escaped and released pets established a breeding population in Florida's Everglades, where they have decimated native mammal numbers and triggered ongoing removal programs.
items.subtitle: Everglades invader

items.name: Brown tree snake
items.reasoning: Accidentally introduced to Guam after WWII, this invasive snake drove most of the island's native forest birds to extinction and causes recurring power outages.
items.subtitle: It ate an island's birds

title: Sea Creatures, Ranked
tagline: From galaxy-brained cephalopods to reef-wrecking menaces.
basis: Ranked on intelligence, ecological impact, charisma, and sheer biological wow-factor — not just how cute they'd look on a poster.

items.name: Giant Pacific octopus
items.reasoning: The largest octopus species, it can solve mazes, open jars, and use nine brains (one central, one per arm) to problem-solve. Camouflage masters that change color and texture in under a second.
items.subtitle: The eight-armed genius

items.name: Orca
items.reasoning: The ocean's top predator, orcas hunt in coordinated pods using culture-specific dialects and techniques, even preying on great white sharks for their livers. No orca has ever killed a human in the wild.
items.subtitle: Apex predator, hunts in packs

items.name: Great white shark
items.reasoning: Can detect a single drop of blood in millions of drops of water and sense the electrical fields of prey via the ampullae of Lorenzini. Breaches fully out of the water at up to 25 mph when hunting seals.
items.subtitle: 450 million years of shark evolution

items.name: Bottlenose dolphin
items.reasoning: One of the few animals to pass the mirror self-recognition test, dolphins use signature whistles as names and hunt cooperatively. Their brains rival primates in relative size.
items.subtitle: Recognizes itself in a mirror

items.name: Sea otter
items.reasoning: Uses rocks as tools to crack open shellfish and holds hands while sleeping so it won't drift apart. As a keystone species, it protects kelp forests by eating the sea urchins that would devour them.
items.subtitle: Uses tools, holds hands

items.name: Giant manta ray
items.reasoning: With wingspans up to 7 meters, it has the largest brain-to-body ratio of any fish and may recognize itself in mirrors. Completely harmless, it filter-feeds on plankton while gliding gracefully.
items.subtitle: Winged glider of the open sea

items.name: Humpback whale
items.reasoning: Males sing complex, evolving songs that spread across ocean populations like hit music. Famous for spectacular breaches and bubble-net feeding, a cooperative hunting technique.
items.subtitle: Composer of the deep

items.name: Leafy seadragon
items.reasoning: A relative of the seahorse whose leaf-shaped appendages make it nearly invisible among kelp. It has no teeth or stomach and males carry the fertilized eggs on their tails.
items.subtitle: Living seaweed cosplay

items.name: Clownfish
items.reasoning: Lives symbiotically inside stinging sea anemones thanks to a protective mucus coating. Every clownfish is born male and the dominant one changes sex to female when needed.
items.subtitle: Immune to its own home

items.name: Green sea turtle
items.reasoning: Migrates thousands of miles and returns to the exact beach where it hatched to lay eggs, navigating by Earth's magnetic field. Can live over 70 years despite crushing odds against hatchlings.
items.subtitle: Ancient navigator

items.name: Chambered nautilus
items.reasoning: Virtually unchanged for 500 million years, it jets through water using a gas-filled spiral shell that is a natural example of logarithmic proportions. It has around 90 tentacles but no suckers.
items.subtitle: A living fossil

items.name: Mandarinfish
items.reasoning: One of only a few animals with true blue pigment cells rather than reflected light. Gorgeous but small and secretive, it coats itself in a foul-smelling, toxic slime instead of scales.
items.subtitle: Psychedelic but tiny

items.name: Moon jellyfish
items.reasoning: Has no brain, heart, or blood and drifts wherever currents take it, powered by a simple nerve net. Harmless to humans but a reminder that not every ocean icon is a mastermind.
items.subtitle: 95% water, 0% brain

items.name: American lobster
items.reasoning: Can live over 100 years and keeps growing throughout life, showing little of the typical decline of aging. Its blood is blue due to copper-based hemocyanin.
items.subtitle: Delicious, biologically immortal-ish

items.name: Horseshoe crab
items.reasoning: Survived four mass extinctions largely unchanged for 450 million years, and its copper-blue blood is harvested to test vaccines for bacterial contamination. Despite the name, it's more closely related to spiders than crabs.
items.subtitle: Blue blood, older than dinosaurs

items.name: Blobfish
items.reasoning: Voted the world's ugliest animal, but its gelatinous droop only appears after decompression from the deep sea; at home under pressure it looks like an ordinary fish. A victim of bad PR.
items.subtitle: Ugliest fish, but it's a lie

items.name: Hagfish
items.reasoning: Produces gallons of choking, fibrous slime in seconds to escape predators and ties itself in knots to clean off. It has no jaws and feeds by burrowing into carcasses.
items.subtitle: The slime cannon

items.name: Sea lamprey
items.reasoning: A jawless parasite with a suction disc of concentric teeth that latches onto fish and rasps out their blood and flesh. As an invasive species it devastated the Great Lakes fisheries.
items.subtitle: Nightmare mouth, invasive menace

items.name: Crown-of-thorns starfish
items.reasoning: A venomous starfish that eats living coral, its population outbreaks are a leading cause of coral loss on the Great Barrier Reef. A single one can consume up to 10 square meters of reef per year.
items.subtitle: Reef killer

items.name: Box jellyfish
items.reasoning: Among the most venomous animals on Earth, its sting can stop a human heart within minutes. It has 24 eyes and actively swims rather than drifting, making it deceptively hard to avoid.
items.subtitle: The most venomous sea creature

title: Motorcycles, Ranked
tagline: Two wheels, zero mercy.
basis: Ranked on engineering significance, cultural impact, and how much they moved the game forward — not just top speed.

items.name: Honda Super Cub
items.reasoning: The best-selling motor vehicle in history, with over 100 million produced since 1958 across more than 160 countries. It put the entire planet on two wheels and is still in production today.
items.subtitle: The people's bike

items.name: Honda CB750
items.reasoning: The 1969 CB750 introduced an inline-four, front disc brake, and electric start at a mass-market price, coining the term 'superbike' and forcing the entire industry to catch up.
items.subtitle: The first superbike

items.name: Ducati 916
items.reasoning: Massimo Tamburini's 1994 design is displayed in museums as an industrial-design landmark, and it won multiple World Superbike titles. It remains a benchmark for how a motorcycle should look.
items.subtitle: Rolling sculpture

items.name: Suzuki Hayabusa
items.reasoning: On its 1999 launch it was the fastest production motorcycle, hitting roughly 312 km/h and effectively triggering the unofficial 300 km/h gentleman's agreement among manufacturers.
items.subtitle: The 300 km/h missile

items.name: Yamaha YZF-R1
items.reasoning: The 1998 R1 stacked its gearbox to shorten the engine and sharpen handling, redefining the 1000cc sportbike; the 2009 crossplane-crank version brought MotoGP firing-order tech to the street.
items.subtitle: Litre-class icon

items.name: BMW R1200GS
items.reasoning: The boxer-twin GS has been one of BMW's best-selling models for years and is widely credited with defining the adventure-touring category; the R1200GS Adventure starred in Ewan McGregor and Charley Boorman's Long Way Down.
items.subtitle: Adventure royalty

items.name: Kawasaki Ninja H2
items.reasoning: The only mass-produced supercharged motorcycle, with the track-only H2R making around 300 horsepower. It is a genuine engineering flex from Kawasaki's aerospace division.
items.subtitle: Supercharged madness

items.name: Triumph Bonneville
items.reasoning: Named after Triumph's speed-record runs at the Bonneville Salt Flats, it became the definitive 1960s parallel-twin and one of the most enduring motorcycle names ever, revived successfully in 2001.
items.subtitle: The British classic

items.name: Honda Gold Wing
items.reasoning: The gold standard of luxury touring since 1975. The original GL1000 was a flat-four; today's Gold Wing carries a smooth flat-six (introduced with the 1988 GL1500), plus reverse gear and an airbag option. Unbeatable for crossing continents, if not for carving canyons.
items.subtitle: The two-wheeled sofa

items.name: Harley-Davidson
items.reasoning: Founded in 1903, Harley is the most culturally iconic motorcycle brand on earth and even tried to trademark its V-twin exhaust note. Heritage carries it; the engineering rarely leads.
items.subtitle: The sound of America

items.name: Kawasaki Z1
items.reasoning: The 1972 Z1 answered the CB750 with a 903cc DOHC four and was the fastest, most powerful Japanese production bike of its day, cementing Kawasaki's muscle-bike reputation.
items.subtitle: The 903cc animal

items.name: Indian Scout
items.reasoning: Indian was the oldest American motorcycle brand, and the Scout of the 1920s built its reputation with the slogan 'You can't wear out an Indian Scout.' A worthy rival to Harley's heritage.
items.subtitle: America's other legend

items.name: Vespa
items.reasoning: Piaggio's 1946 scooter put post-war Italy on wheels and became a global style icon featured in films like Roman Holiday. Not fast, but few machines are more beloved.
items.subtitle: La dolce vita

items.name: Yamaha YZF-R6
items.reasoning: A revered 600cc supersport that revs past 16,000 rpm and dominated club racing, but tightening emissions rules ended street sales in 2020, leaving it a track-only relic.
items.subtitle: The track-day scalpel

items.name: Royal Enfield Bullet
items.reasoning: Holds the record as the longest continuously produced motorcycle model, running since 1932. Charming and cheap, but its old thumper design was long outpaced by rivals.
items.subtitle: The world's oldest model

items.name: Harley-Davidson V-Rod
items.reasoning: The 2001 V-Rod used a liquid-cooled 'Revolution' engine developed with Porsche, a bold move that alienated traditionalists and was discontinued in 2017. Ambitious but a hard sell.
items.subtitle: The Porsche experiment

items.name: Can-Am Spyder
items.reasoning: A three-wheeled 'reverse trike' with two wheels up front, it sidesteps the balance and lean that define motorcycling. Stable and easy, but purists refuse to count it.
items.subtitle: Is it even a motorcycle?

items.name: Suzuki RE5
items.reasoning: Suzuki's mid-1970s Wankel rotary-engined bike was heavy, complex, thirsty, and ran hot, becoming a costly commercial failure that nearly hurt the company. A famous cautionary tale.
items.subtitle: The rotary flop

items.name: Harley-Davidson Street
items.reasoning: Harley's cheap entry-level Street 500/750, partly built in India, was widely panned for cheap finish and weak performance and was quietly discontinued by 2021 after failing to win new riders.
items.subtitle: The budget misfire

title: Flowers, Ranked
tagline: Every bloom judged on beauty, scent, and staying power.
basis: Flowers ranked by a mix of aesthetic impact, fragrance, cultural weight, and how much joy versus revulsion they actually deliver.

items.name: Rose
items.reasoning: Cultivated for over 5,000 years and the world's best-selling cut flower, the rose pairs deep fragrance with symbolic weight in love, art, and heraldry across cultures.
items.subtitle: The undisputed icon

items.name: Tulip
items.reasoning: So coveted that 1630s 'Tulip mania' became history's first speculative market crash, with single bulbs trading for the price of a house in the Dutch Republic.
items.subtitle: Caused an actual bubble

items.name: Peony
items.reasoning: Prized in China for over 1,500 years as the 'king of flowers,' peonies produce enormous, densely petaled blooms and can live and flower for a century.
items.subtitle: Lush, layered, beloved

items.name: Orchid
items.reasoning: With roughly 28,000 accepted species, Orchidaceae is one of the largest plant families on Earth, spanning nearly every habitat and including the vanilla plant.
items.subtitle: Nature's overachiever

items.name: Sunflower
items.reasoning: Young sunflowers track the sun across the sky (heliotropism), and their seed heads pack florets in a mathematically efficient Fibonacci spiral.
items.subtitle: Follows the sun

items.name: Sacred Lotus
items.reasoning: Its self-cleaning, water-repellent leaves inspired the term 'lotus effect,' and seeds have germinated after more than 1,000 years of dormancy.
items.subtitle: Rises clean from mud

items.name: Lavender
items.reasoning: Long used in perfumery and aromatherapy, lavender's fragrant oil is one of the most studied for relaxation, and the plant thrives in poor, dry soils.
items.subtitle: Calm in plant form

items.name: Cherry Blossom
items.reasoning: Japan's sakura bloom for only about one to two weeks, and their fleeting peak draws millions to hanami viewing each spring.
items.subtitle: Beauty on a timer

items.name: Lily
items.reasoning: True lilies are striking, fragrant, and central to funerary and religious imagery, though their pollen is famously toxic to cats.
items.subtitle: Elegant but demanding

items.name: Daffodil
items.reasoning: Among the earliest bloomers, daffodils are the symbol of many cancer charities, but their bulbs are poisonous and occasionally mistaken for onions.
items.subtitle: First sign of spring

items.name: Hydrangea
items.reasoning: Bloom color shifts from blue to pink based on soil pH and aluminum availability, making the same plant a living litmus test.
items.subtitle: Color depends on dirt

items.name: Dahlia
items.reasoning: A single genus bred into more than 50,000 registered cultivars, ranging from tiny pompons to dinner-plate blooms; it's the national flower of Mexico.
items.subtitle: Endless variety

items.name: Marigold
items.reasoning: Easy to grow and used to repel garden pests, marigolds are central to Mexico's Day of the Dead but carry a pungent, divisive scent.
items.subtitle: Hardworking, not glamorous

items.name: Chrysanthemum
items.reasoning: A symbol of imperial Japan and longevity in Asia, yet associated with funerals and death across much of Europe.
items.subtitle: Depends where you are

items.name: Carnation
items.reasoning: Cheap, long-lasting, and a florist staple, but its ubiquity in supermarket bouquets has left it feeling generic.
items.subtitle: The reliable filler

items.name: Iris
items.reasoning: Named for the Greek goddess of the rainbow and the model for the fleur-de-lis, though individual blooms wilt within a few days.
items.subtitle: Pretty but fleeting

items.name: Baby's Breath
items.reasoning: Valued only as cheap filler around real flowers, Gypsophila is considered an invasive weed in parts of North America.
items.subtitle: Bouquet background noise

items.name: Poppy
items.reasoning: Delicate red petals of the corn poppy became a global remembrance symbol, but the flowers shatter almost instantly once cut.
items.subtitle: Gorgeous, then gone

items.name: Corpse Flower
items.reasoning: The titan arum emits the stench of rotting flesh to attract carrion pollinators and blooms unpredictably, sometimes only once a decade.
items.subtitle: Smells like death

items.name: Rafflesia
items.reasoning: A rootless, leafless parasite producing the world's largest single flower, it mimics the smell of decaying meat and cannot survive on its own.
items.subtitle: Parasite that reeks

title: Historical Empires, Ranked
tagline: Every empire wanted to last forever. We graded on a curve.
basis: Ranked on peak territory and population, staying power, cultural and institutional legacy, and how they're remembered — with the genuinely monstrous sent to the bottom.

items.name: Roman Empire
items.reasoning: Dominated the Mediterranean for centuries, peaking around 5 million km² under Trajan, and exported roads, law, Latin and republican-imperial governance that still shape the West. Its very name became the word for 'empire.'
items.subtitle: The template every empire copied

items.name: Mongol Empire
items.reasoning: At its height it stretched roughly 24 million km² from Korea to Eastern Europe, the largest connected land empire in history. It reopened Silk Road trade across Eurasia in a single generation of conquest.
items.subtitle: Largest contiguous land empire ever

items.name: British Empire
items.reasoning: By 1920 it covered about 35.5 million km² and ruled nearly a quarter of the world's population, the largest empire in history by area. English, common law and global trade networks are its lasting fingerprints.
items.subtitle: The sun literally never set

items.name: Achaemenid Empire
items.reasoning: Cyrus and Darius built the first empire to span three continents, ruling an estimated 44% of the world's population around 480 BC — the highest share any empire has ever held. It pioneered satrapies, royal roads and imperial tolerance.
items.subtitle: The first true world empire

items.name: Han Dynasty
items.reasoning: Contemporary and comparable in scale to Rome, it standardized Chinese bureaucracy, gave the majority ethnic group its name, and oversaw the invention of paper and the opening of the Silk Road. Confucian statecraft here set the mold for two millennia.
items.subtitle: China's four-century golden age

items.name: Ottoman Empire
items.reasoning: Lasting from 1299 to 1922, it took Constantinople in 1453 and controlled southeastern Europe, the Middle East and North Africa at its peak. Few empires matched its longevity or its role as a bridge between Europe and Asia.
items.subtitle: Six centuries astride three continents

items.name: Byzantine Empire
items.reasoning: The eastern Roman Empire survived nearly a thousand years past the West, falling only in 1453, and preserved Greco-Roman learning and Justinian's codified law through the Middle Ages. Its diplomacy and Orthodox Christianity reshaped Eastern Europe.
items.subtitle: Rome that refused to die

items.name: Abbasid Caliphate
items.reasoning: Presiding over the Islamic Golden Age, it made Baghdad a global center of science, medicine and translation that preserved and advanced Greek and Indian knowledge. Its scholars propagated the decimal system and algebra to the wider world.
items.subtitle: Baghdad's House of Wisdom

items.name: Macedonia (Alexander's Empire)
items.reasoning: Alexander the Great marched from Greece to the Indus and was never defeated in a pitched battle, spreading Hellenistic culture across the known world. But he built no succession, and the empire fractured among his generals within years of his death in 323 BC.
items.subtitle: Conquered a world, then shattered

items.name: Spanish Empire
items.reasoning: The 16th-century conquest of the Americas gave Spain the first empire spanning the globe, funded by vast New World silver and spreading Spanish across two continents. That wealth also fueled colonial brutality and the collapse of native civilizations.
items.subtitle: The first truly global empire

items.name: Mughal Empire
items.reasoning: At its height it ruled most of the Indian subcontinent and commanded roughly a quarter of the world's economy. It left extraordinary architecture, from the Taj Mahal to the Red Fort, and a lasting Indo-Persian cultural synthesis.
items.subtitle: The empire that built the Taj Mahal

items.name: Russian Empire
items.reasoning: By area it grew to about 22.8 million km², the third-largest empire ever, spanning Eastern Europe to the Pacific. But it rested on serfdom until 1861 and buckled into revolution in 1917.
items.subtitle: Third-biggest empire in history

items.name: Inca Empire
items.reasoning: The largest empire in pre-Columbian America, it stitched together the Andes with tens of thousands of kilometers of road and terraced agriculture without the wheel or writing. It was toppled by fewer than 200 Spaniards exploiting a civil war in the 1530s.
items.subtitle: Master engineers of the Andes

items.name: Aztec Empire
items.reasoning: Its capital Tenochtitlan was one of the largest cities in the world, built on an engineered lake with causeways and floating gardens. The empire fell fast to Cortés and his indigenous allies in 1521, aided by smallpox.
items.subtitle: Tenochtitlan, the island metropolis

items.name: Gupta Empire
items.reasoning: The Gupta era saw major advances in mathematics and astronomy, including the early use of zero and the decimal place-value system. Its art, Sanskrit literature and science defined a classical high point for the subcontinent.
items.subtitle: India's classical golden age

items.name: Carthaginian Empire
items.reasoning: A Phoenician maritime power that dominated the western Mediterranean and produced Hannibal, who nearly broke Rome by marching elephants over the Alps. It lost the Punic Wars so completely that Rome razed the city in 146 BC.
items.subtitle: Rome's greatest rival, erased

items.name: Empire of Japan
items.reasoning: It industrialized in a generation and briefly controlled much of East Asia and the Pacific, but its 1930s–40s expansion brought atrocities like Nanjing and the exploitation of occupied peoples. It ended in unconditional surrender in 1945.
items.subtitle: Modernized fast, expanded brutally

items.name: Nazi Germany
items.reasoning: A regime built on genocide, it murdered six million Jews in the Holocaust and plunged the world into its deadliest war. Its self-proclaimed thousand-year empire collapsed in ruins after barely twelve years.
items.subtitle: The 'Thousand-Year Reich' lasted 12

title: Superhero TV Shows, Ranked
tagline: Capes, cowls, and the shows that actually stuck the landing.
basis: Ranked on critical acclaim, cultural impact, and rewatchability across live-action and animated superhero television.

items.name: Watchmen
items.reasoning: Damon Lindelof's sequel series won Outstanding Limited Series at the 2020 Emmys and took 11 awards total, blending superhero myth with a searing story about American racism.
items.subtitle: HBO, 2019

items.name: Daredevil
items.reasoning: Netflix's Marvel debut was praised for its grounded tone and single-take hallway fight scenes, and Charlie Cox's Matt Murdock proved popular enough to be revived in the MCU.
items.subtitle: Netflix, 2015

items.name: The Boys
items.reasoning: Adapted from Garth Ennis's comic, this ultraviolent satire of corporate superheroes became one of Amazon's biggest hits and a multiple Emmy-nominated cultural phenomenon.
items.subtitle: Prime Video, 2019

items.name: Invincible
items.reasoning: Robert Kirkman's adult animated series stunned audiences with its brutal pilot twist and earned wide critical acclaim for its voice cast and mature storytelling.
items.subtitle: Prime Video, 2021

items.name: Batman: The Animated Series
items.reasoning: An Emmy-winning landmark of animation, it defined the modern Batman aesthetic and introduced Harley Quinn, who became a permanent part of DC canon.
items.subtitle: Fox Kids, 1992

items.name: WandaVision
items.reasoning: Marvel's first Disney+ series framed grief as a decade-hopping sitcom pastiche and earned 23 Emmy nominations for its inventive structure.
items.subtitle: Disney+, 2021

items.name: Peacemaker
items.reasoning: James Gunn's spin-off gave John Cena a surprisingly heartfelt showcase, and its full-cast opening dance number became an instant internet favorite.
items.subtitle: HBO Max, 2022

items.name: Loki
items.reasoning: Tom Hiddleston's time-bending series was among the best-reviewed MCU shows and set up the multiverse saga through its Time Variance Authority premise.
items.subtitle: Disney+, 2021

items.name: Arrow
items.reasoning: Stephen Amell's grounded take on Green Arrow launched the sprawling eight-year Arrowverse of interconnected CW superhero shows.
items.subtitle: The CW, 2012

items.name: The Flash
items.reasoning: Grant Gustin's speedster became the Arrowverse's flagship, running nine seasons and anchoring the network's annual crossover events.
items.subtitle: The CW, 2014

items.name: Jessica Jones
items.reasoning: Krysten Ritter's noir-tinged first season won acclaim for tackling trauma, with David Tennant's Kilgrave hailed as one of Marvel TV's best villains.
items.subtitle: Netflix, 2015

items.name: Smallville
items.reasoning: The Superman origin drama ran a remarkable ten seasons, charting Clark Kent's journey before the cape and cementing a devoted fanbase.
items.subtitle: The WB/CW, 2001

items.name: Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D.
items.reasoning: The first MCU-tied TV series lasted seven seasons, gradually winning fans with time-travel and space arcs after a slow start.
items.subtitle: ABC, 2013

items.name: Supergirl
items.reasoning: Melissa Benoist headlined six seasons as Kara Danvers, moving from CBS to The CW and expanding the Arrowverse with an optimistic, issue-driven tone.
items.subtitle: CBS/The CW, 2015

items.name: The Umbrella Academy
items.reasoning: Based on Gerard Way's comic, this dysfunctional-family superhero saga was a Netflix hit but drew criticism for a divisive final season.
items.subtitle: Netflix, 2019

items.name: Gotham
items.reasoning: A pre-Batman crime drama following young Jim Gordon, it ran five seasons on an increasingly campy origin-story premise for Batman's rogues.
items.subtitle: Fox, 2014

items.name: Titans
items.reasoning: The grittier Teen Titans adaptation launched DC's streaming service and ran four seasons to mixed reviews over its dark tone.
items.subtitle: DC Universe, 2018

items.name: Iron Fist
items.reasoning: Widely regarded as the weakest of Netflix's Marvel shows, its poorly received first season drew criticism for pacing and fight choreography before cancellation after two seasons.
items.subtitle: Netflix, 2017

items.name: Inhumans
items.reasoning: Panned by critics with a 27 Metacritic score, Marvel's IMAX-shot flop was cancelled after one eight-episode season and became a byword for superhero TV failure.
items.subtitle: ABC, 2017

title: Spy Movies, Ranked
tagline: Shaken, stirred, and sorted into tiers.
basis: Ranked on craft, tension, and lasting influence on the espionage genre — not just gadgets and gunfights.

items.name: Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy
items.reasoning: Tomas Alfredson's adaptation of John le Carré's novel earned three Academy Award nominations — including Best Actor for Gary Oldman as George Smiley — and won the BAFTA for Outstanding British Film, defining the slow-burn mole-hunt thriller.
items.subtitle: 2011 · le Carré, ice-cold

items.name: North by Northwest
items.reasoning: Alfred Hitchcock's wrong-man chase built the blueprint later Bond films followed, with the iconic crop-duster and Mount Rushmore set pieces. Selected for the U.S. National Film Registry.
items.subtitle: 1959 · Hitchcock's template

items.name: Casino Royale
items.reasoning: Daniel Craig's debut rebooted 007 as a raw, bleeding operative and holds a 94% on Rotten Tomatoes, widely credited with rescuing the franchise.
items.subtitle: 2006 · the reboot that worked

items.name: The Bourne Ultimatum
items.reasoning: Paul Greengrass's handheld chase film won three Academy Awards (editing, sound mixing, sound editing) and reshaped the modern action-spy aesthetic.
items.subtitle: 2007 · kinetic spycraft

items.name: Mission: Impossible – Fallout
items.reasoning: Christopher McQuarrie's entry is the best-reviewed of the franchise at 97% on Rotten Tomatoes, with Tom Cruise performing a real HALO jump and helicopter chase.
items.subtitle: 2018 · the stunt peak

items.name: The Spy Who Came in from the Cold
items.reasoning: Richard Burton stars in this bleak le Carré adaptation that deliberately rejected Bond's glamour; Burton earned an Oscar nomination for the role.

art.title: The Spy Who Came in from the Cold (film)

items.subtitle: 1965 · anti-glamour espionage

items.name: The Lives of Others
items.reasoning: Florian Henckel von Donnersmarck's East German surveillance drama won the 2007 Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
items.subtitle: 2006 · Stasi surveillance

items.name: Munich
items.reasoning: Steven Spielberg's dramatization of the post-Munich Olympics assassination squad received five Academy Award nominations including Best Picture.
items.subtitle: 2005 · vengeance and doubt

items.name: Argo
items.reasoning: Ben Affleck's dramatization of the CIA's Canadian Caper during the Iran hostage crisis won the Academy Award for Best Picture.
items.subtitle: 2012 · the fake movie caper

items.name: Skyfall
items.reasoning: Sam Mendes's entry became the first Bond film to gross over $1 billion and won two Academy Awards, for Original Song and Sound Editing.
items.subtitle: 2012 · Bond turns 50

items.name: Bridge of Spies
items.reasoning: Spielberg's Cold War prisoner-swap drama earned Mark Rylance the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor as Soviet spy Rudolf Abel.
items.subtitle: 2015 · Cold War exchange

items.name: Kingsman: The Secret Service
items.reasoning: Matthew Vaughn's ultra-violent, tongue-in-cheek riff on gentleman-spy tropes was a surprise hit, grossing over $400 million worldwide.
items.subtitle: 2015 · spy-genre remix

items.name: True Lies
items.reasoning: James Cameron's action-comedy paired Arnold Schwarzenegger with Jamie Lee Curtis, who won a Golden Globe, and was among the most expensive films of its era.
items.subtitle: 1994 · Cameron goes big

items.name: The Man from U.N.C.L.E.
items.reasoning: Guy Ritchie's slick Cold War caper, based on the 1960s TV series, pairs Henry Cavill and Armie Hammer with retro flair that earned a cult following.
items.subtitle: 2015 · stylish 60s throwback

items.name: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery
items.reasoning: Mike Myers's parody of 1960s spy cinema spawned a franchise, though its groovy gags land more as comedy than espionage.

art.title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery

items.subtitle: 1997 · the spoof

items.name: Salt
items.reasoning: Angelina Jolie plays a CIA officer accused of being a Russian sleeper agent; a competent action vehicle originally written for a male lead.
items.subtitle: 2010 · sleeper-agent chase

items.name: Red Sparrow
items.reasoning: Jennifer Lawrence stars as a Russian intelligence 'sparrow,' a lavish but coldly received thriller that drew mixed reviews around 47% on Rotten Tomatoes.
items.subtitle: 2018 · seduction-school spy

items.name: Jack Ryan: Shadow Recruit
items.reasoning: This fifth attempt to launch a Jack Ryan franchise with Chris Pine underperformed at the box office and received tepid reviews, ending the film series.
items.subtitle: 2014 · a reboot that stalled

items.name: xXx
items.reasoning: Vin Diesel's attempt to reinvent the spy as an extreme-sports rebel leans on stunts over espionage, a loud early-2000s time capsule.
items.subtitle: 2002 · extreme-sports 'spy'

items.name: The Tourist
items.reasoning: Despite Johnny Depp and Angelina Jolie, this Venice-set thriller was critically panned (around 20% on Rotten Tomatoes) and drew Razzie-adjacent Golden Globe mockery.
items.subtitle: 2010 · star wattage, no spark

title: Time Travel Movies, Ranked
tagline: Great Scott, we settled the timeline.
basis: Ranked on paradox craftsmanship, rewatchability, and cultural staying power — from flawless S-tier classics down to time-wasting F-tier misfires.

items.name: Back to the Future
items.reasoning: Selected for the U.S. National Film Registry and holds a 96% Rotten Tomatoes score; its DeLorean and 88 mph became the genre's defining icons.
items.subtitle: 1985 · Zemeckis

items.name: Terminator 2: Judgment Day
items.reasoning: Won four Academy Awards, including Best Visual Effects for its groundbreaking liquid-metal CGI, and was the most expensive film ever made at the time. A rare sequel widely rated above its original.
items.subtitle: 1991 · Cameron

items.name: Groundhog Day
items.reasoning: So influential its title became shorthand for a repeating time loop, and it was added to the National Film Registry in 2006. Bill Murray's performance anchors a near-perfect comedy.
items.subtitle: 1993 · Ramis

items.name: Primer
items.reasoning: Made for a reported $7,000 budget yet won the Grand Jury Prize at Sundance; its labyrinthine timelines are famous for demanding diagrams to follow.
items.subtitle: 2004 · Carruth

items.name: Edge of Tomorrow
items.reasoning: A 'live-die-repeat' loop premise executed with sharp editing and Emily Blunt; earned a 91% Rotten Tomatoes score and a devoted cult following.
items.subtitle: 2014 · Liman

items.name: Looper
items.reasoning: Rian Johnson's hitman-meets-his-future-self thriller grossed over $176 million and drew praise for taking its closed-loop logic seriously.
items.subtitle: 2012 · Johnson

items.name: 12 Monkeys
items.reasoning: Terry Gilliam's bleak fixed-timeline mystery earned Brad Pitt an Oscar nomination and a Golden Globe win; inspired by Chris Marker's La Jetée.
items.subtitle: 1995 · Gilliam

items.name: The Terminator
items.reasoning: Launched the franchise and Cameron's career on a $6.4 million budget; added to the National Film Registry in 2008 for its bootstrap-paradox plot.
items.subtitle: 1984 · Cameron

items.name: Predestination
items.reasoning: Adapts Robert Heinlein's '—All You Zombies—' into cinema's tightest causal-loop paradox, praised for Sarah Snook's dual-role performance.
items.subtitle: 2014 · Spierig Brothers

items.name: Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure
items.reasoning: A phone-booth time machine and history-project premise turned into an enduring cult comedy that spawned two sequels and an animated series.
items.subtitle: 1989 · Herek

items.name: About Time
items.reasoning: Richard Curtis's romantic dramedy uses generational time travel as a lens on savoring ordinary life; a modest box-office hit that aged into a comfort favorite.
items.subtitle: 2013 · Curtis

items.name: Star Trek IV: The Voyage Home
items.reasoning: The crew slingshots back to 1986 San Francisco; the lightest, highest-grossing of the original-cast films and a fan-poll favorite.

art.title: Star Trek IV: The Voyage Home

items.subtitle: 1986 · Nimoy

items.name: Donnie Darko
items.reasoning: A box-office flop on release that became a defining cult film through DVD, built on a cryptic tangent-universe time-loop mythology.
items.subtitle: 2001 · Kelly

items.name: Hot Tub Time Machine
items.reasoning: An unapologetically dumb 1980s-nostalgia comedy that turned a self-aware gimmick title into a modest hit and a sequel.
items.subtitle: 2010 · Pink

items.name: Timecop
items.reasoning: Jean-Claude Van Damme's highest-grossing lead vehicle; solid B-movie fun that spawned a short-lived TV series but never rose above genre pulp.
items.subtitle: 1994 · Hyams

items.name: Frequency
items.reasoning: A ham-radio link across 30 years lets father and son alter the past; a well-liked emotional thriller that later became a CW series.
items.subtitle: 2000 · Hoblit

items.name: The Butterfly Effect
items.reasoning: A commercial hit but critically panned at 34% on Rotten Tomatoes; its blackout-journal time-jumping grows increasingly lurid and silly.
items.subtitle: 2004 · Bress & Gruber

items.name: The Time Machine
items.reasoning: H.G. Wells's great-grandson directed this remake, yet it landed at 28% on Rotten Tomatoes for hollow effects and a wasted Morlock premise.
items.subtitle: 2002 · Simon Wells

items.name: Timeline
items.reasoning: A troubled adaptation of the Michael Crichton novel that flopped hard and sits at 13% on Rotten Tomatoes.
items.subtitle: 2003 · Donner

items.name: The Time Traveler's Wife
items.reasoning: Despite a bestselling source novel, critics savaged it at 38% on Rotten Tomatoes, and a later HBO series was cancelled after one season.
items.subtitle: 2009 · Schwentke

title: Anime Movies, Ranked
tagline: From hand-drawn masterpieces to cash-grab misfires.
basis: Ranked on animation craft, storytelling, cultural impact, and lasting influence on the medium.

items.name: Spirited Away
items.reasoning: Won the Academy Award for Best Animated Feature and was the first hand-drawn, non-English film to do so; long the highest-grossing film in Japanese history.
items.subtitle: Studio Ghibli, 2001

items.name: Akira
items.reasoning: Its 160,000-cel animation and pre-scored dialogue set a new technical benchmark and it helped open Western markets to adult anime.
items.subtitle: Katsuhiro Otomo, 1988

items.name: Grave of the Fireflies
items.reasoning: Takahata's WWII drama about two orphaned siblings is routinely cited by critics like Roger Ebert as one of the greatest war films ever made.
items.subtitle: Isao Takahata, 1988

items.name: Your Name
items.reasoning: Grossed over $380 million worldwide and became the highest-grossing anime film at the time, cementing Shinkai as a global draw.
items.subtitle: Makoto Shinkai, 2016

items.name: Princess Mononoke
items.reasoning: Miyazaki's epic ecological fable was Japan's highest-grossing film until Titanic and marked Ghibli's international breakthrough via Miramax.
items.subtitle: Studio Ghibli, 1997

items.name: Perfect Blue
items.reasoning: Kon's psychological thriller pioneered a reality-blurring editing style that later influenced Aronofsky's Requiem for a Dream and Black Swan.
items.subtitle: Satoshi Kon, 1997

items.name: Ghost in the Shell
items.reasoning: Its cyberpunk vision of networked consciousness directly inspired The Matrix, whose creators screened it as a reference for producers.
items.subtitle: Mamoru Oshii, 1995

items.name: A Silent Voice
items.reasoning: Naoko Yamada's drama about bullying and deafness earned wide acclaim and won Best Animation of the Year at the Japan Movie Critics Awards.
items.subtitle: Kyoto Animation, 2016

items.name: The Boy and the Heron
items.reasoning: Miyazaki's return from retirement won the Academy Award and Golden Globe for Best Animated Feature, making him, at 83, the oldest director to win the Oscar in the category.
items.subtitle: Miyazaki, 2023

items.name: Demon Slayer: Mugen Train
items.reasoning: Became the highest-grossing film ever in Japan and, on release, the highest-grossing anime film worldwide, though it functions as a sequel to the TV series.

art.title: Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba – The Movie: Mugen Train

items.subtitle: ufotable, 2020

items.name: Redline
items.reasoning: Took seven years and roughly 100,000 hand-drawn frames to complete, delivering some of the most kinetic 2D racing animation ever produced.
items.subtitle: Madhouse, 2009

items.name: Weathering with You
items.reasoning: Shinkai's follow-up to Your Name was Japan's official Oscar submission and grossed over $190 million worldwide.
items.subtitle: Makoto Shinkai, 2019

items.name: The Girl Who Leapt Through Time
items.reasoning: Hosoda's breakout won the Japan Academy Prize for Animation of the Year and launched him as a major post-Ghibli director.

art.title: The Girl Who Leapt Through Time (2006 film)

items.subtitle: Mamoru Hosoda, 2006

items.name: Ninja Scroll
items.reasoning: A landmark of adult action anime whose fluid, violent swordplay made it a staple of 1990s Western anime home-video culture.
items.subtitle: Yoshiaki Kawajiri, 1993

items.name: Jujutsu Kaisen 0
items.reasoning: A commercial hit grossing over $190 million, but it serves as a prequel that leans on familiarity with the franchise rather than standing alone.
items.subtitle: MAPPA, 2021

items.name: Dragon Ball Super: Broly
items.reasoning: Praised for its frenetic fight animation, but its plot is a fan-service retcon of a non-canon villain aimed squarely at existing fans.
items.subtitle: Toei, 2018

items.name: Pokemon: The First Movie
items.reasoning: A massive box-office earner driven by franchise mania, but its heavily rewritten English dub and thin plot drew critical panning.
items.subtitle: OLM, 1998

items.name: Fist of the North Star
items.reasoning: Notorious for compressing a sprawling manga into an incoherent film, better remembered for gore than coherent storytelling.

art.title: Fist of the North Star (1986 film)

items.subtitle: Toei, 1986

items.name: Mars of Destruction
items.reasoning: Widely ranked among the worst anime ever made, it carries one of the lowest scores on MyAnimeList for its broken animation and nonsensical plot.
items.subtitle: Idea Factory, 2005

title: Monster Movies, Ranked
tagline: Big teeth, bigger box office, zero chill.
basis: Ranked on creature design, craft, and lasting cultural impact — the films that defined what a movie monster can be, from rubber-suit kaiju to practical-effects nightmares.

items.name: Jaws
items.reasoning: Spielberg's malfunctioning mechanical shark forced him to hide the creature, inventing suspense that won three Oscars and created the modern summer blockbuster.
items.subtitle: 1975 · the shark that ate summer

items.name: The Thing
items.reasoning: Rob Bottin's shape-shifting practical effects remain a landmark of creature design, and the film is now widely regarded as one of the greatest horror films ever made.
items.subtitle: 1982 · trust no one

items.name: Godzilla
items.reasoning: Ishiro Honda's atomic allegory launched the longest-running film franchise in history and the entire kaiju genre.
items.subtitle: 1954 · the original kaiju

items.name: King Kong
items.reasoning: Willis O'Brien's stop-motion ape climbing the Empire State Building set the template for every giant-monster movie that followed.
items.subtitle: 1933 · eighth wonder of the world

items.name: Alien
items.reasoning: H. R. Giger's biomechanical xenomorph won the Academy Award for Visual Effects and remains cinema's most iconic creature design.
items.subtitle: 1979 · in space no one can hear you scream

items.name: Jurassic Park
items.reasoning: Its blend of animatronics and pioneering CGI won three Oscars and made resurrected dinosaurs feel genuinely alive.
items.subtitle: 1993 · dinosaurs, done right

items.name: The Host
items.reasoning: Bong Joon-ho's Han River creature feature was a South Korean box-office record-breaker praised for fusing monster thrills with sharp social satire.
items.subtitle: 2006 · Bong before Parasite

items.name: An American Werewolf in London
items.reasoning: Rick Baker's agonizing on-screen werewolf change won the first-ever Academy Award for Best Makeup.
items.subtitle: 1981 · the transformation

items.name: Tremors
items.reasoning: A tongue-in-cheek creature feature about subterranean Graboids that became a beloved cult classic spawning a long-running franchise.
items.subtitle: 1990 · underground and underrated

items.name: The Fly
items.reasoning: Cronenberg's body-horror remake won an Oscar for its grotesque makeup effects and is considered one of the best remakes ever made.
items.subtitle: 1986 · be afraid, be very afraid

items.name: Predator
items.reasoning: Stan Winston's dreadlocked, mandibled hunter became an enduring sci-fi icon anchoring a decades-long franchise.
items.subtitle: 1987 · get to the choppa

items.name: Gremlins
items.reasoning: Joe Dante's puppet-driven creature comedy was a massive hit whose content, alongside Indiana Jones, helped prompt the creation of the PG-13 rating.
items.subtitle: 1984 · don't feed them after midnight

items.name: Cloverfield
items.reasoning: A viral-marketed, handheld monster movie that reinvented the giant-creature attack from street level and became a surprise hit.
items.subtitle: 2008 · found-footage kaiju

items.name: Kong: Skull Island
items.reasoning: A visually striking, well-reviewed reboot that grossed over $560 million and expanded Legendary's MonsterVerse.
items.subtitle: 2017 · MonsterVerse Kong

items.name: Godzilla
items.reasoning: Gareth Edwards' atmospheric relaunch was a commercial success but frustrated some fans by keeping its monster largely off-screen.
items.subtitle: 2014 · the American reboot

items.name: The Meg
items.reasoning: A goofy prehistoric-shark blockbuster that critics found dull but which still grossed over $500 million worldwide.
items.subtitle: 2018 · Statham vs. shark

items.name: Rampage
items.reasoning: The Rock versus a mutated gorilla, wolf, and croc — a video-game adaptation that's dumb, loud fun and little else.
items.subtitle: 2018 · giant animals gone wild

items.name: Godzilla
items.reasoning: Roland Emmerich's much-maligned version so annoyed Toho that it later renamed the creature 'Zilla' to distance it from the real Godzilla.
items.subtitle: 1998 · the one fans disown

items.name: Birdemic: Shock and Terror
items.reasoning: An infamously inept eco-horror film whose static, poorly animated attacking birds make it a staple of 'worst movies ever' lists.
items.subtitle: 2010 · the worst birds ever

title: Drummers, Ranked
tagline: The kit is settled. Fight me.
basis: Ranked on technical mastery, feel, influence on how the instrument is played, and the size of the shadow they cast over everyone who came after.

items.name: John Bonham
items.reasoning: Bonham's thunderous groove and foot speed on tracks like 'Good Times Bad Times' and 'When the Levee Breaks' made him the most sampled and imitated rock drummer ever. He is routinely voted the greatest rock drummer in Rolling Stone and Modern Drummer polls.
items.subtitle: Led Zeppelin

items.name: Neil Peart
items.reasoning: Peart paired a monstrous 360-degree kit with airtight precision and wrote most of Rush's lyrics, redefining progressive rock drumming across songs like 'Tom Sawyer' and 'YYZ.' He was inducted into the Modern Drummer Hall of Fame in 1983 at age 30, its youngest inductee ever.
items.subtitle: Rush

items.name: Buddy Rich
items.reasoning: Rich was a self-taught prodigy with blinding single-stroke speed and control widely called the greatest technician the drums have ever seen. He led his own big band for decades and became a fixture of Johnny Carson's Tonight Show.
items.subtitle: Big-band virtuoso

items.name: Keith Moon
items.reasoning: Moon turned drumming into lead-instrument chaos, playing fills where most players kept time on 'Won't Get Fooled Again' and 'My Generation.' His anarchic style influenced generations even as it defied any rulebook.
items.subtitle: The Who

items.name: Ginger Baker
items.reasoning: Baker brought jazz and African polyrhythms into rock, and his 'Toad' was one of the first extended rock drum solos on record. He is often credited as a founding father of heavy rock drumming.
items.subtitle: Cream

items.name: Stewart Copeland
items.reasoning: Copeland fused reggae, punk and jazz into a bright, hi-hat-driven style all over 'Roxanne' and 'Message in a Bottle.' Readers of Rolling Stone voted him one of the greatest drummers of all time.
items.subtitle: The Police

items.name: Danny Carey
items.reasoning: Carey builds Tool's music around odd time signatures and polymeters, playing pieces like 'Pneuma' and 'Ticks & Leeches' with jaw-dropping independence. He's a modern benchmark for progressive metal drumming.
items.subtitle: Tool

items.name: Dave Grohl
items.reasoning: Grohl's hard-hitting, hook-conscious drumming powered 'Nevermind,' and his fills on 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' are instantly recognizable. He later fronted Foo Fighters, proving rare range from behind and in front of the kit.
items.subtitle: Nirvana, Foo Fighters

items.name: Gene Krupa
items.reasoning: Krupa made the drummer a star in Benny Goodman's band and his showcase on 'Sing, Sing, Sing' put the tom-tom groove at center stage. At his urging, Slingerland developed the modern kit's tunable tom-toms.
items.subtitle: Swing pioneer

items.name: Steve Gadd
items.reasoning: Gadd's iconic groove on Paul Simon's '50 Ways to Leave Your Lover' and Steely Dan's 'Aja' made him the most in-demand session drummer of the 1970s and '80s. His feel is studied by drummers worldwide.
items.subtitle: Session legend

items.name: Phil Collins
items.reasoning: Collins was a formidable progressive drummer with Genesis before the gated-reverb fill on 'In the Air Tonight' became one of the most famous drum moments in pop history. His feel earns respect even from skeptics of his solo pop.
items.subtitle: Genesis

items.name: Questlove
items.reasoning: Questlove anchors hip-hop's premier live band with a deep, human-feel pocket heard nightly on The Tonight Show. He's also a six-time Grammy winner and an Oscar-winning cultural historian of the music.
items.subtitle: The Roots

items.name: Ringo Starr
items.reasoning: Starr is unfairly underrated: his distinctive feel and inventive fills on 'Come Together' and 'A Day in the Life' shaped how pop drums are recorded. His left-handed setup on a right-handed kit gave his playing its unmistakable lopsided swing.
items.subtitle: The Beatles

items.name: Chad Smith
items.reasoning: Smith's funk-rock pocket has locked with Flea's bass since 1988 across hits like 'Give It Away' and 'Californication.' He is a reliably powerful groove drummer and RHCP is in the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
items.subtitle: Red Hot Chili Peppers

items.name: Travis Barker
items.reasoning: Barker is pop-punk's most visible drummer, adding busy, hip-hop-influenced fills that made Blink-182's rhythm tracks stand out. His technical flash is real even if the pop-punk material rarely stretches him.
items.subtitle: Blink-182

items.name: Tommy Lee
items.reasoning: Lee is a hard-hitting showman famous for his rotating, upside-down drum roller coaster more than for subtlety. He's a solid arena-rock timekeeper whose spectacle outruns his chops.
items.subtitle: Mötley Crüe

items.name: Lars Ulrich
items.reasoning: Ulrich co-founded and drives the biggest metal band on earth, but his timing and technique are the most debated among serious drummers, and the drum tone on 'St. Anger' is widely mocked. Influence: enormous; execution: contested.
items.subtitle: Metallica

items.name: Meg White
items.reasoning: White's deliberately primitive, minimalist beats were essential to The White Stripes' garage-rock sound and 'Seven Nation Army.' Judged purely on drumming technique, though, she is the most openly disputed name in any 'best drummers' argument.
items.subtitle: The White Stripes

title: Music Producers, Ranked
tagline: The knob-twiddlers who actually made the hits.
basis: Ranked on influence, hit-making track record, sonic innovation, and how well their reputation has aged.

items.name: Quincy Jones
items.reasoning: Produced Michael Jackson's Thriller, the best-selling album of all time, and earned 28 Grammys from a record 80 nominations across jazz, pop, and film.
items.subtitle: Produced the best-selling album ever

items.name: Dr. Dre
items.reasoning: Pioneered the G-funk sound on The Chronic and launched Snoop Dogg, Eminem, and 50 Cent as a producer before co-founding Beats Electronics.
items.subtitle: Architect of G-funk

items.name: George Martin
items.reasoning: Produced nearly every Beatles recording and translated their studio experiments into orchestral arrangements, earning the nickname 'the Fifth Beatle.'
items.subtitle: The Fifth Beatle

items.name: Rick Rubin
items.reasoning: Co-founded Def Jam and produced landmark records across hip-hop, metal, and folk, from Beastie Boys and Red Hot Chili Peppers to Johnny Cash's American series.
items.subtitle: Genre-agnostic hitmaker

items.name: Timbaland
items.reasoning: Redefined late-90s and 2000s beats with stuttering, syncopated production for Missy Elliott, Aaliyah, and Justin Timberlake's FutureSex/LoveSounds.
items.subtitle: Rhythm innovator

items.name: Max Martin
items.reasoning: Has written or produced roughly 30 Billboard Hot 100 number-ones, second only to Paul McCartney, for Britney Spears, Katy Perry, Taylor Swift, and The Weeknd.
items.subtitle: The pop hit machine

items.name: Pharrell Williams
items.reasoning: As half of The Neptunes he defined the sound of 2000s radio, and later produced global hits including 'Happy' and Daft Punk's 'Get Lucky.'
items.subtitle: Half of The Neptunes

items.name: Nile Rodgers
items.reasoning: Led Chic and produced era-defining records including Bowie's 'Let's Dance,' Madonna's 'Like a Virgin,' and Daft Punk's 'Get Lucky.'
items.subtitle: Disco to Daft Punk

items.name: J Dilla
items.reasoning: His loose, off-grid drum programming on Donuts and work with A Tribe Called Quest and Slum Village made him one of hip-hop's most influential beatmakers.
items.subtitle: A producer's producer

items.name: Brian Eno
items.reasoning: Coined and pioneered ambient music, then produced landmark albums for Talking Heads, U2, and Bowie's Berlin trilogy.
items.subtitle: Inventor of ambient

items.name: Kanye West
items.reasoning: Popularized sped-up 'chipmunk soul' sampling on The College Dropout and produced for Jay-Z, though his later erratic public conduct clouds the legacy.
items.subtitle: Soul-sample visionary

items.name: Metro Boomin
items.reasoning: Shaped the dominant trap sound of the 2010s with Future and 21 Savage, and his solo album Heroes & Villains debuted at number one.
items.subtitle: Trap's defining sound

items.name: Mark Ronson
items.reasoning: Produced Amy Winehouse's Back to Black and the diamond-certified 'Uptown Funk,' winning multiple Grammys and an Academy Award.
items.subtitle: Retro-soul craftsman

items.name: Jack Antonoff
items.reasoning: Won three consecutive Producer of the Year Grammys for shaping records by Taylor Swift, Lana Del Rey, and Lorde.
items.subtitle: Indie-pop's go-to

items.name: Diplo
items.reasoning: Founded Major Lazer and co-produced the global smash 'Lean On,' but his trend-chasing across dancehall, EDM, and country draws mixed critical takes.
items.subtitle: Genre-hopping crossover

items.name: Calvin Harris
items.reasoning: Racked up a record number of UK Top 10 hits and festival anthems, though critics knock the formulaic, interchangeable drops.
items.subtitle: EDM-pop factory

items.name: DJ Khaled
items.reasoning: Assembles chart-topping all-star singles like 'I'm the One,' but is widely mocked for functioning as a hype man and executive rather than a hands-on producer.
items.subtitle: More hype than hands-on

items.name: Dr. Luke
items.reasoning: Produced massive pop hits for Katy Perry and Kesha, but Kesha's abuse allegations and the ensuing legal war made him radioactive in the industry.
items.subtitle: Talent overshadowed by scandal

items.name: Phil Spector
items.reasoning: Invented the influential 'Wall of Sound' production technique, but was convicted of second-degree murder in 2009 and died in prison, permanently tainting his name.
items.subtitle: Genius turned murderer

title: Rap Groups, Ranked
tagline: Crews that changed hip-hop, sorted without mercy.
basis: Ranked on influence, catalog depth, group chemistry, and cultural impact — not solo careers.

items.name: Wu-Tang Clan
items.reasoning: Debut 'Enter the Wu-Tang (36 Chambers)' is a foundational East Coast classic, and the nine-member roster launched solo careers for RZA, GZA, Method Man, Ghostface Killah, and Raekwon. No group did more to redefine the collective in rap.
items.subtitle: Staten Island, 1992

items.name: N.W.A
items.reasoning: 1988's 'Straight Outta Compton' pushed West Coast gangsta rap into the mainstream and drew an FBI warning letter over 'Fuck tha Police.' Members Dr. Dre, Ice Cube, and Eazy-E became genre-defining figures.
items.subtitle: Compton gangsta pioneers

items.name: OutKast
items.reasoning: André 3000 and Big Boi won the Grammy for Album of the Year with 2003's 'Speakerboxxx/The Love Below,' and 'Hey Ya!' became a global smash. They put Southern rap on equal artistic footing with the coasts.
items.subtitle: ATLiens who broke pop

items.name: A Tribe Called Quest
items.reasoning: 'The Low End Theory' (1991) fused jazz sampling with lyrical dexterity and is routinely cited among the greatest hip-hop albums ever. Q-Tip and Phife Dawg's chemistry anchored the Native Tongues movement.
items.subtitle: Jazz-rap architects

items.name: Run-DMC
items.reasoning: Their 'Walk This Way' collaboration with Aerosmith crossed rap onto MTV and rock radio, and in 2009 they became one of the first hip-hop acts inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame. Foundational to the genre's crossover.
items.subtitle: The kings from Hollis

items.name: Public Enemy
items.reasoning: 'It Takes a Nation of Millions to Hold Us Back' (1988) set the standard for politically charged rap, and the Bomb Squad's dense production was revolutionary. Chuck D's booming delivery and Flavor Flav's foil remain iconic.
items.subtitle: Politics at full volume

items.name: Beastie Boys
items.reasoning: 'Licensed to Ill' (1986) was the first rap album to top the Billboard 200, and 'Paul's Boutique' is a sample-collage landmark. They evolved from bratty punks into respected genre-crossers and Hall of Famers.
items.subtitle: Licensed to Ill and beyond

items.name: Mobb Deep
items.reasoning: 'The Infamous' (1995), especially 'Shook Ones, Pt. II,' defined hardcore East Coast rap's bleak, cinematic sound. Prodigy and Havoc's production-and-rhyme partnership was airtight.
items.subtitle: Queensbridge grit

items.name: Migos
items.reasoning: 'Culture' (2017) hit No. 1 and 'Bad and Boujee' topped the Hot 100, and their triplet flow shaped a generation of trap. Massive commercial run, though catalog depth is thin next to the legends.
items.subtitle: Triplet-flow trendsetters

items.name: Bone Thugs-n-Harmony
items.reasoning: 'Tha Crossroads' won a Grammy and their rapid-fire sung-rap style was genuinely new in the mid-'90s. A signature sound, but an uneven catalog after their peak.
items.subtitle: Cleveland's harmonized flow

items.name: Cypress Hill
items.reasoning: First Latino hip-hop group to go platinum, with B-Real's nasal delivery and DJ Muggs' psychedelic production making 'Insane in the Brain' a crossover hit. Later got a Hollywood Walk of Fame star.
items.subtitle: Latin rap, blunted

items.name: Gang Starr
items.reasoning: The duo of Guru and DJ Premier are boom-bap royalty, and Premier's chopped-sample production is among the most influential in rap history. A purist favorite that never chased crossover fame.
items.subtitle: Guru + DJ Premier

items.name: The Roots
items.reasoning: One of the few hip-hop acts built on live instrumentation, with 'Things Fall Apart' going platinum and winning a Grammy for 'You Got Me.' Now the house band on 'The Tonight Show.'
items.subtitle: Rap's greatest live band

items.name: Three 6 Mafia
items.reasoning: First rap group to win an Academy Award, for 'It's Hard out Here for a Pimp' in 2006, and their dark Memphis sound heavily influenced modern trap. Uneven albums keep them out of the top tiers.
items.subtitle: Memphis horrorcore

items.name: Fugees
items.reasoning: 'The Score' (1996) sold over six million copies and launched Lauryn Hill and Wyclef Jean, but the group made only two albums before splintering. A brilliant peak with almost no longevity.
items.subtitle: One perfect album

items.name: Salt-N-Pepa
items.reasoning: One of the first commercially successful female rap acts, with 'Push It' and 'Whatta Man' as enduring hits and a Grammy win in 1995. Historically important, though their run was fairly short.
items.subtitle: Pioneering women of rap

items.name: Rae Sremmurd
items.reasoning: 'Black Beatles' hit No. 1 riding the Mannequin Challenge, and 'SremmLife' produced a string of radio hits. Fun and catchy, but light on the depth that defines the higher tiers.
items.subtitle: Brothers of the mannequin era

items.name: G-Unit
items.reasoning: Rode 50 Cent's early-2000s dominance to a platinum debut with 'Beg for Mercy,' but the group functioned mostly as a vehicle for its star and fizzled amid internal beefs. Little that stands on its own.
items.subtitle: 50 Cent's crew

items.name: Ying Yang Twins
items.reasoning: Best known for 'Wait (The Whisper Song),' a novelty crunk hit widely mocked for its crude, repetitive lyrics. A ringtone-era footnote with almost no lasting critical standing.
items.subtitle: The whisper song

title: Broadway Musicals, Ranked
tagline: The Great White Way, sorted from standing ovation to standing joke.
basis: Ranked on artistic impact, awards pedigree, cultural staying power, and how well the score and staging hold up.

items.name: Hamilton
items.reasoning: Miranda's hip-hop history won the 2016 Pulitzer Prize for Drama and 11 Tony Awards from a record 16 nominations. It reshaped what a Broadway score could sound like and became a global cultural phenomenon.
items.subtitle: Lin-Manuel Miranda, 2015

items.name: Les Misérables
items.reasoning: The Boublil-Schönberg epic won eight 1987 Tony Awards including Best Musical and is the longest-running musical in West End history. Its sweeping score made 'One Day More' a global anthem.
items.subtitle: Boublil & Schönberg, 1985

items.name: The Phantom of the Opera
items.reasoning: Ran 35 years and 13,981 performances to become the longest-running show in Broadway history before closing in 2023. It won seven 1988 Tony Awards including Best Musical.

art.title: The Phantom of the Opera (1986 musical)

items.subtitle: Andrew Lloyd Webber, 1986

items.name: Sweeney Todd
items.reasoning: Sondheim's blood-soaked musical thriller won eight 1979 Tony Awards including Best Musical and is widely considered his masterpiece. Its near-operatic score is a benchmark for musical-theatre ambition.

art.title: Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street

items.subtitle: Stephen Sondheim, 1979

items.name: West Side Story
items.reasoning: Leonard Bernstein's score and Jerome Robbins's choreography reinvented the Broadway dance musical, transplanting Romeo and Juliet to 1950s New York. Its songs 'Tonight' and 'Somewhere' are standards.
items.subtitle: Bernstein & Sondheim, 1957

items.name: The Book of Mormon
items.reasoning: From the creators of South Park and Avenue Q's Robert Lopez, it won nine 2011 Tony Awards including Best Musical. It remains one of Broadway's biggest comedy hits over a decade later.
items.subtitle: Parker, Stone & Lopez, 2011

items.name: Hadestown
items.reasoning: Anaïs Mitchell's folk-jazz retelling of the Orpheus myth won eight 2019 Tony Awards including Best Musical. Its atmospheric staging and 'Way Down Hadestown' proved new work could still dominate the season.
items.subtitle: Anaïs Mitchell, 2019

items.name: Wicked
items.reasoning: This Wizard of Oz prequel has grossed over $1.6 billion on Broadway alone and is among the highest-grossing productions in history. 'Defying Gravity' became a defining showstopper for a generation.
items.subtitle: Stephen Schwartz, 2003

items.name: Fiddler on the Roof
