items.reasoning: It won nine 1965 Tony Awards and was the first Broadway musical to surpass 3,000 performances. 'Tradition' and 'If I Were a Rich Man' remain instantly recognizable.
items.subtitle: Bock & Harnick, 1964

items.name: Rent
items.reasoning: Jonathan Larson's rock musical won the 1996 Pulitzer Prize for Drama and the Tony for Best Musical, spotlighting the AIDS crisis for a young audience. 'Seasons of Love' became a cultural touchstone despite the story feeling dated to some.
items.subtitle: Jonathan Larson, 1996

items.name: The Lion King
items.reasoning: Julie Taymor's puppet-driven staging won six 1998 Tony Awards and is the highest-grossing Broadway production of all time. Its visual spectacle is unmatched even if the score leans on the film.
items.subtitle: Elton John & Julie Taymor, 1997

items.name: In the Heights
items.reasoning: Miranda's Washington Heights debut won four 2008 Tony Awards including Best Musical and Best Score. It fused salsa, merengue, and hip-hop years before Hamilton made him a household name.
items.subtitle: Lin-Manuel Miranda, 2008

items.name: Chicago
items.reasoning: Its 1996 revival is the longest-running American musical in Broadway history and won six 1997 Tony Awards. The Kander and Ebb score and Fosse-style staging remain endlessly revivable.
items.subtitle: Kander & Ebb, 1975

items.name: A Chorus Line
items.reasoning: It won nine 1976 Tony Awards and the 1976 Pulitzer Prize for Drama, running 15 years and 6,137 performances. Its raw, dancer-focused format was groundbreaking for its era.
items.subtitle: Marvin Hamlisch, 1975

items.name: Dear Evan Hansen
items.reasoning: It won six 2017 Tony Awards including Best Musical on the strength of 'Waving Through a Window,' but its protagonist's deception has drawn heavy backlash. The critically panned 2021 film soured its legacy.
items.subtitle: Pasek & Paul, 2016

items.name: Cats
items.reasoning: Lloyd Webber's plotless spectacle ran 18 years and was once Broadway's longest-running show, winning seven 1983 Tonys off the strength of 'Memory.' Its threadbare story and infamous 2019 film keep it divisive.
items.subtitle: Andrew Lloyd Webber, 1981

items.name: Starlight Express
items.reasoning: The roller-skate spectacle about anthropomorphic trains was critically savaged and closed on Broadway after under two years. It survives mostly as a curiosity kept alive by a long-running Bochum, Germany production.
items.subtitle: Andrew Lloyd Webber, 1984

items.name: Spider-Man: Turn Off the Dark
items.reasoning: At roughly $75 million it was the most expensive Broadway show ever and never recouped, plagued by multiple cast injuries during aerial stunts and brutal reviews. It became the industry's byword for a troubled production.
items.subtitle: Bono & The Edge, 2011

title: MMORPGs, Ranked
tagline: The virtual worlds that ate our lives, sorted.
basis: Ranked on genre impact, longevity, endgame depth, and how well each world still holds up today.

items.name: World of Warcraft
items.reasoning: Peaked at over 12 million subscribers in October 2010, making it the most successful subscription MMORPG in history. Two decades on it still sets the template the entire genre copies.
items.subtitle: The one that defined the genre

items.name: Final Fantasy XIV
items.reasoning: After the disastrous 2010 launch, Square Enix rebuilt it from scratch as A Realm Reborn in 2013 and turned it into one of its most profitable properties. It's now routinely cited as the best-written MMORPG on the market.
items.subtitle: Gaming's greatest comeback

items.name: Old School RuneScape
items.reasoning: Relaunched in 2013 from a 2007 backup after players demanded it, and now dwarfs the modern version in popularity. Major updates are decided by community polls, which today require 70% approval to pass.
items.subtitle: The 2007 build that refused to die

items.name: The Elder Scrolls Online
items.reasoning: Ditched its subscription in 2015 and has since grown to over 24 million players worldwide. Its sprawling story chapters make it the closest thing to a persistent single-player Elder Scrolls.
items.subtitle: Tamriel, fully online

items.name: Guild Wars 2
items.reasoning: Launched in 2012 with a buy-once model and dynamic world events that replaced the usual quest-hub grind. Its horizontal endgame and living-world updates keep it a genre favorite over a decade later.
items.subtitle: No subscription, no trinity

items.name: EVE Online
items.reasoning: Runs almost entirely on a single shared server where every player coexists in one galaxy. Player-run wars have destroyed virtual assets worth hundreds of thousands of real dollars in single battles, like the 2014 Bloodbath of B-R5RB.
items.subtitle: Spreadsheets in space

items.name: Lost Ark
items.reasoning: Amazon's 2022 Western launch peaked at over 1.3 million concurrent Steam players, the second-highest in the platform's history at the time. Its raids and combat are elite, though the endgame grind burns many out.
items.subtitle: Isometric loot machine

items.name: Star Wars: The Old Republic
items.reasoning: Was among the most expensive games ever made at the time, reportedly costing around $200 million with every quest fully voice-acted, earning a Guinness record for the largest voice-over project. The class stories are superb even if the surrounding MMO scaffolding aged poorly.
items.subtitle: Fully voiced, cost $200M

items.name: Black Desert Online
items.reasoning: Launched in Korea in 2014 and reached the West in 2016 with action combat and a graphics engine still regarded as best-in-class for the genre. Deep life-skill systems are undercut by a notoriously heavy grind and cash shop.
items.subtitle: The prettiest grind

items.name: EverQuest
items.reasoning: The 1999 title that popularized the 3D MMORPG and was so addictive players dubbed it 'EverCrack.' It laid the raid-and-grind groundwork WoW would later refine for the mainstream.
items.subtitle: Nicknamed EverCrack

items.name: Ultima Online
items.reasoning: Released in 1997, it was one of the first commercially successful graphical MMORPGs and proved a persistent online world could scale. Its open, player-driven economy and PvP influenced everything that followed.
items.subtitle: The graphical pioneer

items.name: Lineage II
items.reasoning: A 2003 NCSoft hit that dominated Korean PC bangs with massive castle-siege PvP. Rampant grind and gold-farming define both its legend and its reputation.
items.subtitle: Korea's siege king

items.name: MapleStory
items.reasoning: A 2003 side-scrolling MMORPG that became a global phenomenon with hundreds of millions of registered accounts. Charming and accessible, but buried under years of power creep and heavy monetization.
items.subtitle: 2D side-scroll giant

items.name: Aion
items.reasoning: NCSoft's 2009 release built its identity around flight and the PvPvE Abyss zone. A strong launch faded fast, pushing it to free-to-play by 2012 in the West.
items.subtitle: Wings and faction war

items.name: RIFT
items.reasoning: Launched in 2011 with a flexible soul-based class system and dynamic rift invasions. Polished and well-received at first, it never carved out enough identity to escape WoW's shadow.
items.subtitle: The competent WoW clone

items.name: Age of Conan
items.reasoning: Its 2008 launch and brutal directional combat drew huge early praise, but content thinned out sharply past the polished starting zone. Subscriptions collapsed within months as players hit the wall.
items.subtitle: Great start, empty endgame

items.name: Warhammer Online
items.reasoning: Sold on realm-vs-realm warfare in 2008 but launched over-scoped, cutting classes and cities before release. It bled players quickly and was shut down entirely in 2013.
items.subtitle: Realm-vs-realm that fizzled

items.name: WildStar
items.reasoning: Hyped for its combat and humor in 2014, it chased hardcore 40-man raiders with punishing attunements that gutted its casual base. It went free-to-play in 2015 and shut down for good in 2018.
items.subtitle: Killed by its own hardcore raids

title: Roguelike Games, Ranked
tagline: Die, learn, run it back until the RNG loves you.
basis: Ranked on run-to-run variety, build depth, "one more run" pull, and lasting influence on the genre.

items.name: Hades
items.reasoning: Supergiant's action-roguelike won the first-ever Hugo Award for Best Video Game and swept multiple 2020 Game of the Year honors, fusing tight combat with story that advances every death.
items.subtitle: Supergiant, 2020

items.name: Slay the Spire
items.reasoning: MegaCrit's 2019 release essentially defined the roguelike deckbuilder and spawned countless imitators, praised for near-endless card synergy across its four characters.
items.subtitle: Deckbuilding blueprint

items.name: Balatro
items.reasoning: LocalThunk's 2024 poker roguelike won a BAFTA (Best Debut Game) and a Golden Joystick award and was a Game of the Year nominee at The Game Awards, selling millions on jokers-and-hands math alone.
items.subtitle: Poker, weaponized

items.name: The Binding of Isaac: Rebirth
items.reasoning: Edmund McMillen's 2014 remake stacks hundreds of items into absurd synergies and remains one of the most-played roguelikes years after launch thanks to sprawling expansions.
items.subtitle: Item soup supreme

items.name: Dead Cells
items.reasoning: Motion Twin's 2018 hit blends fast 2D combat with permadeath progression and earned wide acclaim plus years of free content updates.
items.subtitle: Metroidvania meets roguelite

items.name: Spelunky
items.reasoning: Derek Yu's game turned handcrafted-feeling procedural caves into a genre landmark; its 2020 sequel Spelunky 2 deepened the systems to more acclaim.
items.subtitle: The precision platformer roguelike

items.name: Risk of Rain 2
items.reasoning: Hopoo Games' 2020 shift to 3D kept the escalating item-stacking chaos, where difficulty scales with the clock and builds snowball into screen-clearing madness.
items.subtitle: Time is the real boss

items.name: Enter the Gungeon
items.reasoning: Dodge Roll's 2016 shooter is a dense bullet-hell roguelike stuffed with pun-heavy guns, widely praised for tight controls and secret depth.
items.subtitle: Bullet hell dungeon crawl

items.name: FTL: Faster Than Light
items.reasoning: This 2012 Kickstarter breakout made real-time spaceship management a nail-biter and is credited with helping revive the roguelike genre for a wide audience.
items.subtitle: The tense spaceship classic

items.name: Rogue Legacy
items.reasoning: Cellar Door's 2013 game popularized persistent meta-progression, letting each descendant carry traits and upgrades that soften permadeath's sting.
items.subtitle: Inherit your heir's quirks

items.name: Darkest Dungeon
items.reasoning: Red Hook's 2016 gothic RPG layers a punishing stress-and-affliction system over party roguelike runs, beloved for its narrator and brutal tone.
items.subtitle: Stress will kill you first

items.name: Hades II
items.reasoning: Supergiant's 2024 sequel entered early access to strong reviews, expanding the formula with a new witch protagonist and dual-realm structure, though still unfinished.
items.subtitle: Early access, high ceiling

items.name: NetHack
items.reasoning: First released in 1987 and still updated, it is one of the deepest traditional roguelikes, famous for the mantra that 'the dev team thinks of everything.'
items.subtitle: The ASCII ancestor

items.name: Crypt of the NecroDancer
items.reasoning: Brace Yourself Games' 2015 title fuses roguelike dungeon crawling with rhythm mechanics, a clever hybrid that later powered the official Zelda spin-off Cadence of Hyrule.
items.subtitle: Move to the beat

items.name: Nuclear Throne
items.reasoning: A frantic top-down shooter roguelike from Vlambeer that left early access in 2015, well regarded for punchy gunplay and mutant builds but notoriously unforgiving to newcomers.
items.subtitle: Vlambeer's twitchy swan song

items.name: Rogue
items.reasoning: The 1980 dungeon crawler that gave the entire genre its name, with procedural levels and permadeath that every entry here still descends from.
items.subtitle: The game that named it all

items.name: Vampire Survivors
items.reasoning: This 2022 auto-attacking horde survivor won the BAFTA for Best Game and launched the whole 'survivors-like' wave, though its minimal-input loop stretches the roguelike label.
items.subtitle: Barely a game, wildly addictive

items.name: Moonlighter
items.reasoning: Digital Sun's 2018 game pairs dungeon runs with a shop-management sim; a charming idea, but combat and dungeon variety drew middling criticism.
items.subtitle: Shopkeeper by day, looter by night

items.name: Necropolis
items.reasoning: The 2016 action roguelike from Harebrained Schemes drew mixed-to-negative reviews (Metacritic in the high 50s) for clunky combat and repetitive environments, a notable stumble for the studio.
items.subtitle: A rare Harebrained misfire

title: Sports Video Games, Ranked
tagline: Every dynasty, every clunker, sorted for good.
basis: A ranking of the most influential and well-known sports video games by critical acclaim, gameplay, cultural footprint, and lasting legacy.

items.name: Tony Hawk's Pro Skater 2
items.reasoning: Holds a 98 on Metacritic, one of the highest-rated games ever, and introduced the manual that chained combos into endless flow.
items.subtitle: The genre's high-water mark

items.name: NBA 2K11
items.reasoning: Put Michael Jordan on the cover and built the acclaimed Jordan Challenge mode, cementing 2K's dominance over basketball sims.
items.subtitle: The Jordan Challenge

items.name: ESPN NFL 2K5
items.reasoning: Launched at $19.99 to undercut Madden and is still praised for its presentation; EA's exclusive NFL license soon ended the series.
items.subtitle: The $20 giant-killer

items.name: Wii Sports
items.reasoning: Bundled with the Wii, it became one of the best-selling games of all time and brought motion-controlled sports to living rooms worldwide.
items.subtitle: Everyone's first swing

items.name: NHL '94
items.reasoning: Immortalized in the film Swingers and regarded by many as the greatest hockey game ever, with a devoted competitive community decades later.
items.subtitle: Still the benchmark

items.name: Rocket League
items.reasoning: Soccer played with rocket-powered cars; a runaway hit that went free-to-play in 2020 and sustained a massive esports scene.
items.subtitle: Cars, ball, chaos

items.name: SSX Tricky
items.reasoning: Over-the-top arcade snowboarding fueled by Run-DMC's 'It's Tricky,' a defining extreme-sports game of the early 2000s.
items.subtitle: Uber tricks only

items.name: Madden NFL 2004
items.reasoning: Michael Vick's cover year introduced playmaker control and owner mode, one of the most fondly remembered Madden entries.
items.subtitle: The Vick era

items.name: NBA Jam
items.reasoning: Midway's two-on-two arcade basketball with 'He's on fire!' and gravity-defying dunks was a 1993 phenomenon.
items.subtitle: Boomshakalaka

items.name: MLB The Show 21
items.reasoning: The premier baseball sim and the first entry to leave PlayStation exclusivity, launching day-one on Xbox Game Pass.
items.subtitle: Diamond standard

items.name: Fight Night Champion
items.reasoning: The last of EA's boxing series, praised for its cinematic Champion story mode and brutal, physical combat.
items.subtitle: Sweet science, sharp

items.name: Virtua Tennis
items.reasoning: Sega's pick-up-and-play tennis nailed fast, satisfying rallies and became a Dreamcast standout.
items.subtitle: Arcade ace

items.name: NFL Blitz
items.reasoning: Midway's no-rules arcade football with late hits and on-fire streaks, the gridiron cousin of NBA Jam.
items.subtitle: Football, unpenalized

items.name: FIFA 14
items.reasoning: Solid but iterative football, remembered less for on-pitch leaps than for the money-printing rise of Ultimate Team.
items.subtitle: Ultimate Team era

items.name: Kinect Sports
items.reasoning: Motion-controlled party sports for Xbox Kinect, approachable at launch but shallow and quickly dated.
items.subtitle: Wave your arms

items.name: NBA Live 14
items.reasoning: Rushed out against NBA 2K14, it was widely panned for stiff animation and rough visuals, a low point for the Live line.
items.subtitle: Outmatched by 2K

items.name: NBA Elite 11
items.reasoning: So broken it was scrapped just before release after a glitch of a player frozen mid-air went viral, and EA killed the whole game.
items.subtitle: Cancelled by bugs

items.name: Backbreaker
items.reasoning: Hyped on its Euphoria physics engine but shipped with bizarre tackles, no NFL license, and clunky play, a cautionary tale.
items.subtitle: Physics gone wrong

title: Puzzle Games, Ranked
tagline: Falling blocks, folding worlds, and the tiles that ate your commute.
basis: Ranked on design elegance, influence on the genre, and how well the core idea holds up decades later — not nostalgia or sales alone.

items.name: Tetris
items.reasoning: Created by Alexey Pajitnov in 1984, it is the best-selling video game franchise ever, with over 520 million copies sold as of December 2024. Seven pieces, one rule, endless depth — the platonic ideal of the puzzle game.
items.subtitle: The perfect game

items.name: Portal 2
items.reasoning: Valve's 2011 sequel holds a Metacritic score of 95 and is frequently cited among the greatest games ever made. Its two-player co-op campaign is a masterclass in escalating spatial puzzles.
items.subtitle: Thinking with portals

items.name: Tetris Effect
items.reasoning: Tetsuya Mizuguchi's studio Enhance reinvented the classic in 2018, fusing the ruleset with reactive music and visuals across dozens of evolving stages. It turns a 1984 formula into a synesthetic, near-transcendent experience.
items.subtitle: Tetris as a religious experience

items.name: Portal
items.reasoning: Released in 2007 as part of The Orange Box, it introduced GLaDOS, the Companion Cube, and a mechanic so novel it spawned its own subgenre. A tight, near-flawless few hours that punched far above its length.
items.subtitle: The cake is a lie

items.name: Baba Is You
items.reasoning: Arvi Teikari's 2019 game lets you push the words that define the rules themselves, so 'Wall Is Stop' can become 'Wall Is Win.' Its radical design won IGF Excellence in Design and the Game Developers Choice Best Design award.
items.subtitle: Rewrite the rules

items.name: The Witness
items.reasoning: Jonathan Blow's 2016 open-world island teaches its entire visual language through its roughly 650 puzzles, without a single word of instruction. Its escalating panel logic is one of gaming's finest self-taught systems.
items.subtitle: 650 line puzzles and no tutorial

items.name: Return of the Obra Dinn
items.reasoning: Lucas Pope's 2018 mystery uses a 1-bit art style and a magic pocket watch to have you identify the fate of all 60 souls on a ghost ship. It reinvented the deduction puzzle around pure inference.
items.subtitle: 60 souls, one deduction

items.name: Braid
items.reasoning: Jonathan Blow's 2008 debut built each world around a different manipulation of time, from full rewinds to time-linked shadows. It was a landmark for the indie movement and puzzle-platformer design.
items.subtitle: Rewind and rethink

items.name: The Talos Principle
items.reasoning: Croteam's 2014 first-person game wraps laser-and-jammer puzzles in a story about consciousness and free will. Smart, generous puzzle design paired with genuinely thoughtful writing.
items.subtitle: Puzzles with a philosophy degree

items.name: Myst
items.reasoning: The 1993 Cyan classic was the best-selling PC game of the 1990s until The Sims overtook it in 2002. Its pre-rendered worlds and obtuse, atmospheric puzzles defined an entire era of adventure gaming.
items.subtitle: The island that sold CD-ROMs

items.name: Monument Valley
items.reasoning: ustwo's 2014 mobile game turns impossible M. C. Escher geometry into rotatable, playable architecture. A short, gorgeous experience that proved touchscreens could deliver artful puzzles.
items.subtitle: Escher you can walk through

items.name: Lumines
items.reasoning: Mizuguchi's 2004 PSP launch title synced block-clearing to a sweeping timeline bar driven by music. A stylish, rhythmic take on the falling-block formula that became a handheld favorite.
items.subtitle: The music makes the move

items.name: Puyo Puyo
items.reasoning: Compile's colorful match-four series, born in 1991 and Sega-owned since 1998, is built around chaining combos that dump garbage on your opponent. It remains a staple of the competitive puzzle scene decades on.
items.subtitle: Competitive blob chains

items.name: Bejeweled
items.reasoning: PopCap's 2001 hit codified the swap-and-match-three mechanic that countless games would later copy. Endlessly playable, but its own influence eventually made it feel ordinary.
items.subtitle: The match-three that started it all

items.name: Professor Layton and the Curious Village
items.reasoning: Level-5's 2007 DS game strings together classic riddles, sliding puzzles, and logic teasers with a charming animated mystery. Delightful, if closer to a puzzle anthology than a unified design.

art.title: Professor Layton and the Curious Village

items.subtitle: Brain teasers with a top hat

items.reasoning: Gabriele Cirulli built this number-merging game over a single weekend in 2014, itself derived from Threes and 1024. Wildly addictive and open-source, but essentially a clone of a clone.
items.subtitle: One more swipe

items.name: Angry Birds
items.reasoning: Rovio's 2009 phenomenon has been downloaded billions of times on the strength of one satisfying slingshot mechanic. The physics are fun, but the puzzle depth is thin and the brand drowned in spinoffs.
items.subtitle: Slingshot physics, endless sequels

items.name: Candy Crush Saga
items.reasoning: King's 2012 match-three is a competent puzzle wrapped in one of the most aggressive monetization systems in gaming, with lives, timers, and paywalled difficulty spikes. It has grossed over a billion dollars a year by design, not by depth.
items.subtitle: Pay to keep matching

title: Pasta Dishes, Ranked
tagline: The definitive plate-by-plate verdict on pasta's greatest hits.
basis: Ranked on flavor, technique, cultural staying power, and how badly it can be ruined — from Roman perfection down to the canned crimes.

items.name: Carbonara
items.reasoning: Built from just guanciale, egg, Pecorino Romano and black pepper, it turns pantry staples into a silky emulsion with no cream in sight. Its restraint and technical demand make it the benchmark every cook is judged against.
items.subtitle: Rome's four-ingredient masterpiece

items.name: Lasagne
items.reasoning: Layers of pasta, ragù, béchamel and cheese baked into a self-contained meal; the Bolognese version 'lasagne alla Bolognese' is a documented regional classic. It scales to feed a crowd and reheats better than almost any dish here.
items.subtitle: The layered centerpiece

items.name: Cacio e pepe
items.reasoning: Just Pecorino Romano, black pepper and starchy pasta water emulsified into a glossy sauce with zero fat added beyond the cheese. Its simplicity is deceptive — break the emulsion and you get a clumpy mess, which is exactly why nailing it is a flex.
items.subtitle: Cheese, pepper, and pure nerve

items.name: Ragù alla Bolognese
items.reasoning: A slow-cooked meat sauce from Bologna traditionally served with tagliatelle, not spaghetti, per the official recipe lodged with the city's Chamber of Commerce in 1982. Depth of flavor from hours of simmering makes it a benchmark ragù.
items.subtitle: The slow-simmered meat sauce

items.name: Pesto alla Genovese
items.reasoning: A raw sauce of basil, pine nuts, garlic, Parmesan, Pecorino and Ligurian olive oil pounded in a mortar, classically tossed with trofie or trenette. No cooking means peak fresh-basil aroma when made right.
items.subtitle: Liguria's green gold

items.name: Amatriciana
items.reasoning: From the town of Amatrice, it pairs guanciale and tomato with Pecorino Romano and holds EU 'Traditional Speciality Guaranteed' status. It's the tomato-forward cousin of carbonara and just as beloved in Rome.
items.subtitle: Guanciale, tomato, Pecorino

items.name: Ravioli
items.reasoning: Filled pasta pockets documented in Italian cooking since at least the 14th century, versatile enough to hold ricotta-spinach, meat or squash. The dough-to-filling ratio rewards skilled handmade work.
items.subtitle: Stuffed and sealed

items.name: Spaghetti alle vongole
items.reasoning: A Neapolitan staple of spaghetti with fresh clams, garlic, olive oil and often white wine, letting the brine be the seasoning. Minimal ingredients mean the clams have to be genuinely fresh, which keeps it honest.
items.subtitle: Clams, garlic, white wine

items.name: Fettuccine Alfredo
items.reasoning: Invented in Rome by Alfredo di Lelio around 1908 as just butter and Parmesan tossed with fresh fettuccine. The American cream-and-garlic version is heavier and less refined than the original, dragging its average down.
items.subtitle: Butter and Parmesan, Rome-born

items.name: Pasta alla vodka
items.reasoning: A creamy tomato sauce spiked with vodka that rose to popularity in the 1970s–80s and stormed back via social media. The alcohol genuinely helps release fat-soluble tomato flavors, so it's more than a gimmick.
items.subtitle: Tomato, cream, a splash of vodka

items.name: Gnocchi
items.reasoning: Soft potato-and-flour dumplings that are technically pasta-adjacent and endlessly saucable, from butter-sage to Gorgonzola. Overwork the dough and they turn to gummy erasers, which is the eternal risk.
items.subtitle: Pillowy potato dumplings

items.name: Tortellini
items.reasoning: Tiny navel-shaped stuffed pasta from Emilia, classically served in capon broth as tortellini in brodo. Hand-folding them is painstaking, which is why the good ones command respect.
items.subtitle: Emilia's ring-shaped stuffed pasta

items.name: Pasta alla puttanesca
items.reasoning: A bold Neapolitan sauce of tomatoes, olives, capers, garlic and anchovies that delivers big salty-umami flavor from cheap pantry ingredients. It's polarizing thanks to the anchovies, but that intensity is the point.
items.subtitle: Olives, capers, anchovy punch

items.name: Macaroni and cheese
items.reasoning: Elbow macaroni bound in a cheese sauce, with a version appearing in an American cookbook by 1824 and popularized by Thomas Jefferson's circle. Beloved comfort food, but the boxed-powder ubiquity keeps it out of the upper tiers.
items.subtitle: Comfort in a cheese sauce

items.name: Spaghetti and meatballs
items.reasoning: An Italian-American creation rather than a dish you'll find in Italy, born from cheap-meat abundance among immigrants in the early 1900s. Crowd-pleasing and nostalgic, but purists dock it for not being authentically Italian.
items.subtitle: The Italian-American icon

items.name: Baked ziti
items.reasoning: Ziti baked with tomato sauce, ricotta and mozzarella, a staple of Italian-American home cooking and church potlucks. Reliable and filling, but it's basically deconstructed lasagne with less finesse.
items.subtitle: The casserole-dish workhorse

items.name: Pasta salad
items.reasoning: Cold cooked pasta tossed with vegetables and dressing, a picnic and deli-counter mainstay that peaked in 1980s America. Serviceable in bulk, but overcooked noodles and heavy mayo dressings make it the least ambitious pasta here.
items.subtitle: The cold buffet-table filler

items.name: SpaghettiOs
items.reasoning: A canned pasta product launched by Campbell's Franco-American brand in 1965, made of ring pasta in a sweetened tomato sauce. Mushy texture and candy-sweet sauce make it nutrition-of-convenience, not a dish anyone cooks on purpose.
items.subtitle: Pasta from a can

title: Cuts of Beef, Ranked
tagline: From marbled royalty to jerky-tier tragedy.
basis: Ranked by flavor, tenderness, and marbling — how good the cut actually tastes off the grill or out of the braiser, not by menu price.

items.name: Ribeye
items.reasoning: Cut from the rib primal (ribs 6–12), it carries the heaviest intramuscular fat of any common steak, giving the richest beefy flavor. Its spinalis cap muscle is widely regarded as the single best bite on the cow.
items.subtitle: The marbling king

items.name: Filet Mignon
items.reasoning: Taken from the small end of the tenderloin, a muscle that does almost no work, making it the single most tender cut on the animal. Its buttery texture is why it commands the highest per-pound price of the classic steaks.
items.subtitle: Butter-soft tenderloin

items.name: Prime Rib
items.reasoning: A bone-in standing rib roast is the same muscle as the ribeye, slow-roasted whole and carved tableside. Roasting on the bone yields an evenly rosy, edge-to-edge medium-rare interior no single steak can match.
items.subtitle: The holiday centerpiece

items.name: New York Strip
items.reasoning: Cut from the short loin, it balances tenderness and a firm, beefy chew better than almost any other steak. It's the reliable steakhouse standard precisely because it delivers big flavor without the price of a filet.
items.subtitle: The steakhouse default

items.name: Picanha
items.reasoning: The top sirloin cap, prized in Brazilian churrascarias where it's skewered and grilled with its fat cap on. That rendering fat layer bastes the lean meat, giving huge flavor at a fraction of ribeye's cost.
items.subtitle: Brazil's crown cut

items.name: T-Bone / Porterhouse
items.reasoning: A single cut with a strip steak on one side of the bone and a tenderloin on the other, so you get both textures in one slab. The porterhouse is simply a T-bone cut with a larger tenderloin portion.
items.subtitle: Two steaks, one bone

items.name: Brisket
items.reasoning: One of the toughest raw cuts, from the chest, but hours of low-and-slow smoking break down its collagen into Texas BBQ or pastrami. Reward is enormous, but it punishes any cook who rushes it.
items.subtitle: Low and slow royalty

items.name: Short Ribs
items.reasoning: Heavily marbled ribs from the chuck and plate that turn fall-apart tender after long braising, and star raw and thin-sliced in Korean galbi. Deeply flavorful, but useless without hours of cooking.
items.subtitle: Braise-it-forever gold

items.name: Sirloin
items.reasoning: Cut from just behind the short loin, it's leaner and firmer than premium steaks but genuinely flavorful and affordable. A solid everyday grilling steak that never quite reaches greatness.
items.subtitle: The dependable workhorse

items.name: Tri-Tip
items.reasoning: A triangular cut from the bottom sirloin, made famous by Santa Maria–style barbecue in California. Big beefy flavor and a bargain price, though its varying grain means you must slice it correctly to keep it tender.
items.subtitle: Santa Maria's specialty

items.name: Hanger Steak
items.reasoning: A single cut that hangs from the diaphragm — the source of its name — historically kept by butchers for themselves, hence the nickname 'butcher's steak.' Intensely beefy and tender when cooked to medium-rare and sliced against the grain, but it toughens fast if overcooked.
items.subtitle: The butcher's secret

items.name: Flat Iron
items.reasoning: Cut from the shoulder (chuck) with the tough central sinew removed, it's surprisingly tender for its low price. Good marbling and flavor, but it can't shake the faintly livery note of shoulder meat.
items.subtitle: Cheap and tender

items.name: Flank Steak
items.reasoning: A lean, fibrous cut from the abdominal muscles, popular for fajitas and London broil. Flavorful but genuinely chewy unless marinated and sliced thinly against its coarse grain.
items.subtitle: Marinate or regret it

items.name: Skirt Steak
items.reasoning: The diaphragm muscle, loose-grained and beefy, and the classic cut for carne asada and fajitas. Loaded with flavor but tough and stringy, demanding a hot, fast sear and a sharp knife against the grain.
items.subtitle: Carne asada champ

items.name: Oxtail
items.reasoning: The tail, mostly bone and connective tissue, that melts into rich, gelatinous stews and soups after hours of braising. Beloved in Caribbean and Korean cooking, but it's a slow-cook project, never a steak.
items.subtitle: All collagen, all patience

items.name: Chuck Steak
items.reasoning: From the hard-working shoulder, it's flavorful but riddled with sinew and connective tissue that make it tough as a grilled steak. It's far better ground into burgers or braised into pot roast than cooked over direct heat.
items.subtitle: Grind it or braise it

items.name: Round Steak
items.reasoning: Cut from the rear leg, it's extremely lean with almost no marbling, so it cooks up dry and tough on the grill. Its best destiny is thin deli roast beef, cube steak, or jerky, not a plate on its own.
items.subtitle: Dry, lean, forgettable

items.name: Beef Shank
items.reasoning: From the leg, it's among the toughest cuts on the animal, packed with dense connective tissue and gristle. It's essentially inedible unless braised for hours as osso buco or simmered for stock, and hopeless as a steak.
items.subtitle: Soup bone, basically

title: Donuts, Ranked
tagline: The dozen that matters, glazed and settled.
basis: Ranked on flavor, texture, craft, and cultural staying power — not nostalgia or trend hype.

items.name: Glazed Doughnut
items.reasoning: The yeast-raised, sugar-glazed ring is the template every other donut answers to; Krispy Kreme has served its Original Glazed hot off the line since its 1937 founding, lighting the 'Hot Now' sign when they emerge.
items.subtitle: The platonic ideal

items.name: Boston Cream Doughnut
items.reasoning: A filled round of pastry cream capped with chocolate glaze; it was designated the official doughnut of Massachusetts in 2003, cementing its status as an American classic.
items.subtitle: Custard, chocolate, no hole

items.name: Old-Fashioned Doughnut
items.reasoning: A sour-cream or buttermilk cake donut fried so its craggy edges turn crisp while the center stays tender; its dense crumb is built for dunking in black coffee.
items.subtitle: Cracked edges, coffee's best friend

items.name: Maple Bar
items.reasoning: A rectangular yeast donut topped with maple icing, it's a fixture of Pacific Northwest and California doughnut cases and the archetypal 'bar' shape.
items.subtitle: West Coast in a rectangle

items.name: Beignet
items.reasoning: The square French-Creole fritter buried in powdered sugar and served at New Orleans' Café du Monde since 1862; it was named the official state doughnut of Louisiana in 1986.
items.subtitle: Powdered sugar avalanche

items.name: Jelly Doughnut
items.reasoning: A holeless yeast round injected with fruit jelly and rolled in sugar; simple and reliable, though the ratio of dough to filling is a lifelong gamble.
items.subtitle: Sweet ambush inside

items.name: French Cruller
items.reasoning: Made from choux (pâte à choux) rather than standard donut dough, its ridged rings fry up airy and custardy inside, a texture no other donut matches.
items.subtitle: Ridged, eggy, feather-light

items.name: Bear Claw
items.reasoning: A filled Danish-style pastry slit to fan out like a claw, usually stuffed with almond paste; it eats more like a laminated pastry than a fried ring.
items.subtitle: Almond paste with talons

items.name: Apple Cider Doughnut
items.reasoning: A cake donut made with reduced apple cider and rolled in cinnamon sugar, it's a seasonal fixture of Northeastern U.S. orchards and best eaten warm on-site.
items.subtitle: Autumn orchard staple

items.name: Malasada
items.reasoning: A holeless Portuguese fried dough rolled in sugar, brought to Hawaii by plantation workers and celebrated on Malasada Day; Leonard's Bakery in Honolulu has made them since 1952.
items.subtitle: Portuguese-Hawaiian sugar bomb

items.name: Zeppole
items.reasoning: An Italian fried dough, often piped with custard and dusted with sugar, traditionally eaten on St. Joseph's Day (March 19); great fresh but goes stale fast.
items.subtitle: St. Joseph's Day fritter

items.name: Pączki
items.reasoning: A rich Polish filled donut traditionally eaten before Lent on Fat Thursday and Pączki Day; the enriched dough and rose or plum jam make it heavier than a standard jelly donut.
items.subtitle: Fat Thursday indulgence

items.name: Loukoumades
items.reasoning: Small fried dough balls drenched in honey syrup and cinnamon, among the oldest recorded Greek desserts with roots in antiquity; delicious but only in the first ten minutes before the syrup sogs them.
items.subtitle: Honey-soaked Greek dough balls

items.name: Doughnut Hole
items.reasoning: The punched-out centers sold as Tim Hortons Timbits (1976) and Dunkin' Munchkins (1973); perfectly snackable, but they're literally the leftover holes marketed back to you.
items.subtitle: You're paying for the middle

items.name: Cronut
items.reasoning: Dominique Ansel's trademarked croissant-doughnut hybrid drew hours-long lines and scalpers after its May 2013 debut, spawning a wave of greasy, half-laminated imitators that were more social-media stunt than pastry.
items.subtitle: The two-hour line

title: Dumplings, Ranked
tagline: Every pocket of dough, put in its place.
basis: A cross-cultural ranking of the world's great (and not-so-great) dumplings, judged on flavor, technique, and how hard they are to stop eating.

items.name: Xiaolongbao
items.reasoning: Solidified aspic melts during steaming to trap hot broth inside a paper-thin, pleated wrapper, engineering a burst of soup in one bite. The technical difficulty and the payoff make it the benchmark all dumplings are measured against.
items.subtitle: Shanghai soup dumpling

items.name: Gyoza / Jiaozi
items.reasoning: The Chinese jiaozi and its Japanese gyoza descendant deliver the perfect contrast: a crisp, browned bottom and a steamed top over a juicy pork-and-cabbage filling. One of the world's most-eaten dumplings, it's central to Lunar New Year tables.
items.subtitle: The pan-fried standard

items.name: Khinkali
items.reasoning: A thick twisted knot holds spiced meat and its own broth, eaten by hand from the top with the doughy 'kudi' stem traditionally left behind. Georgians rank each other by how many they can eat, and the honest answer is always more.
items.subtitle: Georgia's soup-filled twist

items.name: Har gow
items.reasoning: A translucent wheat-and-tapioca-starch wrapper shows off whole shrimp inside, and a proper one has at least seven to ten pleats by tradition. It's a technical showpiece and a dim sum litmus test for any kitchen.
items.subtitle: Dim sum's crystal shrimp

items.name: Pierogi
items.reasoning: Boiled then pan-fried with butter and onions, the ruskie version stuffed with potato and farmer's cheese is comfort food perfected. Versatile across sweet and savory fillings, it earns its place as an Eastern European staple.
items.subtitle: Poland's potato pillow

items.name: Sheng jian bao
items.reasoning: A partially leavened bun is pan-fried to a crackly golden base, then finished with steam so it stays fluffy on top while releasing pork broth inside. It sits at the delicious intersection of bun and soup dumpling.
items.subtitle: Pan-fried and juicy

items.name: Momo
items.reasoning: A Tibetan and Nepali staple served with fiery tomato-based achar, it's beloved across the Himalayas and one of the most popular street foods in the region. The chili dip alone makes it addictive.
items.subtitle: Himalayan street steamer

items.name: Shumai
items.reasoning: An open cup of thin wrapper cradles pork and shrimp, often topped with roe or a pea for color, and it's a dim sum cart mainstay. Reliable and satisfying, if a notch less thrilling than the sealed soup dumplings.
items.subtitle: Open-topped dim sum

items.name: Mandu
items.reasoning: Whether steamed, boiled, or fried, Korean mandu pack a filling of pork, tofu, and glass noodles seasoned with garlic and sesame. Dropped into rice-cake soup for the new year, it's flexible and dependable.
items.subtitle: Korea's versatile parcel

items.name: Wonton
items.reasoning: Thin, silky wrappers around a small pork or shrimp filling shine floating in clear broth or noodle soup. It's a comfort classic, though the wrapper-to-filling ratio leaves it lighter than the heavyweights.
items.subtitle: The soup-floater

items.name: Baozi
items.reasoning: A leavened, steamed bun wrapped around char siu pork or vegetables, it's a portable breakfast icon across China. Whether it counts as a 'dumpling' is a fair debate, but the pillowy dough earns respect.
items.subtitle: The fluffy filled bun

items.name: Pelmeni
items.reasoning: Small unleavened dumplings of ground meat, historically frozen outdoors in Siberia and boiled to order, served with sour cream or vinegar. Solid and warming, but the thin filling keeps it firmly mid-pack.
items.subtitle: Siberian meat pockets

items.name: Manti
items.reasoning: Spiced lamb or beef folded into pinched dough, steamed and dressed with yogurt and garlic across Turkey and Central Asia. Hearty and flavorful, though the larger, doughier ratio holds it back from the top tiers.
items.subtitle: Central Asian steamed parcels

items.name: Ravioli
items.reasoning: Fresh egg pasta pockets of cheese or meat are unimpeachable, but the category is dragged down by the canned Chef Boyardee version that defined a generation of lazy dinners. A tale of two ravioli.
items.subtitle: Italy's stuffed pasta

items.name: Gnocchi
items.reasoning: When light and pillowy it's sublime, but overworked dough turns them into dense, gummy erasers, and getting the ratio right is genuinely hard. High ceiling, low floor, and too often the floor.
items.subtitle: Potato dumpling gamble

items.name: Matzah ball
items.reasoning: This unleavened meal dumpling in chicken soup splits families into camps over light 'floaters' and dense 'sinkers.' Comforting in broth, but on its own it's a bland sponge with nothing to prove.
items.subtitle: The 'floater vs. sinker' debate

items.name: Tangyuan
items.reasoning: Glutinous rice balls in sweet ginger syrup, often filled with black sesame or peanut, eaten at the Lantern Festival. Culturally rich, but the chewy, gluey texture is polarizing and it barely reads as a savory dumpling.
items.subtitle: Sweet glutinous globes

items.name: Knödel
items.reasoning: A fist-sized ball of bread or potato boiled into a dense, largely unseasoned mass meant mainly to soak up gravy. It's filler masquerading as a main event, and it sits like a rock afterward.
items.subtitle: The stodge bomb

title: Peppers & Chilis, Ranked
tagline: From flavor royalty to pure gimmick pain
basis: Ranked on culinary versatility, flavor beyond heat, and how often it actually earns a spot in real cooking versus novelty burn.

items.name: Jalapeño
items.reasoning: At a manageable 2,500-8,000 Scoville units it delivers heat and grassy flavor without wrecking a dish, making it the most-used chili in American and Mexican cooking. Poppers, salsa, nachos, pickled slices - it does everything.
items.subtitle: The reliable workhorse

items.name: Habanero
items.reasoning: Runs 100,000-350,000 SHU but carries a distinctive tropical, citrusy aroma that makes it far more than raw heat. It's the backbone of Yucatán cooking and countless premium hot sauces.
items.subtitle: Fruity fire

items.name: Serrano
items.reasoning: Hotter than a jalapeño at roughly 10,000-25,000 SHU with a crisp, bright flavor and thin walls that need no seeding. It's the go-to for fresh pico de gallo and salsas across Mexico.
items.subtitle: Jalapeño's hotter sibling

items.name: Scotch Bonnet
items.reasoning: Same 100,000-350,000 SHU range as the habanero but sweeter, and it's essential to Jamaican jerk and West African pepper sauces. Irreplaceable flavor identity for an entire cuisine.
items.subtitle: Soul of the Caribbean

items.name: Poblano
items.reasoning: A gentle 1,000-2,000 SHU with thick walls ideal for stuffing, it becomes chiles rellenos fresh and the rich, raisiny ancho when dried. One of the most useful peppers in the kitchen.
items.subtitle: Mild and meaty

items.name: Thai Bird's Eye
items.reasoning: Tiny pods packing 50,000-100,000 SHU that define Thai, Vietnamese, and much of Southeast Asian cooking. Big clean heat with a floral edge in a pepper the size of a fingernail.
items.subtitle: Small but savage

items.name: Cayenne
items.reasoning: At 30,000-50,000 SHU it's the ground red powder in nearly every spice rack and the base of Frank's-style sauces. Ubiquitous, dependable, and quietly essential.
items.subtitle: The pantry staple

items.name: Chipotle
items.reasoning: A ripe jalapeño smoke-dried into deep, leathery flavor, then canned in adobo for instant complexity. It adds barbecue depth that no fresh chili can match.
items.subtitle: Smoke in a pod

items.name: Hatch Chile
items.reasoning: The New Mexico chile roasted every autumn has a rich, earthy flavor that anchors green and red chile stews. A regional obsession serious enough to have its own harvest season.
items.subtitle: New Mexico's pride

items.name: Fresno
items.reasoning: Similar heat to a jalapeño but fruitier and sweeter as it ripens red, great raw or quick-pickled. Slightly harder to find, which keeps it out of the top tier.
items.subtitle: The red jalapeño look-alike

items.name: Bell Pepper
items.reasoning: A recessive gene leaves it at 0 SHU, making it the sweet, crunchy base of countless dishes but bringing none of the chili's point. Useful, but it's a vegetable pretending to be a chili.
items.subtitle: Zero heat, all crunch

items.name: Padrón
items.reasoning: Blistered in olive oil as a tapas classic, most are mild but roughly one in ten packs a real kick. The gamble is half the fun.
items.subtitle: Spanish roulette

items.name: Tabasco Pepper
items.reasoning: A 30,000-50,000 SHU pepper best known as the sole chili in Tabasco sauce since 1868. Great as a mash, but rarely used fresh for anything else.
items.subtitle: One sauce, forever

items.name: Banana Pepper
items.reasoning: Mild at 0-500 SHU and mostly encountered pickled on sandwiches and pizza. Pleasant tang, but almost no heat and little culinary ambition.
items.subtitle: Pickle-jar filler

items.name: Pepperoncini
items.reasoning: A mild 100-500 SHU Italian-Greek pepper served pickled with a bright, briny bite. Fine as a garnish, forgettable as a chili.
items.subtitle: The salad-bar regular

items.name: Shishito
items.reasoning: Usually mild and blistered as a Japanese appetizer, with roughly one in ten surprising you with heat. Trendy, but a one-trick snack.
items.subtitle: Blister and pray

items.name: Ghost Pepper
items.reasoning: The Bhut jolokia broke 1,000,000 SHU and briefly held the Guinness record in 2007, but the flavor is buried under punishing heat. Mostly a dare, not an ingredient.
items.subtitle: The first over a million

items.name: Carolina Reaper
items.reasoning: Held the Guinness record as world's hottest at roughly 1.6 million SHU from 2013 to 2023, and has sent people to the ER. It exists for YouTube challenges, not cooking.
items.subtitle: Pain as a personality

title: MLB Teams, Ranked
tagline: 27 rings talk. Everyone else listens.
basis: Ranked on franchise history, championship pedigree, rivalries, and cultural weight across MLB's 30 clubs.

items.name: New York Yankees
items.reasoning: Own 27 World Series titles, by far the most of any MLB franchise, and produced legends from Babe Ruth and Lou Gehrig to Mickey Mantle and Derek Jeter.
items.subtitle: The Evil Empire

items.name: Los Angeles Dodgers
items.reasoning: Eight World Series titles including 2020 and 2024, a modern juggernaut with baseball's highest payrolls and the franchise that broke the color barrier with Jackie Robinson.
items.subtitle: Blue heaven, deep pockets

items.name: St. Louis Cardinals
items.reasoning: Eleven World Series championships, second only to the Yankees, backed by generations of stars like Stan Musial, Bob Gibson, and Albert Pujols.
items.subtitle: The best fans in baseball

items.name: Boston Red Sox
items.reasoning: Nine titles overall and four since 2004, finally ending the 86-year Curse of the Bambino at storied Fenway Park, MLB's oldest ballpark.
items.subtitle: Curse, reversed

items.name: San Francisco Giants
items.reasoning: Eight championships across New York and San Francisco, winning three in five years (2010, 2012, 2014) and home to Willie Mays and Barry Bonds.
items.subtitle: Even-year magic

items.name: Atlanta Braves
items.reasoning: Four World Series titles and an unprecedented run of 14 straight division crowns from 1991 to 2005, capped by another championship in 2021.
items.subtitle: America's team of the '90s

items.name: Houston Astros
items.reasoning: Won the World Series in 2017 and 2022 and reached seven straight ALCS, though the 2017 title is forever tainted by their electronic sign-stealing scandal.
items.subtitle: Rings with an asterisk

items.name: Chicago Cubs
items.reasoning: Ended a 108-year championship drought with a dramatic 2016 World Series win, the longest title drought in North American pro sports.
items.subtitle: Lovable, finally winners

items.name: Philadelphia Phillies
items.reasoning: Two titles (1980, 2008) and MLB's most losses of any franchise historically, but a passionate fanbase and a strong recent run of deep October appearances.
items.subtitle: Loud, red, relentless

items.name: New York Mets
items.reasoning: Two championships in 1969 and 1986, both stunning underdog runs, though decades of chaotic ownership kept them from sustained contention.
items.subtitle: The Miracle and the mess

items.name: Baltimore Orioles
items.reasoning: Three World Series titles and home to Cal Ripken Jr.'s legendary 2,632 consecutive-games streak, now rebuilt into a young contender.
items.subtitle: Cal Ripken's house

items.name: Cleveland Guardians
items.reasoning: Two early titles (1920, 1948) but nothing since, enduring the longest active championship drought in MLB despite pennants in 1954, 1995, 1997, and 2016.
items.subtitle: Still waiting since '48

items.name: Toronto Blue Jays
items.reasoning: Back-to-back champions in 1992 and 1993, the only Canadian club in MLB and the first outside the U.S. to win the World Series.
items.subtitle: North of the border

items.name: Minnesota Twins
items.reasoning: Won thrilling seven-game World Series in 1987 and 1991, but later endured a record 18-game postseason losing streak before snapping it in 2023.
items.subtitle: Homer Hankies

items.name: Texas Rangers
items.reasoning: Captured their first-ever World Series in 2023 after 62 seasons and two heartbreaking Game 6 collapses in the 2011 Fall Classic.
items.subtitle: First ring at last

items.name: San Diego Padres
items.reasoning: Zero championships in over five decades, with only two pennants (1984, 1998) to show despite recent big-spending rosters.
items.subtitle: Sunny, still ringless

items.name: Milwaukee Brewers
items.reasoning: One pennant in 1982 and no World Series title, a consistent recent playoff club that has yet to break through in October.
items.subtitle: Small market, no ring

items.name: Pittsburgh Pirates
items.reasoning: Five titles including Bill Mazeroski's 1960 walk-off and Roberto Clemente's era, but decades of losing seasons since their last crown in 1979.
items.subtitle: Glory long gone

items.name: Miami Marlins
items.reasoning: Two titles (1997, 2003) immediately followed by infamous fire sales, and they routinely draw among MLB's lowest attendance figures.
items.subtitle: Win it, then sell it

items.name: Seattle Mariners
items.reasoning: The only MLB team that has never reached a World Series in nearly five decades, despite a record-tying 116-win season in 2001.
items.subtitle: The endless wait

title: National Parks, Ranked
tagline: Sacred ground, tourist traps, and one glorified parking lot.
basis: A definitive ranking of famous (mostly U.S.) national parks weighing scenery, natural significance, uniqueness, and how well they justify their protected status.

items.name: Yellowstone National Park
items.reasoning: Established in 1872 as the world's first national park, it holds roughly half of Earth's geysers including Old Faithful and sits atop a massive volcanic caldera. Wolves, bison, and grizzlies roam a landscape unlike anywhere else.
items.subtitle: The original, and still the king

items.name: Yosemite National Park
items.reasoning: Home to El Capitan and Half Dome, two of the most iconic granite monoliths on the planet, plus some of North America's tallest waterfalls. Its glacier-carved valley helped launch the entire American conservation movement under John Muir.
items.subtitle: Granite cathedral of the Sierra

items.name: Grand Canyon National Park
items.reasoning: The Colorado River carved a canyon up to a mile deep and 277 river miles long, exposing nearly two billion years of geologic history in its walls. It draws well over four million visitors a year and remains genuinely jaw-dropping.
items.subtitle: A mile deep, incomprehensibly vast

items.name: Zion National Park
items.reasoning: Towering Navajo sandstone cliffs and slot-canyon hikes like The Narrows and Angels Landing make it one of the most visited and dramatic parks in the country. Angels Landing is so popular it now requires a permit lottery.
items.subtitle: Red-rock canyon carved by the Virgin River

items.name: Glacier National Park (U.S.)
items.reasoning: Over a million acres of glacier-sculpted peaks, alpine lakes, and the engineering marvel of the Going-to-the-Sun Road. Its namesake glaciers have shrunk dramatically, making it a stark front line of climate change.
items.subtitle: Crown of the Continent

items.name: Denali National Park and Preserve
items.reasoning: Centered on 20,310-foot Denali, the highest mountain in North America, it protects six million acres of subarctic wilderness with grizzlies, caribou, and wolves. A single 92-mile road is the only way in, keeping it wild.
items.subtitle: North America's tallest peak

items.name: Grand Teton National Park
items.reasoning: The Teton Range juts abruptly over 7,000 feet above the Jackson Hole valley floor with no foothills, creating one of the most photographed skylines in America. Glacial lakes and the Snake River complete the postcard.
items.subtitle: Mountains that rise straight from the valley

items.name: Olympic National Park
items.reasoning: A UNESCO World Heritage Site spanning temperate rainforest, glaciated peaks, and rugged Pacific shoreline within a single park. The Hoh Rain Forest receives up to 14 feet of rain a year.
items.subtitle: Rainforest, glaciers, and wild coast in one

items.name: Great Smoky Mountains National Park
items.reasoning: With over 13 million annual visitors it is by far the most visited U.S. national park, prized for misty ridgelines and extraordinary biodiversity. The crowds and traffic are the cost of that popularity.
items.subtitle: The most-visited park in America

items.name: Arches National Park
items.reasoning: Concentrates over 2,000 natural sandstone arches, including the iconic Delicate Arch featured on Utah's license plate. It's spectacular but small and now uses timed-entry reservations to manage overcrowding.
items.subtitle: More than 2,000 stone arches

items.name: Sequoia National Park
items.reasoning: Protects giant sequoias including General Sherman, the largest tree on Earth by volume, and California's Mount Whitney. The scale of the trees alone earns its keep.
items.subtitle: Home of the largest tree on Earth

items.name: Acadia National Park
items.reasoning: The crown jewel of the New England coast, with Cadillac Mountain being among the first spots in the U.S. to catch the sunrise. Compact and stunning, though it gets packed in summer.
items.subtitle: Where mountains meet the Atlantic

items.name: Bryce Canyon National Park
items.reasoning: Not a true canyon but a series of natural amphitheaters filled with the largest concentration of hoodoos, thin rock spires, found anywhere on Earth. Its dark skies also make it a premier stargazing spot.
items.subtitle: An amphitheater of orange hoodoos

items.name: Rocky Mountain National Park
items.reasoning: Trail Ridge Road climbs above 12,000 feet, giving easy access to alpine tundra and 60-plus peaks over 12,000 feet. Gorgeous, but so crowded it required timed-entry permits for peak months.
items.subtitle: Alpine tundra above the tree line

items.name: Joshua Tree National Park
items.reasoning: Sits at the meeting of the Mojave and Colorado deserts, defined by its twisted namesake trees and world-class boulder climbing. Otherworldly, though relatively flat and hot much of the year.
items.subtitle: Where two deserts collide

items.name: Death Valley National Park
items.reasoning: Contains Badwater Basin at 282 feet below sea level and recorded 134°F, the hottest reliably measured air temperature on Earth. A place of extremes worth seeing once, if you can handle it.
items.subtitle: Hottest, driest, lowest

items.name: Everglades National Park
items.reasoning: The largest tropical wilderness in the U.S. and the only place where alligators and crocodiles coexist. Its ecosystem is heavily degraded by water diversion and invasive Burmese pythons, dimming the experience.
items.subtitle: A river of grass under siege

items.name: Hot Springs National Park
items.reasoning: One of the smallest and most urban national parks, centered on historic bathhouses in downtown Hot Springs, Arkansas. Charming, but it feels more like a spa town than a great wilderness.
items.subtitle: A bathhouse row that got promoted

items.name: Cuyahoga Valley National Park
items.reasoning: A green corridor between Cleveland and Akron built around a scenic railroad and waterfall, frequently cited by critics as not meeting the grandeur bar of a national park. Nice for a day trip; hard to call a crown jewel.
items.subtitle: Pleasant, but a national park?

items.name: Gateway Arch National Park
items.reasoning: At just 91 acres it is the smallest national park by far, and it's essentially a stainless-steel arch and museum in downtown St. Louis. Its 2018 rebranding from national monument to national park is widely mocked as a status upgrade it doesn't earn.
items.subtitle: A monument masquerading as a park

title: Mountains, Ranked
tagline: The peaks that earned their altitude — and the walk-up that didn't.
basis: Ranked on iconic status, mountaineering prestige, and sheer geological drama — not just raw elevation.

items.name: Mount Everest
items.reasoning: At 8,849 m it is Earth's highest peak above sea level, first summited in 1953 by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay. No mountain is more universally recognized.
items.subtitle: Roof of the world

items.name: Matterhorn
items.reasoning: A near-symmetrical 4,478 m horn on the Swiss-Italian border, arguably the most photographed mountain on Earth and the inspiration for the Toblerone logo. Its 1865 first ascent ended in a deadly descent.
items.subtitle: The perfect pyramid

items.name: K2
items.reasoning: The world's second-highest peak at 8,611 m and vastly harder and deadlier than Everest, with no easy route. It resisted all winter attempts until January 2021.
items.subtitle: The Savage Mountain

items.name: Denali
items.reasoning: At 6,190 m it is the highest peak in North America, and its base-to-summit rise is among the largest of any mountain on land. Brutal subarctic cold makes it a serious objective.
items.subtitle: North America's giant

items.name: Mont Blanc
items.reasoning: The Alps' highest peak at about 4,807 m, whose 1786 first ascent by Balmat and Paccard effectively launched modern mountaineering. It remains one of the most climbed big mountains on Earth.
items.subtitle: Birthplace of alpinism

items.name: Mount Fuji
items.reasoning: A 3,776 m active stratovolcano and Japan's highest point, its symmetrical form is a UNESCO World Heritage site and a national symbol depicted for centuries in art.
items.subtitle: Japan's sacred cone

items.name: Mount Kilimanjaro
items.reasoning: At 5,895 m it is the highest peak in Africa and the world's tallest free-standing mountain. Uniquely among the Seven Summits, fit trekkers can reach the top without technical climbing.
items.subtitle: Africa's rooftop

items.name: Aconcagua
items.reasoning: At 6,961 m it is the highest mountain outside Asia and the tallest in both the Southern and Western Hemispheres. A true high-altitude giant with a non-technical normal route.
items.subtitle: King of the Andes

items.name: Mount Rainier
items.reasoning: A 4,392 m active volcano looming over Seattle, carrying the most glacial ice of any peak in the contiguous US. It's a classic training ground for Everest-bound climbers.
items.subtitle: The glaciated volcano

items.name: Eiger
items.reasoning: At 3,967 m its infamous Nordwand, nicknamed the 'Mordwand' (murder wall), is one of the deadliest and most storied faces in alpine history. A legend among climbers.
items.subtitle: The Ogre's north face

items.name: Annapurna Massif
items.reasoning: Annapurna I, at 8,091 m the tenth-highest peak, was the first 8,000 m mountain ever climbed (1950) but historically carried the highest death-to-summit ratio of any 8,000er.
items.subtitle: The deadliest 8,000er

items.name: Nanga Parbat
items.reasoning: The ninth-highest peak at 8,126 m, earning its nickname after a wave of early fatalities. Its Rupal Face rises about 4,600 m, among the tallest mountain faces on Earth.
items.subtitle: The Killer Mountain

items.name: Half Dome
items.reasoning: A 2,694 m granite dome sheared to a sheer 1,500 m vertical face, it's the visual signature of Yosemite. Its cable route to the summit is a bucket-list hike.
items.subtitle: Yosemite's granite icon

items.name: Ben Nevis
items.reasoning: At 1,345 m it's the highest peak in the British Isles, but its summit is shrouded in cloud most of the year and the tourist path draws over 100,000 walkers annually.
items.subtitle: Britain's crowded high point

items.name: Table Mountain
items.reasoning: A striking flat-topped landmark rising about 1,086 m above Cape Town and a New7Wonders of Nature finalist. Scenic, but a rotating cable car ferries most visitors to the top.
items.subtitle: Cape Town's flat top

items.name: Mount Washington
items.reasoning: At 1,917 m it's the highest peak in the Northeastern US and held the world-record surface wind gust of 231 mph for decades. Notorious weather, but you can drive to the top.
items.subtitle: Home of the world's worst weather

items.name: Mount Kosciuszko
items.reasoning: At just 2,228 m it is mainland Australia's highest point and by far the weakest of the Seven Summits — a gentle graded path lets casual walkers reach the top in an afternoon.
items.subtitle: The Seven Summit you can stroll up

title: Fighter Jets, Ranked
tagline: The definitive dogfight over which jets actually earn their afterburner.
basis: Ranked on air-combat capability, technology, combat record, and reliability across the jet age.

items.name: F-22 Raptor
items.reasoning: The first operational fifth-generation fighter, combining stealth, supercruise, and thrust-vectoring that no rival has matched in air-to-air. It has never been lost in combat and remains widely regarded as the best air-superiority fighter ever built.
items.subtitle: Stealth air-superiority king

items.name: F-35 Lightning II
items.reasoning: The most-produced fifth-gen fighter, with over 1,000 built and adopted by more than a dozen nations. Its sensor fusion and data-sharing make it as much a flying command node as a stealth strike fighter.
items.subtitle: The networked sensor-fusion jet

items.name: F-15 Eagle
items.reasoning: Designed to never lose a dogfight, the Eagle holds an air-to-air record above 100 confirmed kills with no losses in aerial combat. Still in frontline service 50 years after its debut via the modernized F-15EX.
items.subtitle: 104 kills, zero losses

items.name: Su-27 Flanker
items.reasoning: The USSR's long-range counter to the F-15, with huge range, heavy missile load, and famous maneuverability demonstrated by Pugachev's Cobra. Its airframe spawned the entire Flanker dynasty of Su-30/33/35 jets.
items.subtitle: The Cobra-pulling Soviet answer

items.name: F-16 Fighting Falcon
items.reasoning: The most successful Western jet fighter of the modern era, with over 4,500 built and operators on every continent. Its fly-by-wire agility and low cost made it the definitive multirole export fighter.
items.subtitle: The workhorse everyone flies

items.name: Eurofighter Typhoon
items.reasoning: A twin-engine, canard-delta jet built by a UK/German/Italian/Spanish consortium and prized for its energy retention in a turning fight. It anchors the air defense of most major European air forces.
items.subtitle: Europe's canard-delta brawler

items.name: Dassault Rafale
items.reasoning: A fully French design flying from both land and carriers, marketed as 'omnirole' for handling air, strike, recon, and nuclear missions. Big export wins in India, Egypt, and the UAE cemented its reputation.
items.subtitle: France's carrier-capable omnirole

items.name: F/A-18 Super Hornet
items.reasoning: The mainstay of American carrier air wings, valued for reliability, weapons versatility, and buddy-refueling capability. It replaced both the Tomcat and legacy Hornet in fleet service.
items.subtitle: The US Navy's carrier backbone

items.name: Su-35 Flanker-E
items.reasoning: A heavily upgraded Flanker with 3D thrust-vectoring nozzles and a powerful radar, arguably the best non-stealth dogfighter flying. Its lack of true stealth keeps it out of the top tier.
items.subtitle: Thrust-vectoring 4++ generation

items.name: MiG-29 Fulcrum
items.reasoning: A superb short-range dogfighter with a helmet-mounted sight and high-off-boresight missiles that shocked Western analysts. Limited range and short airframe life held it back from greatness.
items.subtitle: Deadly close-in, short on legs

items.name: JAS 39 Gripen
items.reasoning: Sweden's lightweight fighter designed to operate from highways with a small ground crew and low running costs. Capable and cheap to fly, though smaller payload and range cap its ceiling.
items.subtitle: The road-base bargain fighter

items.name: F-14 Tomcat
items.reasoning: The Navy's long-range interceptor with variable-sweep wings and the 100-mile AIM-54 Phoenix missile, immortalized by Top Gun. Maintenance-heavy and retired by the US in 2006, but a legend of its era.
items.subtitle: Swing-wing Top Gun icon

items.name: Chengdu J-20
items.reasoning: China's first operational stealth fighter, a large twin-engine jet built for long-range air dominance in the Pacific. Impressive on paper, but engine maturity and unproven combat record leave it mid-pack for now.
items.subtitle: China's stealth heavyweight

items.name: MiG-21 Fishbed
items.reasoning: A dirt-cheap, fast interceptor produced in greater numbers than any other supersonic jet, serving dozens of air forces for decades. Short range and primitive avionics make it a numbers legend rather than a great fighter.
items.subtitle: Most-produced jet fighter ever

items.name: F-4 Phantom II
items.reasoning: A powerful, versatile Cold War workhorse flown by the Air Force, Navy, and Marines, but its early guns-less design and heavy smoke trail exposed it in Vietnam dogfights. Muscle and payload carried a jet that couldn't turn with lighter foes.
items.subtitle: Brute force over finesse

items.name: MiG-25 Foxbat
items.reasoning: Built for speeds near Mach 3 to chase high-altitude bombers, it terrified the West until a 1976 defection revealed steel construction and crude vacuum-tube electronics. A record-setting interceptor that was a poor dogfighter.
items.subtitle: Blazing fast, barely a fighter

items.name: F-104 Starfighter
items.reasoning: A blazing-fast missile-shaped interceptor whose tiny wings made it unforgiving and accident-prone, especially in low-level German service. Hundreds were lost in crashes, earning it the grim 'Widowmaker' nickname.
items.subtitle: The 'Widowmaker' rocket with wings

items.name: MiG-23 Flogger
items.reasoning: A variable-sweep replacement for the MiG-21 that gained speed and radar but was notoriously tricky to fly and unforgiving of pilot error. It fared poorly against Western jets in combat over the Middle East.
items.subtitle: Swing-wing that never clicked

items.name: F7U Cutlass
items.reasoning: A radical tailless Navy jet plagued by underpowered engines and a nose-high landing attitude that made carrier approaches lethal. Over a quarter of the fleet was lost in accidents, killing several test and fleet pilots.
items.subtitle: The 'Gutless Cutlass'

items.name: Yak-38 Forger
items.reasoning: The Soviet Navy's attempt at a Harrier had almost no combat range or payload in hot conditions and a dangerous reliance on automatic ejection. It was widely judged a near-useless fighter and quietly retired.
items.subtitle: The VTOL that barely flew

title: Chemical Elements, Ranked
tagline: The periodic table has favorites. We said it.
basis: Ranked on real-world impact, usefulness, ubiquity, and how badly the element tries to kill you.

items.name: Carbon
items.reasoning: Every known living thing is built on a carbon backbone, and its bonds form both diamond (the hardest natural material) and soft graphite.
items.subtitle: The element of life

items.name: Oxygen
items.reasoning: Makes up about 21% of the atmosphere and is required for respiration and combustion; it's also the most abundant element in Earth's crust.
items.subtitle: Breath and fire

items.name: Hydrogen
items.reasoning: The lightest and most abundant element in the universe, making up roughly 75% of normal matter and powering stars through fusion.
items.subtitle: Element number one

items.name: Iron
items.reasoning: Forms Earth's core and the hemoglobin in your blood, and as the base of steel it literally named an age of civilization.
items.subtitle: Age-defining metal

items.name: Silicon
items.reasoning: The second most abundant element in Earth's crust and the semiconductor foundation of every computer chip, plus the basis of glass.
items.subtitle: Brains of tech

items.name: Gold
items.reasoning: Prized for millennia because it never corrodes or tarnishes, and it's an excellent electrical conductor used in electronics.
items.subtitle: The eternal standard

items.name: Helium
items.reasoning: The second most abundant element in the universe; its extremely low boiling point makes it essential for cooling MRI magnets and rockets.
items.subtitle: Lighter than air

items.name: Nitrogen
items.reasoning: Makes up 78% of the air and is a core component of proteins and DNA; the Haber process fixes it into fertilizer that feeds billions.
items.subtitle: Fills the sky

items.name: Copper
items.reasoning: One of the first metals humans ever worked, and its superb conductivity makes it the default choice for electrical wiring.
items.subtitle: The wired one

items.name: Lithium
items.reasoning: The lightest metal, it powers nearly all rechargeable batteries and is also a frontline treatment for bipolar disorder.
items.subtitle: Battery king

items.name: Uranium
items.reasoning: The fissile isotope U-235 fuels nuclear reactors and weapons, releasing enormous energy from tiny amounts of matter.
items.subtitle: Nuclear fuel

items.name: Titanium
items.reasoning: As strong as steel but roughly 45% lighter, and its corrosion resistance and biocompatibility make it ideal for implants and aircraft.
items.subtitle: Strong and light

items.name: Sodium
items.reasoning: A reactive alkali metal that forms half of table salt and is essential for nerve signaling, though pure sodium explodes in water.
items.subtitle: Salt of life

items.name: Sulfur
items.reasoning: The pungent yellow element behind sulfuric acid, the most-produced industrial chemical, and the vulcanization that makes rubber usable.
items.subtitle: Brimstone

items.name: Neon
items.reasoning: An inert noble gas that reacts with nothing but glows a brilliant orange-red when charged, defining the look of the neon sign.
items.subtitle: City lights

items.name: Mercury
items.reasoning: The only metal liquid at room temperature, but it's a potent neurotoxin that has been phased out of thermometers and many products.
items.subtitle: Quicksilver poison

items.name: Arsenic
items.reasoning: A classic tasteless poison used in countless historic murders, and today it contaminates groundwater for millions of people.
items.subtitle: The inheritance powder

items.name: Lead
items.reasoning: A cumulative neurotoxin that likely poisoned the Romans and, via leaded gasoline and paint, lowered IQ in a generation of children.
items.subtitle: Toxic legacy

items.name: Plutonium
items.reasoning: Intensely radioactive and chemically toxic, it exists mainly as a weapons material and was the fissile core of the Nagasaki bomb.
items.subtitle: Bomb core

items.name: Polonium
items.reasoning: So radioactive it glows, with essentially no everyday use except infamy; it was used to assassinate Alexander Litvinenko in 2006.
items.subtitle: Assassin's isotope

title: Spiders, Ranked
tagline: Eight legs, eight opinions, one definitive verdict.
basis: Ranked on a mix of biological wow-factor, hunting ingenuity, harmlessness-to-humans, and sheer charisma versus notoriety.

items.name: Peacock spider
items.reasoning: Males of the genus Maratus flip up a vividly iridescent abdominal flap and wave their legs in an elaborate courtship dance. At roughly 4 mm long and completely harmless to humans, they are the internet's favorite spider.
items.subtitle: The rainbow disco dancer

items.name: Jumping spider
items.reasoning: Salticidae have the sharpest vision of any spider, with two enormous forward-facing eyes, and they pounce on prey rather than using a web. Their curious, cat-like behavior makes them the most beloved spiders.
items.subtitle: Big eyes, bigger personality

items.name: Goliath birdeater
items.reasoning: Theraphosa blondi is the largest spider on Earth by mass, weighing up to about 175 g with a leg span near 30 cm. Despite the name it rarely eats birds and its bite is medically minor to humans.
items.subtitle: The heavyweight champ

items.name: Sydney funnel-web spider
items.reasoning: Atrax robustus has some of the fastest-acting venom of any spider, yet no deaths have occurred since an effective antivenom was introduced in 1981. Its glossy black body and reared-up fangs make it unforgettable.
items.subtitle: Danger with a dress code

items.name: Tarantula
items.reasoning: Theraphosidae are large, long-lived and often kept as pets, with females of some species living 20 to 30 years. New World species flick irritating urticating hairs rather than delivering a dangerous bite.
items.subtitle: The gentle giant

items.name: Diving bell spider
items.reasoning: Argyroneta aquatica is the only spider that spends its entire life underwater, building a silk 'diving bell' filled with air it carries from the surface. Research shows the bell works as a physical gill, drawing dissolved oxygen from the water.
items.subtitle: The only true aquanaut

items.name: Trapdoor spider
items.reasoning: These spiders build silk-hinged burrow lids and burst out to seize passing prey. One Australian trapdoor spider named Number 16 lived to about 43 years old, the longest-lived spider on record.
items.subtitle: The ambush landlord

items.name: Wolf spider
items.reasoning: Lycosidae hunt on foot instead of spinning webs and have excellent night vision that reflects in a torch beam. Females carry their egg sac attached to their spinnerets and then ferry the hatched spiderlings on their backs.
items.subtitle: Devoted single mom

items.name: Jorō spider
items.reasoning: Trichonephila clavata spins large golden-hued orb webs and has spread rapidly across the southeastern United States since it was introduced to Georgia around 2013. It is strikingly colored and, despite its size, essentially harmless to people.
items.subtitle: The golden-web invader

items.name: Bolas spider
items.reasoning: Instead of a web, Mastophora females swing a sticky silk droplet on a line like a cowboy's bolas to snag moths. They lure the male moths in by chemically mimicking female moth pheromones.
items.subtitle: The lasso hunter

items.name: Huntsman spider
items.reasoning: Sparassidae are famous for their large leg span and speed, and their flattened bodies let them squeeze under bark and into car sun-visors. They startle people but are not dangerously venomous and help control insects.
items.subtitle: Big, fast, harmless

items.name: European garden spider
items.reasoning: Araneus diadematus is the classic cross-marked orb-weaver seen rebuilding its wheel-shaped web each night in gardens across Europe and introduced North America. Common and useful, but not exactly exotic.
items.subtitle: The reliable orb-weaver

items.name: Redback spider
items.reasoning: Latrodectus hasselti, a close relative of the black widow, carries a potent neurotoxin, but bites are rarely life-threatening and an antivenom has existed since 1956. It usually hides in sheds and mailboxes rather than seeking people out.
items.subtitle: Shy but venomous

items.name: Cellar spider
items.reasoning: Pholcidae are the fragile long-legged spiders that dangle in basement corners, and they vibrate their webs into a blur when disturbed. The myth that they have the deadliest venom but fangs too small to bite is simply false.
items.subtitle: The daddy-longlegs myth

items.name: Camel spider
items.reasoning: Despite viral war-zone photos, Solifugae are a separate arachnid order and are not true spiders at all, nor are they venomous. They earn a low rank for a fearsome reputation built almost entirely on forced-perspective myths.
items.subtitle: The fraud (not a spider)

items.name: Six-eyed sand spider
items.reasoning: These desert-dwelling ambush hunters burrow beneath the sand and possess a cytotoxic venom for which there is no antivenom. Bites are rare, but the combination of potent tissue-destroying venom and no treatment lands it near the bottom.
items.subtitle: Buried and untreatable

items.name: Brown recluse spider
items.reasoning: Loxosceles reclusa is one of the few North American spiders whose bite can cause necrotic skin lesions in some cases. Combined with widespread over-diagnosis and public fear, it is the most notorious household spider in the US.
items.subtitle: The flesh-wound reputation

items.name: Brazilian wandering spider
items.reasoning: Phoneutria are among the most venomous spiders to humans and, unlike most spiders, actively roam the forest floor at night rather than staying in a web. Their defensive, up-reared posture and potent neurotoxin make them genuinely feared.
items.subtitle: Aggressive and armed

title: Horse Breeds, Ranked
tagline: From desert legends to Budweiser icons, every breed put out to pasture.
basis: Ranked on historical influence, athletic prowess, versatility, and iconic status, with notoriety pulling a breed down.

items.name: Arabian horse
items.reasoning: One of the oldest breeds on Earth, depicted in art over 4,500 years ago, and a foundation bloodline for the Thoroughbred and countless others; still dominates long-distance endurance racing.
items.subtitle: The desert original

items.name: Thoroughbred
items.reasoning: The world's premier racehorse, with every individual tracing to three foundation stallions imported to England from the Middle East in the late 1600s and early 1700s; they can gallop around 40 mph.
items.subtitle: Born to race

items.name: American Quarter Horse
